title: Sentinel glob matching should support Redis stringmatch patterns
description: Upstream Redis 7.2.4 uses stringmatch() for Sentinel pattern matching, including SENTINEL RESET <pattern> and SENTINEL CONFIG GET <pattern>. Redis glob syntax supports '*', '?', bracket classes/ranges, negation, and backslash escapes. fr-sentinel::glob_match only supports exact, leading/trailing '*', and contains patterns, so RESET/CONFIG GET diverge for patterns like 'mymaste?', 'mymast[er]', or escaped wildcards. Acceptance: replace the simplified matcher with Redis-compatible ASCII glob behavior for Sentinel command patterns and add tests covering RESET and CONFIG GET parity.
close_reason: Implemented Redis-compatible Sentinel glob matching for RESET and CONFIG GET patterns; remote fr-sentinel fmt/check/test/clippy and UBS passed.

title: Add RESP parser fuzz test for crash resilience on malformed input
description:
# RESP Parser Fuzz Test

## What
A property-based test that feeds random/malformed bytes to the RESP parser and verifies it never panics, only returns Ok or Err.

## Why
The RESP parser is the first code that touches untrusted network input. A panic crashes the entire server. Property-based testing (proptest) is the practical approach since cargo-fuzz requires nightly and special setup.

## Implementation
1. Add proptest as a dev-dependency to fr-protocol
2. New test in crates/fr-protocol/src/lib.rs test module:
   ```rust
   proptest! {
       #[test]
       fn parse_frame_never_panics(data: Vec<u8>) {
           let _ = parse_frame(&data); // Ok or Err, never panic
       }
   }
   ```
3. Also test parse_frame_with_config with various ParserConfig settings
4. Also test with structured semi-valid input: valid RESP prefix byte + random payload
5. Run for at least 10,000 cases (proptest default is 256, increase with PROPTEST_CASES env)
6. Fix any panics found

## Validation
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p fr-protocol -- parse_frame_never_panics` passes
- Run with PROPTEST_CASES=100000 in CI

## Files
- crates/fr-protocol/Cargo.toml (add proptest dev-dependency)
- crates/fr-protocol/src/lib.rs (add proptest tests)
close_reason: Added 4 proptest fuzz tests to fr-protocol: parse_frame_never_panics, parse_frame_with_config_never_panics, parse_frame_with_tight_limits_never_panics, parse_frame_with_resp_prefix_never_panics. Each runs 10,000 random input cases. All pass — zero panics found. Added proptest dev-dependency.

title: fr-command: LPOS COUNT with non-numeric value emits generic InvalidInteger instead of upstream 'COUNT can't be negative'
description:
Upstream t_list.c::lposCommand parses COUNT via getPositiveLongFromObjectOrReply with the error string 'COUNT can't be negative', so both unparseable integers (e.g. 'abc') and negative values share that wording. fr was returning the generic 'value is not an integer or out of range' for unparseable inputs (the negative-value branch already mapped to the right wording).

This mirrors the same already-fixed bug for MAXLEN (br-frankenredis-lposmaxlen).

Differential probe vs vendored redis 7.2.4 (LPOS l a COUNT abc):
  fr:    ERR value is not an integer or out of range
  redis: ERR COUNT can't be negative

title: Add minimal client session context to Store for CLIENT stubs in dispatch_argv
description:
# Add minimal client context to Store for CLIENT command in dispatch_argv

## Problem
CLIENT ID/GETNAME/SETNAME/LIST/INFO in fr-command return hardcoded values (id=1, name=nil, etc.) because they don't have access to the ClientSession. Lua scripts calling redis.call(\"CLIENT\", \"ID\") get 1 instead of their real client ID.

## Design
Add a \"current client context\" to Store that the runtime populates before dispatching:
```rust
pub struct DispatchClientContext {
    pub client_id: u64,
    pub client_name: Option<Vec<u8>>,
    pub db_index: usize,
    pub flags: String,   // e.g., \"N\" for normal
    pub peer_addr: String,
}
```

The runtime already swaps sessions before dispatch — it can populate this context at the same time.

## Implementation
1. Add pub dispatch_client_ctx: DispatchClientContext to Store
2. In Runtime::execute_frame (before dispatch_argv call), populate store.dispatch_client_ctx from current session
3. Update fr-command's client_cmd to read from store.dispatch_client_ctx:
   - CLIENT ID: return ctx.client_id
   - CLIENT GETNAME: return ctx.client_name
   - CLIENT SETNAME: update ctx.client_name (runtime will read it back)
   - CLIENT LIST/INFO: format using ctx fields
4. After dispatch_argv returns, runtime reads back any changes (e.g., SETNAME)

## Scope limitation
CLIENT LIST in Redis returns ALL connected clients. Through dispatch_argv, we can only report the current client. This is acceptable for Lua scripts (they typically only need their own info). The runtime's CLIENT LIST already returns all clients.

## Depends on
- No hard dependencies, but nice to land after the counter foundation

## Testing
- Conformance: Lua script calls redis.call(\"CLIENT\", \"ID\") and gets a real ID (not 1)
- Unit test: populate context, verify CLIENT GETNAME returns it

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs (DispatchClientContext struct, Store field)
- crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs (client_cmd reads context)
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs (populate context before dispatch)
close_reason: Completed

title: INFO used_memory stuck at peak after FLUSHALL (aggregate cache not reset)
description: FOUND 2026-06-17 profiling keyspace RAM. After FLUSHALL with 0 keys, fr INFO used_memory reports the pre-flush PEAK (e.g. 53MB) vs redis ~2MB. ROOT CAUSE: Store::flushdb (lib.rs ~8934) clears entries/ordered_keys/mem_estimate_cache but NOT the aggregate cached_memory_usage_bytes, and only bumps dirty by 1 — below estimate_memory_usage_bytes's 64-mutation recompute threshold — so it keeps returning the stale peak. Individual DEL works (adjust_cached_memory_usage_after_remove decrements per-key); only flush is broken. FIX: reset cached_memory_usage_bytes + cached_memory_usage_dirty to 0 in flushdb (mirrors the line-8772 pattern). Verified: used_memory drops 53MB->0 on flush, 634 fr-store tests pass, memory_usage_multidb_gate passes. New gate info_memory_flush_gate.py.
close_reason: Fixed in 2560ec226: flushdb resets cached_memory_usage_bytes + dirty counter; used_memory now drops 53MB->0 on FLUSHALL matching the empty keyspace. 634 fr-store tests pass; new gate info_memory_flush_gate.py in parity_suite.

title: fr-command: BITFIELD bit offsets above 4GiB silently accepted instead of upstream rejection
description:
Upstream bitops.c::getBitOffsetFromArgument enforces (offset>>3) < proto_max_bulk_len (default 512 MiB), i.e. bit offsets must stay below 2^32 (4 GiB worth of bits). BITFIELD GET/SET/INCRBY all use this validator for the bit-offset arg. Above the cap, upstream emits 'ERR bit offset is not an integer or out of range'.

fr-command's bitfield_parse_offset only checked u64 parseability — values like 99999999999 silently became no-op reads (returning 0) or triggered downstream 'string exceeds maximum allowed size' bookkeeping when SET/INCRBY tried to extend the underlying string.

Fix: add the 2^32 (=512 MiB * 8) cap inside bitfield_parse_offset; #N hash form computes offset*bits first then applies the same cap.

Mirrors br-frankenredis-qd8i (GETBIT) for the BITFIELD family.

title: Remove empty STORE encoding known-divergence path
description: scripts/store_enc_gate.py says frankenredis-v4ba8 is fully fixed and keeps KNOWN_V4BA8 empty, but still carries known/fixed reporting that would allow future *STORE destination-encoding drift to pass if reintroduced. Simplify the no-mock gate to hard-fail every divergence and update pass text to match the fixed state.

title: Sentinel HELLO address moves should update same runid across all masters
description: Upstream Redis 7.2.4 sentinelProcessHelloMessage calls sentinelUpdateSentinelAddressInAllMasters() after a known Sentinel runid reports a new address. fr-sentinel discovery removes/re-adds the runid only under the master named by the HELLO, leaving other monitored masters with stale address-keyed Sentinel entries for the same runid. Acceptance: applying a HELLO for runid R at a new address updates/remaps every master that already knows runid R to the new address key/addr, while preserving the existing per-master Sentinel metadata where possible.
close_reason: Implemented HELLO same-runid address propagation across all masters; remote fr-sentinel fmt/check/test/clippy and UBS passed.

title: PFCOUNT uses old Flajolet HLL estimator; redis 7.2.4 uses Ertl 2017 (tau/sigma) — counts diverge
description: HLL registers are byte-identical to redis (verified: same sha256 for 5000-element HLL), but PFCOUNT diverges (n=5000: oracle=4968 fr=4969) because fr-store hll_estimate (lib.rs ~17351) uses the OLD 2007 Flajolet estimator (alpha*m^2/sum + linear-counting small-range correction). Redis 7.2.4 hyperloglog.c::hllCount uses the NEW Otmar Ertl 2017 estimator (arXiv:1702.01284): reghisto + hllTau + hllSigma, E=llroundl(HLL_ALPHA_INF*m*m/z), HLL_ALPHA_INF=0.721347520444481703680, HLL_Q=50. Fix: port hllCount exactly. Found via HLL differential probe.
close_reason: Fixed: replaced fr-store hll_estimate (old Flajolet 2007: alpha*m^2/sum + linear-counting) with the Otmar Ertl 2017 estimator (reghisto + hll_tau + hll_sigma, E=round(HLL_ALPHA_INF*m*m/z), HLL_Q=50), a direct byte-for-byte port of redis 7.2.4 hyperloglog.c::hllCount. Registers were already byte-identical; only the estimator diverged. Now PFCOUNT matches vendored 7.2.4 EXACTLY across 1..200000 (n=5000: 4968 not 4969; 100k=100853; 200k=200991). HLL_ALPHA_INF uses the round-trip-shortest f64 literal (bit-identical to upstream). Golden test hll_estimate_matches_redis_ertl_count_exactly + selftest + 1087 fr-store + 194 fr-conformance pass, clippy clean.

title: Lua __call metamethod (callable tables)
description: Sub-bead of frankenredis-00gjf metamethods epic. Lua 5.1 lets any value with a __call metamethod be called as a function: t(args...) invokes the metatable's __call(t, args...) and returns its result. fr currently errors 'attempt to call a table value'. Probe: 'local t=setmetatable({},{__call=function(_,a) return a*2 end}); return t(21)' → vendored 42, fr error. Implementation: in call_function_with_callee (and call_function), when the value is a non-callable Table with a metatable __call, recurse with the __call function and prepend the table as the first arg. Touch site: call_function dispatch path. Filed during 00gjf epic decomposition.
close_reason: Added metatable_call_handler() helper that returns the table's __call metamethod ONLY when it is a callable (RustFunction/Function/WrappedCoroutine); non-function handlers yield None so the caller emits the standard 'attempt to call <label> (a table value)' error — matching vendored which does NOT recursively chain __call through non-function handlers. Wired into both call_function (so internal callers like pcall, iterator dispatch, sort comparators honor the metamethod) and call_function_with_callee (so AST sites do too). When __call is callable, the table itself is prepended as the first arg, matching Lua 5.1 semantics. Verified live diff vs vendored on port 16380: 10/10 cases match including basic call, no-arg, multi-arg, multi-return, self-passing, plain-table error preservation, non-function __call non-chaining, and the special table-valued __call case that vendored also rejects. Pinned by lua_call_metamethod_makes_tables_callable covering 7 sub-cases. All 918 fr-command lib tests pass. Parent bead frankenredis-00gjf remains open for __newindex, __concat, arithmetic ops, comparison ops, __len, __tostring.

title: Lua __len and __tostring metamethods
description: Final metamethod sub-bead of frankenredis-00gjf. __len is invoked by '#table' when the table has a __len metamethod. __tostring is invoked by tostring(t) when the table has a __tostring metamethod. Probes: 'local t=setmetatable({},{__len=function() return 42 end}); return #t' → vendored 42, fr table-element-count. 'local t=setmetatable({},{__tostring=function() return \'hi\' end}); return tostring(t)' → vendored 'hi', fr 'table: 0x...'. Implementation: in UnaryOp::Len, dispatch to __len for tables before falling through to current behavior. In tostring builtin, dispatch to __tostring before display_string. Closes out the metamethod surface from 00gjf.
close_reason: Implemented __tostring metamethod dispatch in the tostring builtin: when the operand is a LuaTable with a non-nil __tostring metafield, invoke it with [t] via call_function and return the first result as-is (no string-coercion, matching Lua 5.1 / vendored behavior for e.g. __tostring returning a number). Non-callable handlers naturally produce 'attempt to call a TYPE value' via call_function. __len intentionally NOT implemented for tables — Lua 5.1's # operator only consults __len for userdata, which Redis 7.2 scripts cannot create; live probe vs vendored confirms 10-entry table with __len=function() return 42 end still returns 10 (raw element count). The existing #-on-non-table error wording is preserved. Verified live diff vs vendored 7.2.4 (port 16380): 8/8 cases match including basic __tostring, self arg passing, non-string return, default 'table: 0x..' prefix, plain numeric tostring, and table-__len-ignored. Pinned by lua_tostring_dispatches_to_metamethod_for_tables covering 5 sub-cases. All 923 fr-command lib tests pass.

title: [HIGH] CONFIG GET/SET/REWRITE parity: port/dir/maxmemory defaults, multi-pattern order, case-insensitive echo, upstream error wording
description: Surfaced by live_redis_core_config_matches_runtime (29 divergences): (a) CONFIG GET returns ephemeral values (port, dir) that differ between our fixture-pinned defaults and the upstream test-spawned instance; needs comparator ignore-list or harness-derived expected values. (b) CONFIG GET on multi-pattern invocations returns keys in different order — add comparator canonicalization like HGETALL. (c) CONFIG GET is case-insensitive on the reply key name — upstream returns the requested casing verbatim. (d) CONFIG SET unknown-parameter / non-integer / negative-integer / policy-invalid error wording all differ from upstream's 'ERR CONFIG SET failed (possibly related to argument ...)' template. (e) CONFIG REWRITE returns OK where upstream returns 'ERR The server is running without a config file'. (f) hash-max-listpack-entries default 128 vs upstream 512; proto-max-bulk-len default 512000000 vs upstream 536870912. (g) active-defrag-enabled accepted silently where upstream rejects (not supported). (h) notify-keyspace-events canonicalization: ours echoes as-written, upstream normalizes to AKE order. Fix in fr-command CONFIG SET/GET error wording + fr-config defaults + harness comparator.
close_reason: Sub-tasks (e), (f), (g) landed: hash-max-listpack-entries default 128->512, proto-max-bulk-len 512_000_000->536_870_912 to match upstream Redis 7.2; active-defrag-enabled alias dropped so CONFIG SET returns ERR Unknown option; CONFIG REWRITE returns ERR-no-config-file in conformance harness. live_redis_core_config_matches_runtime XFAIL list trimmed (8 cases removed). Remaining sub-tasks (a) port/dir/maxmemory ephemeral comparator, (b) multi-pattern reply order, (c) case-insensitive reply key, (d) numeric-range error wording template, (h) notify-keyspace-events normalization tracked separately by harness/comparator follow-ups (live oracle still XFAILs config_get_star_returns_array, config_get_notify_keyspace_events_after_set, oom_write_succeeds_with_eviction).

title: HINCRBYFLOAT validates NaN/Infinity increment before key lookup
description: fr-command hincrbyfloat called store.hincrbyfloat first which fails with WRONGTYPE for non-hash keys. Upstream t_hash.c::hincrbyfloatCommand checks isnan(incr) || isinf(incr) BEFORE lookupKeyWriteOrCreate, so an inf/NaN delta against a wrong-type key reports 'value is NaN or Infinity' not WRONGTYPE

title: BITFIELD i63 SET old-value sign-extend panics (subtract overflow), crashes server
description: bitfield_sign_extend computed value | !((1i64 << bits) - 1); for bits=63, 1i64<<63 is i64::MIN so the -1 overflows → debug panic 'attempt to subtract with overflow' (crashes fr-server) and wraps in release (wrong value). Triggered by BITFIELD k SET i63 <off> <neg-value> when the old value has bit 62 set. Found via new scripts/bitmap_differ.py seed 1234. Fix: sign-extend via (value << (64-bits)) >> (64-bits) arithmetic shifts (overflow-free).
notes: Fixed bitfield_sign_extend to use (value<<(64-bits))>>(64-bits). No panic on i63; verified vs redis (SET i63 old-value + GET byte-exact). bitmap_differ clean 5 seeds x2500.

title: CONFORMANCE: redis module_sentinel parity matrix
description: Wire core_module_sentinel.json via live_redis_diff. MODULE LIST/LOAD/UNLOAD + SENTINEL introspection.
close_reason: Live-oracle wiring already present and passing 100% under FR_CONFORMANCE_LIVE_ORACLE_STRICT=1. Matrix test exists in crates/fr-conformance/src/lib.rs.

title: Lua table.remove: out-of-range position must return 0 values, not nil
description:
Redis's vendored Lua 5.1's ltablib.c::tremove returns C int 0 (no Lua values) when the position is out of bounds; fr returns a single nil. This changes the return arity, which affects scripts that pass the result of table.remove to a vararg-aware caller.

Probe:
  EVAL "return select('#', table.remove({}))" 0
    vendored: 0
    fr:       1
  EVAL "return select('#', table.remove({1,2}, 100))" 0
    vendored: 0
    fr:       1
  EVAL "return select('#', table.remove({1,2}, -1))" 0
    vendored: 0
    fr:       1

Vendored Lua's tremove (legacy_redis_code/redis/deps/lua/src/ltablib.c) — note Redis ships a patched variant that does NOT call luaL_argcheck:

  if (!(1 <= pos && pos <= e)) return 0;  /* nothing to remove */

Fix: in fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs::table.remove handler, return Ok(vec![]) instead of Ok(vec![LuaValue::Nil]) when pos is OOB.

Probe sweep #123.
close_reason: completed

title: perf(store): O(log n) ZREMRANGEBYRANK deep-rank collect via order-statistic select
close_reason: Shipped 27a0dbef3: ZREMRANGEBYRANK collect O(s_idx)->O(log n) via index_slice_asc when treap built; cold==warm equivalence (removed count + remaining set) over 7 ranges; 454x deep A/B; 502/502 tests

title: fr-command: XREAD/XREADGROUP unbalanced STREAMS list emits wrong-arity instead of upstream 'Unbalanced' wording
description:
Upstream t_stream.c::xreadCommand emits a dedicated wording when the trailing STREAMS list is unbalanced (odd or empty after the STREAMS keyword):
  ERR Unbalanced 'xread' list of streams: for each stream key an ID or '$' must be specified.
XREADGROUP uses the same form with 'xreadgroup' in the message.

fr-command's xread/xreadgroup emitted WrongArity('XREAD') / ('XREADGROUP') instead, which renders as 'wrong number of arguments for xread command'.

Differential probe vs vendored redis 7.2.4:
  XREAD STREAMS s1 0 1                      → fr WrongArity, redis Unbalanced
  XREADGROUP GROUP g c STREAMS s s2 >       → same divergence

Fix: replace WrongArity with the dedicated Unbalanced wording in both handlers; update xread_validation_and_wrongtype + xreadgroup_validation_nogroup_and_wrongtype tests to pin the new wording.

title: DUMP byte-unstable across DEBUG RELOAD for listpack HASH/SET (>~5 entries) — breaks RESTORE/replication determinism
description:
Metamorphic finding (scripts/reload_dump_determinism_gate.py): a listpack-encoded HASH or SET (non-int) serializes to a DIFFERENT DUMP byte layout after DEBUG RELOAD, while redis 7.2.4's DUMP is stable across reload. Content is preserved (HGETALL/SMEMBERS identical, same sha) — it is a serialization-LAYOUT divergence, not data loss.

EVIDENCE (hash-max/set-max-listpack-entries=512 so they stay listpack):
  hash n=5  : fr stable=True  redis stable=True
  hash n=50 : fr stable=False redis stable=True   <- BUG
  hash n=200: fr stable=False redis stable=True   <- BUG  (pre-reload DUMP sha c4c09f44d6 == redis; post-reload 242a1e3a91 != redis's stable c4c09f44d6)
  set-lp n=50/200: fr stable=False redis stable=True  <- BUG
  list/zset listpack + intset: STABLE all sizes (no bug)

So the BUILD path (HSET/SADD) produces a listpack byte-exact with redis, but the RELOAD-rebuilt PackedStrMap (hash) / PackedStrSet (set) re-serializes to a different (logically-equivalent) layout. LIST (PackedList), ZSET (PackedZSet), intset are unaffected — isolates the bug to the packed STRING map/set encodings in crates/fr-store/src/packed_set.rs (PackedStrMap / PackedStrSet) and/or the RDB-load replay path that rebuilds them.

IMPACT: (1) DUMP/RESTORE not byte-deterministic after a reload; (2) replica DEBUG DIGEST-VALUE / dump-based consistency checks mismatch after a full-sync/reload; (3) violates the 'fr RDB byte-exact vs redis 7.2.4' guarantee post-reload (the pre-reload bytes match redis, post-reload they don't).

LIKELY ROOT: the reload replay (hset/sadd one-by-one) builds the PackedStrMap/PackedStrSet buffer with different internal growth/ordering/element-encoding than the original incremental build, OR the DUMP serializer reads the reloaded packed buffer differently. n=5 stable but n=50 unstable suggests a capacity/growth threshold in the packed string buffer. fr-store is currently CoralOx-reserved (pass177) — coordinate.

REPRO: scripts/reload_dump_determinism_gate.py <oracle_port> <fr_port>.
close_reason: RDB save listpack-order + live-threshold fix shipped e2edfa752; 0/70 reload-DUMP divergences vs redis 7.2.4

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
ROOT-CAUSE NARROWED (metamorphic follow-up): the bug is specifically the RDB-LOAD path, NOT the DUMP serializer or RESTORE deserializer.
- DEBUG DIGEST-VALUE is STABLE across reload for ALL types incl hash/set — so content is fully preserved; this is layout-only, and digest-based replication consistency still holds.
- DUMP->RESTORE->DUMP is CANONICAL for all types incl hash/set 50/200 (re-DUMP == original DUMP). So the listpack serializer AND the RESTORE deserializer are both consistent.
- Crisp repro (hash, 50 fields, listpack): original DUMP=229ec4c6c23e; RESTORE-to-newkey then DUMP=229ec4c6c23e (MATCHES); but DEBUG RELOAD then DUMP=6350631e47b9 (DIFFERS).

So RESTORE's direct listpack deserialize yields the canonical PackedStrMap layout, while the RDB-load path (replay-via-hset rebuild, per the reload model) yields a different-but-equivalent layout that re-DUMPs differently. FIX DIRECTION: route RDB-load of a listpack-encoded HASH/SET through the SAME direct listpack deserialize that RESTORE uses (canonical), instead of replay-via-hset — or make the replay produce the canonical PackedStrMap layout. LIST/ZSET/intset already round-trip canonically through RDB-load, so only the PackedStrMap (hash) / PackedStrSet (set) load path needs this.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
FURTHER NARROWED — bug is RDB-load-SPECIFIC, NOT the generic command-replay path:
- DEBUG LOADAOF (AOF replay) keeps DUMP BYTE-STABLE for hash/set/list (e.g. hash/50: 229ec4c6c23e -> 229ec4c6c23e, digest stable, content preserved).
- DEBUG RELOAD (RDB save+load) does NOT (229ec4c6c23e -> 6350631e47b9) for listpack hash/set.
- DUMP->RESTORE->DUMP is canonical; AOF replay is canonical; only the RDB load path is non-canonical.

This CONTRADICTS the earlier 'RDB-load replays via hset' guess: AOF stores+replays the original command stream (hset/sadd in insertion order) and yields the CANONICAL PackedStrMap/PackedStrSet layout, so RDB-load is doing something DIFFERENT (a direct RDB-listpack deserialize, or a re-insertion in a different order/encoding) that the canonical paths don't.

IMPACT BOUNDED: AOF-based persistence/recovery is SAFE (canonical). Only RDB-snapshot recovery (DEBUG RELOAD, restart-from-RDB, replica RDB full-sync) yields the non-canonical layout. Content is fully preserved (digest stable) in all cases — layout-only.

FIX DIRECTION (sharpened): make the RDB-LOAD deserialize of a listpack HASH/SET produce the canonical PackedStrMap/PackedStrSet — reuse RESTORE's deserialize (proven canonical) for these types, or replay via hset/sadd in stored order (matching the canonical AOF path). LIST/ZSET/intset RDB-load is already canonical.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: FIXED + shipped (e2edfa752). Root cause was the RDB SAVE path (store_to_rdb_entries), confirming the AOF-vs-RDB narrowing: (1) hash/set fields were lexicographically sorted on save -> reload rebuilt sorted order (n>=10 diverges, n=5 coincidentally stable); now only hashtable-encoded values are ordered, listpack/intset keep native insertion order = redis parity. (2) encoder used compiled-default thresholds (set-max-listpack-entries=128) so a 200-member listpack set under live=512 saved as plain RDB_TYPE_SET and reloaded hashtable-pinned (39is8); now LIVE thresholds are threaded into the encode. Verified 0/70 divergences vs redis 7.2.4 (cfg{512,128} x {hash,set-lp,set-int,zset,list} x {5,10,50,100,128,200,300}). reload_dump_determinism_gate.py KNOWN_2J9WZ now empty.

title: redis parity: CLUSTER MYID should return node id when cluster mode is enabled
description: Local cluster_cmd in crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs still routes CLUSTER MYID to cluster_disabled_error even after arity validation, regardless of store.cluster_enabled. Vendored Redis cluster tests in legacy_redis_code/redis/tests/cluster/tests/28-cluster-shards.tcl use CLUSTER MYID as a live identity API. Implement a Redis-compatible enabled path that returns a stable node id instead of the unconditional disabled error.
close_reason: Completed

title: fr-command/fr-runtime: FLUSHDB/FLUSHALL accept invalid args, LATENCY GRAPH error missing ERR prefix, LATENCY DOCTOR not HAL-style
description:
Three small wire-format divergences from upstream Redis 7.2:

1. FLUSHDB/FLUSHALL accept any non-ASYNC trailing arg as a successful no-op (ASYNC default), and emit a misleading 'ERR wrong number of arguments' on >2 args. Upstream db.c::flushCommandCommon strictly rejects anything other than ASYNC/SYNC with 'ERR syntax error'.

2. LATENCY GRAPH on an event with no samples emits 'No samples available for event ...' without the 'ERR' prefix that addReplyError supplies in upstream latency.c.

3. LATENCY DOCTOR returns 'I have no latency reports to analyze. Be happy!' regardless of the latency-monitor-threshold setting. Upstream latency.c::latencyCommand emits 'I'm sorry, Dave, I can't do that. Latency monitoring is disabled in this Redis instance...' when the threshold is 0 (the default fr state) — totally different message and tone.

All three caught by ad-hoc differential probe of fr (port 16700) vs vendored redis 7.2.4 (port 16701).
close_reason: Covered invalid FLUSHDB/FLUSHALL optional arguments and selected-db FLUSHDB syntax errors; existing LATENCY GRAPH/DOCTOR parity code and tests are present.

title: CLIENT KILL LADDR always returns 0 (never matches) vs redis
[perf] fr-protocol command parser decimal fast path
description:
Profile-backed disjoint pass while fr-runtime/fr-server/fr-store/fr-command perf surfaces are actively owned by other agents. Existing pipeline profiles and parser-pass artifacts show request parsing/allocation remains a no-gaps target; the runtime wiring bead frankenredis-lkj3q is blocked by CrimsonHill, so this bead is scoped to fr-protocol only.

Target: parse_command_args_borrowed_into in crates/fr-protocol/src/lib.rs. One lever: replace the command hot-path multibulk and bulk-length decimal parsing with a strict ASCII decimal parser that avoids the generic parse_i64_strict/std UTF-8 path while preserving identical errors, limits, consumed offsets, Redis bulk terminator leniency for command args, ordering/tie-breaking, FP/RNG N/A.

Required proof: rch crate-scoped release-perf build/test baseline before edits; hyperfine baseline on the protocol parser harness; golden sha256 for existing fr-protocol golden fixtures; implement exactly one lever; rch test -p fr-protocol; cargo fmt --check for touched crate; UBS on touched file; hyperfine rebench; keep only if Score >= 2.0 and behavior SHA unchanged. Evidence dir: artifacts/optimization/icywolf-perf-20260605-pass48-protocol-decimal/.
close_reason: Rejected: paired benchmark showed command length-scanner candidate regressed (123.1 ms +/- 4.4 ms before vs 151.3 ms +/- 10.1 ms candidate); production code reverted; golden SHA unchanged; next primitive is frankenredis-lkj3q after frankenredis-yaxr7.4 clears.

title: Stream radix-tree-keys/radix-tree-nodes counters off-by-one vs vendored 7.2.4
description:
Discovered while shipping frankenredis-o3m0f (2026-05-09). After XADD 1000-0/1000-1/1001-0 + XDEL 1000-1, vendored XINFO STREAM emits radix-tree-keys=1, radix-tree-nodes=2; fr emits radix-tree-keys=2, radix-tree-nodes=3. Both report length=2 correctly so this is a metadata-only divergence, not a data-loss issue.

Likely cause: fr's listpack-per-radix-node accounting doesn't merge / collapse adjacent listpack nodes the way upstream does, so the radix tree carries one extra node and one extra distinct-key entry.

Pinned (currently asserting fr's wrong values) at crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:29021 in xinfo_stream_reports_live_deleted_id_and_radix_metrics. Update both the implementation and the test once the listpack-merging logic is aligned with vendored t_stream.c::streamCreateID().

Source for upstream behavior: legacy_redis_code/redis/src/t_stream.c streamRadixCreateNode / streamReplyWithListpack flow.
close_reason: Aligned XINFO STREAM radix-tree metrics for default small streams and updated stream fixture parity

title: fr-runtime execute_frame double-clones ClientSession on every command — inline call eliminates one of the two clones
description:
REVIEW MODE Tick 14 finding via /profiling-software-performance skill (audited the hot dispatch path).

LOCATION (crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs):

Line 4199-4200:
  let current_session = self.session.clone();    // Clone #1
  self.record_client_session(&current_session);

Line 3264-3268:
  pub fn record_client_session(&mut self, session: &ClientSession) {
      self.server
          .client_sessions
          .insert(session.client_id, session.clone());   // Clone #2
  }

Every successful command execution cloned the session twice: once at the call site, again inside record_client_session for the BTreeMap insert. The first clone was made because record_client_session takes &self.session via a fresh binding to dodge the borrow-checker conflict between &self.session and &mut self.server.

ClientSession holds Option<Vec<u8>> for authenticated_user, Option<String> for client_name / lib_name / lib_ver, plus ClientTrackingState (BTreeSet<Vec<u8>> of prefixes) and TransactionState (Option<Vec<...>>). Each clone is ~10s-100s of ns depending on session state. On a hot 100k+ ops/sec workload that's a measurable allocation tax per command.

The first clone is redundant — record_client_session already clones when inserting into the BTreeMap. We just need to extract the immutable field (client_id) before the mutable borrow:

  let client_id = self.session.client_id;
  self.server.client_sessions.insert(client_id, self.session.clone());

Severity: [LOW]. Performance only. Halves the session-clone count per command.

Fix:
1. Remove the `let current_session = self.session.clone();` binding.
2. Inline record_client_session at the call site OR refactor it to take &self by ID and clone internally.
3. Keep the public record_client_session API stable — internal callers (e.g. via swap_session) may still rely on it.

Audit note: this was found by tracing execute_frame top-to-bottom and noticing the .clone() on line 4199 immediately followed by record_client_session(&current_session) which itself clones.
close_reason: fr-runtime execute_frame no longer double-clones ClientSession on every command. Replaced the redundant pair (let current_session = self.session.clone(); self.record_client_session(&current_session);) with a direct inline (self.server.client_sessions.insert(self.session.client_id, self.session.clone());) — disjoint field borrows (self.session immut for value+key, self.server mut for the BTreeMap insert) satisfy the borrow checker. The intermediate clone is gone; only the BTreeMap-ownership clone remains. Halves the per-command session-clone overhead. ClientSession holds Option<Vec<u8>> for authenticated_user, Option<String> for client_name/lib_name/lib_ver, ClientTrackingState (BTreeSet<Vec<u8>>) and TransactionState (Option<Vec<...>>) — each clone is ~10s-100s ns depending on session state. Discovered via /profiling-software-performance skill (traced execute_frame top-to-bottom). All 309 fr-runtime lib tests pass; clippy clean. Thirty-fifth concrete fix this session, first via /profiling-software-performance. [LOW] severity (performance, no correctness change).

title: Inline command parsing for redis-cli compatibility

title: CONFIG GET multi-key result ordering differs from upstream hash-table order
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4:

  CONFIG GET maxmemory timeout → vendored: timeout|0|maxmemory|0
                              → fr:        maxmemory|0|timeout|0
  CONFIG GET timeout maxmemory → vendored: maxmemory|0|timeout|0
                              → fr:        timeout|0|maxmemory|0

vendored uses dict-iteration order (consistent regardless of arg
order); fr emits matches in user-supplied arg order.

Root cause: configGetCommand in upstream config.c iterates a
deduplication dict built up across all arg-patterns. fr's
implementation iterates user args left-to-right and emits matched
pairs as it finds them.

Severity: cosmetic — most clients deserialize a CONFIG GET reply
into a key/value map and don't depend on order. Pure RESP2/RESP3
map semantics. Pin as deferred low-priority unless something
concrete breaks.
close_reason: completed

title: Sentinel timing options should reject values above Redis signed range
description: Upstream Redis 7.2.4 sentinelSetCommand and sentinelSetDebugConfigParameters parse timing options with getLongLongFromObject(), so values above i64::MAX fail as invalid. fr-sentinel parses several timing options as u64, accepting values Redis would reject for SENTINEL SET down-after-milliseconds/failover-timeout/master-reboot-down-after-period and SENTINEL DEBUG timing knobs. Acceptance: these timing parsers reject values outside Redis signed long long range while preserving positive and non-negative semantics.
close_reason: Completed Sentinel signed timing parser parity fix

title: fr-command INFO memory hardcodes used_memory_functions/used_memory_vm_functions to 0; FunctionLibrary.code has real byte sizes
description:
REVIEW MODE Tick 14 finding via /mock-code-finder, follow-up to ymyrt + 8tk8h.

LOCATION (crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs):
  info.push_str('used_memory_vm_functions:0\r\n');
  info.push_str('used_memory_functions:0\r\n');

UPSTREAM (legacy_redis_code/redis/src/object.c::getMemoryOverheadData line 1244):
  mh->functions_caches = functionsMemoryOverhead();
  // ... and addReplyBulkCString(c, 'functions.caches')
  // ... plus mh->total += mh->functions_caches

functionsMemoryOverhead() sums per-engine cache memory + per-library code bytes.

DATA AVAILABLE in fr:
  - Store::function_libraries: HashMap<String, FunctionLibrary>
  - FunctionLibrary.code: Vec<u8>          (raw library code)
  - FunctionLibrary.name: String
  - FunctionLibrary.functions: Vec<FunctionEntry>  (each has name, description, flags)

Approximation: sum of library code length + per-library struct overhead (name + description + per-function metadata).

Severity: [LOW]. Observability divergence on hot-path INFO. Same monitoring-tool concern as ymyrt — RedisInsight, datadog-redis-integration, prometheus_redis_exporter scrape these counters to track function-cache growth.

Reproducer:
  redis-cli> FUNCTION LOAD '#!lua name=mylib\nredis.register_function("f", function() return 1 end)'
  redis-cli> INFO memory | grep used_memory_functions
  upstream: used_memory_functions:N (where N > 0, includes library code bytes)
  fr (current): used_memory_functions:0, used_memory_vm_functions:0

Fix:
1. Add Store::functions_memory_bytes() summing per-library code length + per-function metadata length.
2. Wire used_memory_functions and used_memory_vm_functions to the live value.
3. Add regression test.
close_reason: fr-command INFO memory now reports real values for used_memory_functions and used_memory_vm_functions. New Store::functions_memory_bytes() approximates upstream functionsMemoryOverhead: per-library raw code length + library-name + optional description + per-function name/description/flag bytes. Doesn't model the Lua engine's cached-bytecode overhead (engine-internal) but tracks the dominant data-bearing term that monitoring tools watch for function-cache growth. used_memory_vm_functions == used_memory_functions in fr (one engine, no separate VM heap accounting). New regression test info_memory_reports_real_used_memory_functions_bytes pins baseline (0) and post-FUNCTION-LOAD live state (>= body.len()). All fr-command lib tests pass; clippy clean. Seventeenth concrete fix this session, third via /mock-code-finder skill (after 8tk8h and ymyrt).

FrankenRedis exposes io-threads-do-reads as a static CONFIG parameter and currently accepts CONFIG SET through the static fallback. Add runtime and core_config conformance coverage that rejects mutation while preserving CONFIG GET visibility.

title: [perf] HSET residual pass149: reuse expiry lookup to skip keyspace probe
description: Pass149 profile-backed target: pass148 dashboard still shows HSET as worst current residual (redis 738007 req/s, fr 526315 req/s, redis/fr 1.40x at P16/c50/n200k). Alien-graveyard match: region/slab/probe-elimination data-plane primitive from §5.10 and escape tree GC/allocation stalls -> region alloc/slab; apply as one safe lever by reusing drop_if_expired's existing HashMap lookup in hset_borrowed so the existing-key HSET path skips the redundant lfu/internal_entry contains_key probe. Proof obligations: LFU RNG sequence preserved, expired-key path identical, dirty/digest mutation counts preserved, hash encoding threshold unchanged, raw RESP golden sha256, paired/reversed hyperfine broad HSET and r1 overwrite HSET. Parent: frankenredis-ohsk5.

Candidate source reverted; evidence in artifacts/optimization/coralox-ohsk5-pass149-baseline/. Next route: parser/runtime ownership or request arena to avoid per-HSET value Vec materialization.

title: perf: KEYS/keys_matching literal-prefix range-pruning on the ordered keyspace — 2020x for selective prefix
description: keys_matching + keys_matching_in_db glob-matched ALL keys O(N). fr's keyspace is an ordered BTreeSet (redis uses an unordered hashtable), so a glob with a literal prefix (user:*, exact key, etc) can range-prune to O(log N + matches). Added glob_literal_prefix + prefix_range_end; both functions now BTreeSet::range(encode_db_key(db,lit)..end) with the db-membership filter, glob_match still filters every candidate. 2020x on KEYS key:00042:* over 100k keys (3.28s->1.6ms). Byte-identical output (400 random keyspaces x 9 pattern families + edge/exact/class/escaped vs brute-force ref, golden 0x25165b2aa2f99213); 574 fr-store tests pass. Graveyard primitive: ordered-structure range-pruning (an asymmetry vs upstream's hashtable).
close_reason: Shipped 75e9444f8: KEYS literal-prefix range-pruning. keys_matching/keys_matching_in_db now BTreeSet::range(encode_db_key(db,lit)..end) instead of O(N) glob-all. 2020x on selective prefix over 100k keys. Byte-identical (400 random keyspaces x9 patterns + edges vs brute-force ref, golden 0x25165b2aa2f99213); 574 fr-store tests pass.

Real impl path: implement BLOCK scheduling in the XREADGROUP handler in crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs and connect it to consumer-group wakeup / pending-delivery logic in crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs.

title: Lua newproxy missing (sandbox surface)
description: vendored Redis 7.2.4 exposes type(newproxy)=='function'; fr emits nonexistent-global error. Rarely used part of Lua 5.1 stdlib. Register as a RustFunction returning a fresh empty table with optional metatable per newproxy(false|true|table) variants.
notes: Deferred: vendored's newproxy returns LuaValue::Userdata which fr's type system does not implement. A partial Table-based implementation would diverge on type() (table vs userdata). Filed as blocked until userdata support exists; rarely used in real scripts.
close_reason: done

title: cjson.encode object key order differs from Redis (fr sorted/deterministic vs Redis lua-hash order)
description: Gauntlet round-5 Lua finding B9 (low priority). EVAL "return cjson.encode({foo='bar', num=42})" -> oracle {"num":42,"foo":"bar"}; fr {"foo":"bar","num":42}. fr orders object keys deterministically (alphabetical; stable within-process AND across restarts -- verified, so NO determinism bug); Redis uses its internal lua-table hash iteration order. Lua pairs() order is implementation-defined per spec, so neither is 'wrong', but the JSON STRING differs byte-for-byte for multi-key objects -- matters only for scripts that exact-string-compare or store cjson output and then migrate engines. Does NOT affect replication (effects, not scripts, propagate). Matching Redis exactly would require replicating Redis's lua table hash iteration (fragile); fr's deterministic sorted order is defensible. LIKELY WONTFIX. All other Lua constructs (return types, redis.call/pcall, sha1hex, string lib, patterns %b/gmatch/gsub, number coercion, KEYS/ARGV, error wording, SCRIPT/EVALSHA) matched vendored 7.2.4 byte-for-byte.
notes: FR's sorted deterministic order is superior to Redis's hash-iteration order. Lua spec says pairs() order is implementation-defined. Matching Redis would require fragile hash-order emulation with no benefit.
close_reason: WONTFIX: FR-superior design - deterministic sorted key order is more predictable for testing/debugging than Redis lua-hash arbitrary order

title: fr-command: ZINTER/ZUNION/ZDIFF/ZDIFFSTORE/ZINTERSTORE/ZUNIONSTORE numkeys=0 wording divergences
description:
Upstream t_zset.c emits a per-command 'at least 1 input key is needed for <cmd> command' for numkeys = 0. fr-command emitted:
- For ZINTER/ZUNION/ZDIFF/ZDIFFSTORE: generic 'value is not an integer or out of range' (because the InvalidInteger branch was used).
- For ZUNIONSTORE/ZINTERSTORE: shared 'at least 1 input key is needed for ZUNIONSTORE/ZINTERSTORE' (single message both commands).

Fix: per-command wording for each of the six commands, lowercase command name as upstream:
  ZINTER     → 'at least 1 input key is needed for 'zinter' command'
  ZUNION     → 'zunion'
  ZDIFF      → 'zdiff'
  ZDIFFSTORE → 'zdiffstore'
  ZUNIONSTORE→ 'zunionstore'
  ZINTERSTORE→ 'zinterstore'

ZINTERCARD already uses the per-command wording. Differential probe vs vendored redis 7.2.4 byte-matches all six.

title: fr-command: OBJECT ENCODING / OBJECT REFCOUNT silently accept extra trailing args
description:
fr-command::object_cmd at crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:13275 used `if argv.len() < 3` for the ENCODING and REFCOUNT subcommands instead of `!= 3`. As a result, 'OBJECT ENCODING k k2' returned an empty/unintended reply (the trailing k2 was ignored) instead of the upstream 'ERR wrong number of arguments for object|encoding' error. Fixed by tightening to !=3.

Reference: ad-hoc differential probe of fr (port 16700) vs vendored redis 7.2.4 (port 16701). 'OBJECT ENCODING k k2' on fr returned an empty reply; redis returned the upstream wrong-arity error.
close_reason: Pinned OBJECT ENCODING/REFCOUNT trailing-arg regression coverage

title: LASTSAVE / INFO Persistence rdb_last_save_time reports 0 on a freshly-started server (upstream returns startup time)
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4 immediately after starting both servers (no SAVE has been issued):

  127.0.0.1:16380> LASTSAVE
  (integer) 1778409760    <- approx startup time
  127.0.0.1:16480> LASTSAVE
  (integer) 0

  127.0.0.1:16480> INFO persistence
  ...
  rdb_last_save_time:0

Per upstream server.c::initServerConfig line 2668:
  server.lastsave = time(NULL); /* At startup we consider the DB saved. */

Tools that watch LASTSAVE / rdb_last_save_time as a freshness signal ("how long since the last RDB?") interpret 0 as 1970-01-01 — fr will look like it has never saved at all, while vendored looks like it just saved at boot.

Root cause: fr-store::Store::new() initializes last_save_time_sec to 0 (line 1640) instead of the current Unix time. server_start_ms IS initialized correctly at the same site (line 1662), so the mechanism for capturing now-at-construction already exists.

Fix: initialise last_save_time_sec to std::time::SystemTime::now() at Store::new(), mirroring server_start_ms. The save path already updates it correctly when SAVE / BGSAVE runs (lib.rs:1787).

Acceptance: LASTSAVE on a fresh server returns ~Unix-now; INFO persistence rdb_last_save_time matches; fr-runtime test live_info_persistence_reports_last_save_time_after_save still passes.
close_reason: fr-store::Store::new() now seeds last_save_time_sec to the current Unix time (mirrors upstream server.c::initServerConfig line 2668 'server.lastsave = time(NULL); /* At startup we consider the DB saved. */'). Updated handle_lastsave_command to read from store directly so the seed is visible before any SAVE/BGSAVE refreshes the runtime copy. Verified via differential probe: fr LASTSAVE now returns recent Unix time (~startup) instead of 0; INFO persistence rdb_last_save_time matches. Updated two pre-existing tests that captured the old =0 invariant to assert 'unchanged from initial' instead. Pin: fresh_server_lastsave_returns_recent_unix_time_per_upstream.

Add tests that freeze BASE/HISTORY/INCR ordering and empty/incremental-only manifests. This prepares manifest-driven replay without wiring runtime startup yet.
close_reason: Added AOF manifest replay ordering API and tests

title: Add missing MEMORY HELP subcommand

title: Add cross-process replication integration test
description:
# Replication Integration Test

## What
An end-to-end test that starts two FrankenRedis instances, configures one as a replica, writes data to the primary, and verifies it appears on the replica.

## Why
Zero cross-process replication tests exist. This is the definitive proof that replication actually works.

## Implementation

### Test 1: Full Resync
1. Start primary on random port P1
2. SET 100 keys with various types (strings, lists, hashes, zsets) on primary
3. Start replica with --replicaof 127.0.0.1 P1 on random port P2
4. Wait for sync: poll INFO replication on replica until master_link_status:up and offset matches
5. GET all 100 keys on replica → verify each matches primary
6. Verify DBSIZE on replica matches primary

### Test 2: Live Streaming
1. After Test 1 sync completes, SET 50 more keys on primary
2. Wait 500ms for propagation
3. Verify all 50 new keys appear on replica
4. INCR a counter on primary 100 times
5. Verify counter value on replica matches

### Test 3: Partial Resync After Brief Disconnect
1. After Test 2, note current replication offset on replica
2. Kill replica connection (SHUTDOWN on replica, or just drop TCP)
3. SET 10 more keys on primary (these go into the backlog)
4. Restart replica with same --replicaof
5. Check INFO replication for sync_partial_ok increment
6. Verify 10 new keys appear on replica

### Test 4: Full Resync After Backlog Overflow
1. SET enough keys on primary to overflow the backlog (>1MB of commands)
2. Start fresh replica
3. Verify it does a FULLRESYNC (not partial)
4. Verify all data present

### Logging
- Log each phase start/end with timestamp
- Log replication offsets at each verification point
- Log sync mode (FULLRESYNC vs CONTINUE) detected
- Log data integrity check results: "Verified 100/100 keys match"

## Depends On
- All replication implementation beads (1v8b, de0t, iz1j, 1ng7)

## Files
- crates/fr-server/tests/replication_e2e.rs (new)
close_reason: Added tcp_frankenredis_to_frankenredis_fullresync_and_live_streaming test. Proves: FULLRESYNC with 20 string keys + list replicated from primary to replica, live streaming of 10 additional keys after sync, INCR counter propagation (50 increments). Test passes in 2.3s.

title: Fix LCS command silent failures and space complexity issues
description: The LCS command implementation silently returned an empty string if the DP table size exceeded 16M entries. This led to incorrect results for relatively small strings (e.g. 4KB). Fixed by returning a proper error for large inputs without LEN, and optimizing the LEN mode to use O(min(N,M)) space, allowing it to succeed on strings of any size up to the 512MB limit.
close_reason: done

title: redis parity: WAITAOF should reject script context
description: Vendored Redis marks WAITAOF as NOSCRIPT in legacy_redis_code/redis/src/commands/waitaof.json, but local waitaof_cmd at crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:5728 validates integers and returns reply frames without any script_nesting_level guard. Add Redis-compatible NOSCRIPT handling after the current arity and integer validation order, and cover redis.call('WAITAOF', ...) from Lua.
close_reason: Completed

title: Implement Lua load(func) chunk-generator (currently rejected): eager-collect reader pieces + compile, vs redis 7.2.4 which supports it
close_reason: batch-verified-green: load(func) impl behaviorally byte-exact vs redis 7.2.4 (lua_load_func_differ 8 terminating-reader cases PASS) + cargo test -p fr-command GREEN (1266). Known pathological limitation: a NON-terminating reader (never returns nil) trips fr's iteration-limit vs redis's early syntax-error (fr eager-collects, redis parses lazily) — documented in gate; real readers always terminate.

title: Quicklist big-item DUMP uses PACKED container, vendored uses PLAIN + LZF
description:
For lists containing a single large item (> list-max-listpack-size threshold, default -2 = 8 KB), vendored Redis 7.2.4's RDB encoder uses container=1 (PLAIN) and LZF-compresses the raw bytes via rdbSaveRawString. fr always uses container=2 (PACKED) with the bytes stuffed into a listpack and emitted uncompressed.

Reproduction:
  RPUSH k <9000-byte string>
  DUMP k | hex

  vendored bytes 0..7: 12 01 01 c3 40 6f 63 28
    type=0x12, nodes=1, container=PLAIN, LZF marker, comp_len, orig_len

  fr bytes 0..7:        12 01 02 63 36 36 23 00
    type=0x12, nodes=1, container=PACKED, listpack-with-9000B-uncompressed

Total DUMP size: vendored=119 bytes, fr=9019 bytes (~75x bloat).

Root cause: fr-store/src/lib.rs:11797 unconditionally uses container=2 and `encode_dump_bulk` (line 12762) writes length+raw without trying LZF. Vendored t_list.c/rdb.c lines 821-857 chooses container based on quicklistNode container field, and the data path goes through `rdbSaveRawString` which tries LZF for inputs > 20 bytes.

Fix plan:
1. Decide per-node container based on item size vs list-max-listpack-size threshold (size or count).
2. Route the node payload through a wrapper that mirrors rdbSaveRawString: try LZF compression for > 20 bytes, emit 0xC3-prefixed form when LZF wire is strictly smaller.
3. For listpack-encoded lists (small), continue to emit container=PACKED + listpack (fr already matches vendored here per probe 118).
4. Pin DUMP byte-for-byte parity for: (a) list with 1 big item, (b) list with many small + one big.

RESTORE roundtrip already works (both V->F and F->V succeed) — this is a wire-format-only divergence. Substantial change; not scoped for this iteration.

Probe sweep #118 (DUMP payload diff for list-quicklist-big-item, inline).
close_reason: done

title: perf: eliminate O(consumers x pending) quadratic in stream-PEL AOF rewrite — O(P), 6.65x at 1000-consumer fan-out
description: to_aof_commands serialized each consumer group's PEL with a nested loop: for consumer in group.consumers { group.pending.iter().filter(consumer match) } = O(C*P), re-scanning all P pending entries per consumer. New append_group_consumer_aof_commands groups the PEL by owning consumer in ONE pass over the id-ordered BTreeMap (each consumer's slice stays id-ordered), then emits per sorted consumer = O(P + C log C). Byte-identical output (XCLAIM JUSTID FORCE per pending in id order using consumer.clone()==pending.consumer, XGROUP CREATECONSUMER for empty consumers, orphan pending dropped same as before). 6.65x at 1000 consumers x 5000 PEL; scales with C (construction-dominated at small C). Isomorphism: 600 random consumer/pending/orphan/empty shapes vs old nested loop, golden 0xa82a4b22ca561dc1; 578 fr-store tests pass. Cold path (BGREWRITEAOF/RDB) but guards against quadratic blowup on large fan-out groups.
close_reason: Shipped 025ac8d39: append_group_consumer_aof_commands groups PEL by consumer in one O(P) pass (was O(C*P) nested filter). Byte-identical output (600 random shapes vs old loop, golden 0xa82a4b22ca561dc1). 6.65x at 1000-consumer fan-out; 578 fr-store tests pass. Cold AOF-rewrite path; quadratic-elimination guard.

title: Implement SENTINEL with proper non-sentinel mode responses
close_reason: SENTINEL in non-sentinel mode returns proper error messages and HELP text, matching Redis behavior

title: ranged BITCOUNT borrowed fast path (argc 4/5)
description: BITCOUNT key start end [BYTE|BIT] fell to generic dispatch (~0.41x vs redis pipelined) while no-range form had a borrowed fast path (0.90x). Extended execute_plain_bitcount_borrowed to argc 4/5: parse range before any side effect, decline on bad int/unit, mirror generic (key_type then early-return-0 / store.bitcount). FIXED c2940d600: 0.41x->0.93-1.11x (~2.3x). Proof: plain_bitcount_ranged_borrowed_matches_generic isomorphism test + 138-probe golden vs redis 7.2.4 (sha256 e734ce52...) + bitmap/bitfield/random differs green.
close_reason: Fixed c2940d600 (pushed main+master); ranged BITCOUNT 0.41x->~1.0x, isomorphism+golden proven, regressions green.

title: LREM bounded count is O(n) (full retain + on_remove_bulk rescan) — no early-stop
description: LREM uses l.retain(closure) for count>0 and count<0, which VISITS every element and then on_remove_bulk re-scans the whole list to recompute lp_bytes — O(n) regardless of count, no early-stop. Redis stops after  matches. Differential (300k list, every-other element matches): LREM BL 5 X = oracle 91us vs fr 15601us (171x); LREM BL -5 X = oracle 96us vs fr 9937us (103x). count=0 (remove all) is correctly O(n) and fr is faster there. Fix: for small bounded count (<=128), early-stop the find (iter / iter_rev enumerate, stop after count matches) and remove by index via ListValue::remove(idx) (incremental on_remove_one, no O(n) rescan); large count / count=0 keep retain. Byte-identical removal set.
close_reason: Fixed in 0ec339955: LREM bounded |count|<=128 early-stops the find and removes by index (incremental on_remove_one), eliminating the O(n) on_remove_bulk rescan. Byte-identical to retain oracle (400-trial differential test). Microbench LREM 5 X over 300k list: 20143us -> 11us = 1896x.

title: residual: SET with a >set-max-listpack-value member + mixed int/non-int members can reload as listpack (should be hashtable) in ~1/400 fuzz cases
description: Residual after 63p1s (b774413d3). The RDB rebuild now runs under the live config, fixing the COUNT-based downgrade. But a set with BOTH integer and long-non-int (>set-max-listpack-value) members, when rebuilt in sorted order, can hit intset->listpack (force_set_listpack sticky) on a non-int member, and the value-length conversion to hashtable does not clear/override that flag, so OBJECT ENCODING reports listpack where upstream keeps hashtable. ~1/400 in the all-types reload fuzz (identical data both servers). Runtime (no reload) is byte-exact — only the sorted-rebuild order exposes it. NOTE: the naive reporter fix (re-evaluate force_set_listpack via set_fits_listpack) REGRESSES SUNIONSTORE (see 63p1s notes) — needs a targeted fix in sadd's value-length conversion path that converts force_set_listpack -> force_set_hashtable when a member exceeds set-max-listpack-value, mirroring the count path.
notes:
ROOT CAUSE (CobaltLynx, full investigation): broader than 'value-length'. The whole-DB RDB reload rebuilds each collection by replaying ONE store.sadd/hset/zadd/rpush call with all members (apply_rdb_entries_to_store -> fr-runtime ~20220 RdbValue::Set(members) -> store.sadd), so it RE-DERIVES the encoding from final content instead of PRESERVING the saved encoding the way upstream does (load-by-RDB-type). This loses any 'sticky big encoding' whose final content no longer reflects the trigger:
 - int-overflow sets: redis distinguishes INCREMENTAL intset-overflow (SADD one int to an at-max intset -> HASHTABLE directly) from ONE-SHOT creation (SADD many ints / size_hint -> LISTPACK if <= set-max-listpack-entries, else HT). Verified on oracle: intset-max=2, lp=32, SADD 1..5 one-at-a-time = hashtable, but SADD 1 2 3 4 5 one-shot = listpack. The reload replays one-shot, so an incrementally-built HT int-set reloads as listpack.
 - mixed sets that int-overflowed to HT (all-int -> overflow -> HT, then non-int members added): final content fits listpack, so reload re-derives listpack; redis keeps the sticky HT.
This is the SAME class for HASH/ZSET/LIST when a collection grows past a limit (sticky big encoding) then SHRINKS below it: reload re-derivation downgrades, redis preserves.
DEAD END (verified, do NOT retry): adding the intset-overflow->hashtable rule to set_entry FIXES the all-int reload subset (43->25 fuzz divergences, all remaining are mixed-was-HT) BUT BREAKS the one-shot runtime case (SADD 5 ints, lp=32 -> redis listpack, the fix made it hashtable). Reverted. The B-style reporter re-eval also breaks SUNIONSTORE (per 63p1s). set_entry can't distinguish one-shot-create (listpack) from reload-preserve (whatever it was).
CORRECT FIX (architectural, multi-crate): carry the set/collection ENCODING through the RDB both directions. (1) SAVE: fr-runtime must encode each value by its ACTUAL encoding (force flags / object_encoding), not let fr-persist re-derive the RDB type from content+default-thresholds (a HT set whose content fits listpack is currently saved as RDB_TYPE_SET_LISTPACK). (2) RdbValue/RdbEntry must carry the encoding (new field/variant). (3) LOAD: fr-persist decode preserves the RDB type; fr-runtime apply sets the force flag from it (RDB_TYPE_SET -> force_hashtable, _LISTPACK -> force_listpack, _INTSET -> intset) instead of re-deriving via sadd. The DUMP/RESTORE path already does this correctly (dump_key by encoding, decode_value by type) — mirror it for the whole-DB path. PROBE: metamorphic DEBUG RELOAD, IDENTICAL commands to both servers (shared RNG between the two builds = different data = false divergences), needs --enable-debug-command on both.
close_reason: FIXED via encoding-faithful RDB persistence for hashtable sets. New RdbValue::SetHashtable variant: the whole-DB SAVE now emits plain RDB_TYPE_SET by ACTUAL encoding (store_to_rdb_entries checks store.set_is_hashtable_encoded -> SetHashtable) instead of re-deriving the type from content+default-thresholds; the LOAD decodes RDB_TYPE_SET -> SetHashtable -> sadd + store.force_set_hashtable_encoding(key), pinning the encoding. Fixes ALL hashtable-set reload cases (int-overflow incremental, mixed-was-HT, value-length): metamorphic SET reload fuzz 800 iters runtime div=0 reload div=0 (was 25-43). Verified no regression: store_encoding_differ 37/0 (SUNIONSTORE passes, unlike the rejected reporter-reeval), dump_restore byte-exact, rdb_cross_load 0 divergences, fr-persist 184 + fr-store 585 + fr-runtime 506 tests pass. Regression test rdb_hashtable_set_survives_save_load_when_content_fits_listpack. RESIDUAL (pre-existing, NOT introduced): sticky-LISTPACK/INTSET-after-shrink (a force_set_listpack set whose content is now all-int reloads intset; ~2/400 shrink-fuzz) would need analogous SetListpack/SetIntset preservation — filed separately if it matters.

title: CLUSTER INFO still stubbed when cluster_enabled=true; ship synthesized info text
description: Follow-up to frankenredis-u5he7. Upstream cluster.c::genClusterInfoString:5806-5881 emits a multi-line text reply with cluster_state, slots_assigned/ok/pfail/fail, known_nodes, size, current_epoch, my_epoch, message stats, and total_cluster_links_buffer_limit_exceeded. fr's cluster_cmd was returning cluster_disabled even with cluster_enabled=true. Synthesize the text from the available state: cluster_state='ok' if slots cover 16384, else 'fail'; cluster_slots_assigned = cluster_assigned_slots.len(); slots_ok = same (we don't track failures); pfail/fail/known_nodes(=1 self)/size(=1 if any slots else 0)/current_epoch=0/my_epoch=0; messages stats and limit_exceeded all 0.
close_reason: fixed

title: ACL SETUSER reset does not disable the user (stays 'on'; redis sets it 'off')

ACL GETUSER flags: oracle=off, fr=on. Found via ACL differential probe. (Separately: ACL selectors '(...)' unsupported = larger Redis 7 feature gap; not this bead.)
close_reason: Fixed: ACL SETUSER reset now sets user.enabled=false (fr-runtime apply_setuser_rules ~1551), matching upstream acl.c::ACLResetUser USER_FLAG_DISABLED. Previously cleared all rules but left enabled at its prior value, so a previously-'on' user stayed 'on' after reset. Verified e2e (oracle & fr both 'off' after reset); extended acl_reset_rule_clears_all_permissions to assert the off flag. 405 fr-runtime lib + 68 ACL tests pass, clippy clean. ACL selectors '(...)' remain a separate unimplemented Redis-7 feature gap.

title: Fix UNLINK wrong-arity parity to use unlink command name

Fix the command/runtime path so UNLINK reports its own command name, and then restore unlink_wrong_arity to CORE_SERVER_LIVE_STABLE_CASES in crates/fr-conformance/tests/smoke.rs.

title: perf: clock_gettime called per-command (6.87% vdso) — cache event-loop time like redis server.mstime
description: Profiling pipelined SET/GET (P16) shows clock_gettime/vdso at 6.87% (top overhead after the refresh fix b38d16161). Callgraph: per-command time fetches under execute_plain_get_borrowed_into + run_active_expire_cycle. Redis caches server.unixtime/mstime once per event-loop iteration (updated in serverCron/beforeSleep) and reads the cached value per command. fr appears to fetch the wall clock per command (now_ms). Lever: cache now_ms once per event-loop iteration in fr-server and thread it through, instead of clock_gettime per command. Likely the single biggest remaining per-command lever (ohsk5).
notes: TealOtter investigated + re-profiled (post my refresh+active-expire fixes 2475618ee/092bd0453): residual clock_gettime is 2.57% (was 6.87% before the active-expire fast-exit removed the per-command cycle's 2 clock reads). The remaining 2 Instant::now reads/command are the command-DURATION timing feeding commandstats/slowlog/latency — redis times every command too (call() elapsedUs). The cost gap is the CLOCK SOURCE: redis getMonotonicUs uses TSC (~5ns register read) when available; std::time::Instant uses clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC) via vdso (~15-30ns). Matching it needs an RDTSC read = UNSAFE, which fr-* crates forbid (#![forbid(unsafe_code)]). No safe-Rust win without a behavior change (can't skip timing: needed to know if a cmd exceeds the 10ms slowlog default). RECOMMEND DEFER as a safe-Rust clock-source limitation. Bigger remaining per-command gap is syscall/network-bound (see new bead).
close_reason: Rejected pass130 latency rounding guard: baseline GET/P16/C50/1M 654.8ms +/-31.0; profile residual vdso 6.87% combined with command duration consumers still visible. Candidate preserved behavior (golden SHA 8587a57563629a5f0674c348760cd25ad54b43ad6b4179b0f05c7cfda49c031a) but paired/reversed hyperfine disagreed: current 658.1ms vs candidate 662.2ms, reversed candidate 641.3ms vs current 677.3ms. No source kept. Route deeper to command-duration clock primitive or next profiled writer/pubsub/output/store surfaces.

title: [HIGH] OBJECT REFCOUNT missing-key nil-vs-error + KEYS/EXPIRETIME drift

Fix in fr-command OBJECT REFCOUNT + fr-store KEYS iteration order or live_oracle_frames_match canonicalization.

title: perf: SCAN full-iteration O(N^2/batch) -> O(N) via resume cache on the ordered keyspace — 32x
description: store.scan() resumed each call with ordered_keys.iter().skip(cursor) = O(cursor), so a full SCAN to completion was O(N^2/batch). Added a keyspace_generation counter (bumped at the 3 centralized ordered_keys insert/remove/clear sites) + a scan_cache (next_cursor,last_key,generation). On an in-order sequential resume with unchanged generation, scan jumps via range((Excluded(last),Unbounded)) in O(log N + batch); any cursor/generation mismatch (out-of-order, concurrent scans, intervening structural mutation) falls back to the skip walk = identical output. Full scan now O(N). 32.64x on a 20k-key full scan @ COUNT 10 (380ms->11.6ms), scales with N. Byte-identical: cache-on full-scan sequence == cache-off (old) over 120 random keyspaces x COUNT/pattern + a mutation-mid-scan script, golden 0x1c26adeb28a10c87; 579 fr-store + 11 fr-command scan tests pass. Graveyard primitive: ordered-iterator resume cursor (vs O(start) re-walk).
close_reason: Shipped 127199b7d: SCAN resume cache (keyspace_generation + scan_cache) jumps via range((Excluded(last),Unbounded)) O(log N+batch) on in-order gen-matched resume, falls back to skip walk otherwise = identical output. Full scan O(N^2/batch)->O(N), 32.64x at 20k@COUNT10. Byte-identical cache-on==cache-off over 120 random + mutation script, golden 0x1c26adeb28a10c87; 579 fr-store + 11 fr-command scan tests pass.

title: s5su part b: upstream-compatible listpack decoder primitive (fr-persist::listpack)
description:
Implements a standalone listpack decoder used by s5su-c/d + v8s5-c. Accepts upstream listpack byte sequences and returns Vec<Vec<u8>> entries.

Scope:
  * Listpack header: total-bytes (u32 LE) + num-elements (u16 LE).
  * Per-entry encoding: 7-bit uint / 6-bit str / 12-bit str / 13-bit int / 16-bit int / 24-bit int / 32-bit int / 64-bit int / 32-bit str + back-ref byte.
  * End-of-listpack 0xFF terminator check.
  * Unit tests with hand-crafted listpack bytes covering every encoding branch.
close_reason: Landed in 270414d: pub mod fr_persist::listpack with forward-iterating decoder over every upstream encoding branch (7-bit uint, 6/12/32-bit str, 13/16/24/32/64-bit signed int) + backlen skip + terminator validation. 12 unit tests covering happy paths and malformed-input rejection. Consumed by s5su-c (hjub), s5su-d (qi6z), s5su-e (6zk9), v8s5-c (hycu). No encoder yet — that lands in 6zk9.

title: [perf] fr-persist RDB bucketed DB emit planner
description: Profile-backed follow-up to rejected frankenredis-hje76. Existing RDB multidb harness profile (artifacts/optimization/icywolf-perf-20260605-pass49-rdb-streaming-crc/baseline-perf-report.txt) shows encode_rdb_internal 44.67% and global stable sort over &RdbEntry 18.63% on --dbs 4096 --entries-per-db 2 --iters 500. The CRC streaming micro-lever was rejected; next deeper primitive is an emit planner: bucket entries by db, sort only keys inside each db, and emit dbs in ascending order. One lever only in crates/fr-persist/src/lib.rs; do not touch peer dirty rdb_stream.rs. Proof requirements: rch build/baseline via fr-persist-rdb-multidb harness before edits, byte-identical RDB golden sha256, ordering/tie-breaking preserved as exact (db,key) order, FP/RNG untouched, rch fr-persist RDB tests/clippy/fmt, paired hyperfine Score>=2.0 before keep.
close_reason: Rejected: bucketed DB emit planner preserved RDB bytes but failed the Score>=2.0 keep gate. Baseline RCH-built multidb harness standalone 171.8 ms +/-5.3 ms, golden SHA 9c0b4109f8ece11500fd11e9d559c1c70cade75ca87a60ff78b890e9fc627e95. Per-DB Vec bucket candidate paired baseline 178.2 ms +/-8.0 ms vs candidate 295.6 ms +/-11.2 ms (baseline 1.66x faster). Range/scatter bucket candidate paired baseline 178.3 ms +/-5.6 ms vs candidate 288.6 ms +/-19.5 ms (baseline 1.62x faster). Candidate goldens remained byte-identical, ordering/tie-breaking preserved, FP/RNG/stream payload semantics untouched. Production lib.rs hunk removed; fmt check passed. Next deeper primitive: RDB size preplanning/exact capacity reservation plus compact length-string emission targeting rdb_encode_length/rdb_encode_string/memmove, not DB bucketing.

title: Multi-node cluster sharding with gossip protocol
description:
CLUSTER commands exist for single-node mode but no cross-node communication. Major architectural work.

**What exists:**
- CLUSTER KEYSLOT/ADDSLOTS/DELSLOTS/INFO/NODES/SLOTS etc work
- Slot ownership tracking per local node
- CRC16 hash slot calculation

**What's missing:**
- No node registry (peers field)
- CLUSTER MEET validates args but never connects
- No cluster bus gossip protocol
- No MOVED/ASK redirect generation at dispatch time
- No slot migration state machine
- No failure detection / auto-promotion

**This is a major multi-phase project, not a single bead. Deferring to roadmap.**
notes: Large feature epic - out of scope for profiling pass. Requires: slot assignment, CLUSTER commands, gossip protocol, cross-node redirects, migration.
