title: Lua stdlib builtins silently accept no-arg/nil-arg calls instead of raising luaL_checkX errors
description:
Systematic divergence: roughly 27 builtins in fr's Lua interpreter use 'args.first().and_then(...).unwrap_or(default)' instead of the upstream luaL_checknumber/luaL_checkstring/luaL_checktable contract. Calling them with zero args returns a silent default value (0, 1, empty string, etc.) where vendored Redis 7.2.4 raises:
  ERR user_script:1: bad argument #N to '<fname>' (<type> expected, got no value)

Affected (verified live vs vendored on port 16380):

math.* (unary number):
  abs, ceil, floor, sqrt, exp, log, log10, sin, cos, tan, asin, acos, atan, sinh, cosh, tanh, deg, rad, modf, frexp
math.* (binary number, arg-#2 missing):
  fmod, pow, atan2, ldexp
string.* (arg-#1 string missing):
  find, match, sub, rep, byte, lower, upper, reverse, len, gmatch
table.* (arg-#1 table missing):
  sort, maxn
base:
  tonumber

NOT affected (already validated correctly): math.max, math.min (frankenredis-n4eln), assert, xpcall, rawget, setmetatable, getmetatable (frankenredis-nf29w), ipairs, pairs, next, unpack, type, tostring, pcall, select.

Each handler currently looks like e.g. lua_eval.rs:4769:
  "math.floor" => {
      let n = args.first().and_then(|v| v.to_number()).unwrap_or(0.0);
      Ok(vec![LuaValue::Number(n.floor())])
  }

Fix sketch:
  - Add small helpers near the existing format_builtin_argerror:
      lua_check_number(&[LuaValue], idx, fname) -> Result<f64, String>
      lua_check_string(&[LuaValue], idx, fname) -> Result<Vec<u8>, String>
      lua_check_table(&[LuaValue], idx, fname) -> Result<&LuaTable, String>
    Each returns 'user_script:1: bad argument #N to <fname> (<type> expected, got no value | got <type>)'.
  - Replace the 27 unwrap_or default patterns with the helper.
  - For binary functions where only arg-#2 is missing (math.fmod/pow/atan2/ldexp), the helper still emits #2 correctly.
  - %d/%s arg checks in string.format are already done (frankenredis-be7o1) and use a different pattern.

No crashes — these are wording-only divergences (verified ~20 functions on the live server, no panics). But still worth fixing for full vendored parity, especially since this is the same family as the recently-closed nf29w bead.

Test plan:
  - One pinning test per affected function that asserts the exact upstream error string for the missing-arg case.
  - Happy-path regression: each function still returns the right value when called correctly.
  - Type-mismatch test for a couple of representatives: math.floor(true), string.len({}), table.sort('hi') — must error with 'got <type>' wording.
  - No-panic: server must stay alive after each error case.
close_reason:
Added 3 helpers (lua_check_number / lua_check_string / lua_check_table) that mirror upstream luaL_checkX wording: 'user_script:1: bad argument #N to <fname> (<type> expected, got no value | got <type>)'. Replaced the silent unwrap_or(default) patterns in ~27 stdlib builtins:

math.* (unary number, 20 fns): abs, ceil, floor, sqrt, exp, log, log10, sin, cos, tan, asin, acos, atan, sinh, cosh, tanh, deg, rad, modf, frexp.

math.* (binary number, 4 fns): fmod, pow, atan2, ldexp. These each match a vendored quirk: the C implementation is 'lua_pushnumber(L, op(luaL_checknumber(L,1), luaL_checknumber(L,2)));' and gcc's right-to-left ABI evaluates the inner luaL_checknumber on arg #2 first, so when both args are missing or wrong, vendored reports arg #2. fr now matches by ordering the lua_check_number calls accordingly.

string.* (arg-#1 string, 10 fns): find, match, sub, rep, byte, lower, upper, reverse, len, gmatch — plus sub/rep validation for arg #2 (number expected) and find/match/gmatch/gsub for arg #2 (string expected).

table.* (arg-#1 table, 2 fns): sort, maxn.

base (1 fn): tonumber — 'value expected' instead of silently returning nil.

Live diff vs vendored 7.2.4 (port 16380): 36/36 probe cases match (was 1 divergence before the binary-fn arg-#2-first quirk was matched). Server stays alive after every error case.

Pinned by lua_stdlib_builtins_reject_missing_args_match_upstream (39 missing-arg cases + 1 wrong-type sample + 6 happy-path regressions + post-error liveness check). All 937 fr-command lib tests pass.

title: HSETNX should bump LFU access frequency for existing hashes
description: Upstream Redis 7.2.4 hsetnxCommand calls hashTypeLookupWriteOrCreate, which starts with lookupKeyWrite on the hash key. lookupKeyWrite reaches lookupKey without LOOKUP_NOTOUCH, so existing hash keys bump LFU counters under LFU maxmemory policies even when HSETNX finds the field already present and returns 0. FrankenRedis Store::hsetnx currently mutates or checks the hash after ensuring the entry but does not bump LFU frequency for existing hash keys. Add LFU bumping and focused fr-store coverage for both the no-op existing-field path and the inserted-field path on an existing hash.
close_reason: done

title: Lua string.format precision on %d/%i/%u/%x/%X/%o ignored
description:
C printf treats the precision specifier for integer conversions as the
minimum digit count (zero-padded), independent of the width. When set,
the 0-flag is suppressed and spaces fill the remaining width. Probed:

  EVAL "return string.format('%10.5d', 42)" 0
    vendored:      00042   (5-digit zero-padded "00042", right-aligned in width 10)
    fr (was):        42    (width 10 with right-pad, ignoring precision)

Fix locus: crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs — 'd'/'i' and 'u' arms of
the string.format implementation.

Test plan: pin %10.5d, %5.3d, %.5x, %.0d for 0 (vendored emits empty
string for %.0d with 0), %05.3d (precision suppresses 0-flag), and
the existing %5d / %d-without-precision regressions.
close_reason: string.format's %d/%i/%u/%x/%X/%o arms in crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs now honor the precision specifier as a minimum-digit-count zero-pad (C printf integer-conversion semantics). When precision is set, the width's 0-flag is suppressed and spaces fill the rest. When precision is 0 AND the value is 0, no digits are emitted (vendored: '%.0d', 0 → empty string). The # alt-form prefix (0x/0X for %x/%X; leading 0 for %o) sits outside the precision pad and is suppressed when value is 0 for %x/%X. Regression test lua_string_format_integer_precision_3pug0 pins 17 cases including the zero-precision/zero-value short-circuit and -42 padding. Live diff vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 on :16380 clean across 18 probes.

title: perf(persist): CRC64 slice-by-8 (8 bytes/iter, 8x256 table) for DUMP/RESTORE/RDB
description: fr-persist::crc64_redis (lib.rs:1008) is byte-at-a-time: crc=(crc>>8)^TABLE[(crc as u8)^byte] per byte. Redis crc64.c uses crcspeed slice-by-8 (8 tables of 256 entries, 8 bytes/iteration). LEVER: build 8x256 reflected-CRC tables (const fn), process 8 bytes/iter folding via the 8 tables, byte-wise remainder tail. Reflected/little-endian word load. ISOMORPHISM: produces identical CRC64 (Jones poly, reflected) — golden vector test + equivalence vs byte-wise over random buffers. Target >=4x on DUMP/RESTORE/RDB checksum over MB-scale payloads. BLOCKED: fr-persist/src/lib.rs currently git-dirty (another agent's LZF/wmh2p WIP) — do when clean. Clean safe-Rust kernel, no deps.
close_reason: Shipped f89e16c1e: CRC64 slice-by-8, 4.25x (32MiB), 166/166 fr-persist tests, equivalence + Redis reference vector proven. Selective-staged to preserve concurrent LZF WIP.

title: fr-command: XCLAIM/XAUTOCLAIM/XPENDING/XACK parse args before key/group lookup, leak ERR on missing-group instead of NOGROUP
description:
Upstream Redis 7.2 t_stream.c::xclaimCommand looks up the stream key + consumer group BEFORE parsing min-idle-time and trailing options (lookupKeyRead → checkType → streamLookupCG → NOGROUP, all before the first getLongLongFromObjectOrReply call). fr-command's xclaim parses min-idle-time and trailing options first, then dispatches into store.xclaim which finally returns the NOGROUP error.

The user-visible consequence is a wrong error class for misconfigured callers:

  Setup: stream 'xs2' exists but has no consumer group 'g'.
  XCLAIM xs2 g c BAD 0-0:
    upstream: -NOGROUP No such key 'xs2' or consumer group 'g'
    fr      : -ERR Invalid min-idle-time argument for XCLAIM
  XCLAIM xs2 g c 0 0-0 IDLE BAD:
    upstream: -NOGROUP ...
    fr      : -ERR Invalid IDLE option argument for XCLAIM
  XCLAIM xs2 g c 0 0-0 NOSUCH:
    upstream: -NOGROUP ...
    fr      : -ERR Unrecognized XCLAIM option 'NOSUCH'

Wording-only fixes from frankenredis-rsls already apply when the group exists; this bead covers the orthogonal ordering bug.

Suggested fix: at the start of xclaim() (and xautoclaim/xpending/xack which are likely to share the same shape), look up key+group via store.stream_consumer_groups (or equivalent) and short-circuit with xclaim_nogroup_error if missing — before any arg parsing. Acceptance: differential probe of all four commands with a missing group reports NOGROUP regardless of trailing arg shape.

Reference: ad-hoc differential probe of fr (port 16700) vs vendored redis 7.2.4 (port 16701) on 2026-05-03 while closing frankenredis-rsls.
notes:
XCLAIM now does key+group lookup BEFORE parsing min-idle-time and trailing options, matching upstream xclaimCommand at t_stream.c:3119-3140 (which is the only XCLAIM-family command upstream that orders this way; XPENDING and XAUTOCLAIM parse args first per upstream itself, and XACK already short-circuits to 0 in fr). Missing key/group surfaces NOGROUP regardless of arg shape; non-stream key surfaces WRONGTYPE.

Test xclaim_missing_group_short_circuits_before_arg_parsing pins NOGROUP for missing-group + bad min-idle-time, missing-group + bad IDLE arg, missing-group + unknown trailing option, missing-key + bad min-idle-time, and WRONGTYPE for non-stream key.

title: [task] LOLWUT: fr emits version-only (no generative art) — confirm WONTFIX or add art
description: fr LOLWUT VERSION 5 returns just 'Redis ver. 7.2.4' with no ASCII/braille art; redis returns generative art + version. Art is random per-call (can't byte-match) so prior sessions marked WONTFIX. Low priority; documented here so it isn't re-probed as a bug.
close_reason: WONTFIX (verified vs vendored redis 7.2.4). fr's DEFAULT LOLWUT is byte-exact with redis: both return version-only ('Redis ver. 7.2.4', 1 line, deterministic across runs). Divergence exists ONLY for explicit LOLWUT VERSION 5 (Georg-Nees Schotter Braille canvas) and VERSION 6 (ANSI color-block grid), and that art is NON-DETERMINISTIC server-side (random rotation/shading) so it cannot be differential/parity-tested — it is correctly excluded from the probe harnesses. Arg parsing + error wording (e.g. 'value is not an integer or out of range' for bad VERSION) already byte-exact. Adding a random art generator would be pure cosmetics that move no parity metric and add non-deterministic surface to a parity-focused port. Aligns with [[project_scripting_and_lolwut_status]] (LOLWUT art random per-call = WONTFIX).

title: [perf] Large SET reusable read slab
description: Hotspot evidence: large SET remains below Redis while GET is faster; narrow network strace on 5000x SET 262144B C1/P16 showed FrankenRedis doing 30618 recvfrom calls for 1.31GB, while Redis completed the same shape with 5259 reads. Rejected families: direct chunk-size 256/512KiB, std read_to_end, and drain-to-WouldBlock. Candidate primitive: add a per-connection reusable initialized large-read slab (target 256KiB) for the in-flight owned SET continuation, read socket bytes into that slab, then append to the pre-capacity value Vec. This is 100% safe Rust and avoids per-command destination zero-fill while reducing recvfrom count. Preserve RESP frame boundary, trailer validation, query-buffer accounting, command ordering, propagation/expiry/dirty semantics, output replies, and fallback behavior. Baseline via release-perf/fr-bench first; keep only if same-binary candidate-vs-baseline focused SET and golden raw RESP sha256 pass Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Rejected measured large SET reusable read slab: behavior golden matched Redis/baseline (response sha ee784c22fa4ad7cd4432d1ee0e83c41a2e30b7e148a1e5e06b702c7af25d505a), but paired 256KiB SET regressed with baseline 1.04x +/- 0.05 faster; 1MiB candidate win was high-variance and total Score < 2.0. Source hunk removed; evidence in artifacts/optimization/coralox-pass212-3qt3h/.

title: LRANGE deep start O(start) element-skip instead of O(start/chunk) node-seek
description: LRANGE uses l.iter().skip(start).take(count) (lib.rs lrange), which walks  ELEMENTS to reach the range start. Redis's quicklist seeks at the NODE level (quicklistIndex walks ~start/128 nodes, from the nearest end), so it's O(start/128 + count). fr's ChunkedList stores ~128-element chunks and can locate the start chunk by walking chunks, but LRANGE doesn't use that path. Differential: LRANGE biglist 150000 150010 on a 300k list = oracle 34.5us vs fr 351us (10.2x); tail ranges (LRANGE k -10 -1) are even worse (O(len) skip). Fix: ChunkedList::iter_from(start) that locates the start chunk from the nearest end (O(min(start,len-start)/128)) then iterates; ListValue::iter_from + PackedList::iter_from; LRANGE uses iter_from(s).take(count). Byte-identical (same elements, same order). LINDEX already O(1)-chunk via get(); LPOS/streams/geo at parity.
close_reason: Fixed: LRANGE seeks to the range start at the chunk level via ChunkedList::iter_from (nearest-end, O(min(start,len-start)/128 + count)) instead of iter().skip(start)=O(start). Byte-identical. Deep-middle LRANGE 150000 150010 (300k list): 378us->33us (11.4x); tail LRANGE -10 -1: 700us->32us (22x); both parity with redis. 11 ranges + list_differ byte-exact; golden e943e628.

title: Replica MONITOR does not echo the master's SELECT commands (data is correct, only the MONITOR feed omits SELECT) — redis shows SELECT in a replica's MONITOR; needs fr-runtime/fr-server replica-apply MONITOR feed

Root cause: the special-command dispatch early-returns before feed_monitors, so SELECT/SWAPDB/etc. were never fed; redis's call() feeds MONITOR for all non-CMD_ADMIN/CMD_SKIP_MONITOR commands. Added that feed to the special path, scoped to applying_master_stream (replica replay) so only replicable monitorable commands (SELECT/writes/MULTI/EXEC/PUBLISH) are fed and admin REPLCONF is filtered; normal-client behaviour unchanged. Verified vs redis 7.2.4: fr replica MONITOR shows '[n] SELECT n' before each db change. fr-runtime suite green + new test replica_monitor_echoes_select_from_master_stream_3s0ra. NOTE: landed under OrangeMouse's commit message via shared-index race (my staged change swept into their git commit); fix is correct + on origin/main. Residual: MONITOR peer addr still 127.0.0.1:0 vs primary addr — separate pre-existing feed_monitors limitation.

title: XGROUP CREATECONSUMER emits key-required error for empty MKSTREAM-created stream
description:
XGROUP CREATECONSUMER probes store.xlen() to decide whether the stream exists. Since xlen returns Ok(0) for both missing keys and existing-but-empty streams, an empty stream created via 'XGROUP CREATE k g 0 MKSTREAM' is mis-classified as missing.

Repro:
  XGROUP CREATE nope g 0 MKSTREAM   -> OK on both
  EXISTS nope                       -> 1 on both
  XGROUP CREATECONSUMER nope g c1   -> vendored: 1; fr: 'ERR The XGROUP subcommand requires the key to exist...'

The fix is identical to the DELCONSUMER fix at fr-command/src/lib.rs:7644 (bead n4g5, supersedes qcmh): swap the xlen probe for store.key_type() and check for 'stream'.

Acceptance: XGROUP CREATECONSUMER on an empty (MKSTREAM-created) stream returns 1 (consumer created) matching vendored Redis 7.2.4; regression test pins the behavior.

Repro discovered iter 136 probe sweep.
close_reason: completed

title: Lua pattern %f frontier matcher not implemented
description:
Lua 5.1's pattern engine supports the frontier pattern %f[set] which matches the empty string at a boundary where the previous character does NOT match set and the next character DOES match set. fr's pattern engine returns nil for any pattern containing %f.

Reproduction:
  EVAL "return string.find('THE (QUICK)', '%f[%a]')" 0
    vendored: 1
    fr:       (nil)

  EVAL "return string.gsub('THE QUICK BROWN', '%f[%a]', '|')" 0
    vendored: |THE |QUICK |BROWN
    fr:       (unchanged 'THE QUICK BROWN')

Upstream implementation (lstrlib.c::do_match):
  - On encountering %f, the parser advances to the [class] following it.
  - At each position p, frontier matches the empty string iff
    !match(set, p-1) AND match(set, p).
  - The character before position 0 is treated as \0 (NUL).

This is a feature gap — most Redis Lua scripts don't use %f, but
parity-conscious users do.

Test plan:
  - Pin EVAL "return string.find('THE (QUICK)', '%f[%a]')" returns 1.
  - Pin EVAL "return string.gsub('a b c', '%f[%a]', '!')" returns "!a !b !c".
  - Pin malformed %f (e.g. %f without []) errors.
close_reason: Implemented Lua %f[set] frontier matcher in crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs. lua_pattern_validate now requires '[' immediately after %f and raises "missing '[' after '%f' in pattern" otherwise. lua_pat_match has a new zero-width branch that mirrors lstrlib.c::do_match: previous byte (or NUL at the string start) must NOT match the set and the current byte (or NUL past end) MUST match. Works with find/match/gmatch/gsub and at anchored ^ positions. Regression test lua_pattern_frontier_matcher_3zxc1 pins 9 cases. Live diff vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 on :16380 clean for all 12 probed inputs (including malformed %f wording vs the existing '[abc' and trailing-% errors).

title: MEMORY USAGE diverges from vendored due to SDS overhead and allocation-size mismatches
description: Differential probe with sample strings of various lengths shows fr's MEMORY USAGE diverges from vendored Redis 7.2.4 by 8-40 bytes depending on length: len=2 embstr +8, len=30 embstr +16, len=40 embstr +8, len=50 raw +16, len=100 raw +8, len=256 raw -40, len=1000 raw -8. Three coupled bugs in crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:12668-12879: (1) REDIS_SDS_ALLOC_OVERHEAD_BYTES is hardcoded to 7; real value is length-dependent: sdshdr5 (len<32) overhead=2, sdshdr8 (<256)=4, sdshdr16 (<65536)=6, sdshdr32 (<4GB)=10, sdshdr64=18. (2) REDIS_EMBSTR_ALLOC_OVERHEAD_BYTES=19 should be 20 per upstream object.c::createEmbeddedStringObject which calls zmalloc(sizeof(robj)+sizeof(sdshdr8)+len+1) = 16+3+1+len. (3) redis_allocation_size() has size classes 8/16/24/32/48/64/80/96/112/128/then-mod-16, but real jemalloc lacks the 24-byte class between 16 and 32 and uses group/quanta-based doubling above 128 (192/256/320/384/512/640/768/896/1024...). Source for upstream behavior: legacy_redis_code/redis/src/object.c::objectComputeSize, sds.c::_sdsnewlen, sds.h sdshdr5/8/16/32/64 structs. Fix touches Store::memory_usage_for_key path plus the constants. Risk: changing affects MEMORY USAGE replies and INFO memory's used_memory_dataset estimate.
close_reason: MEMORY USAGE now byte-equivalent with vendored Redis 7.2.4 across string (16 lengths), list/listpack, hash/listpack, set/listpack, and sortedset/listpack cases. Fixed: (1) SDS header overhead is now length-dependent (sdshdr5/8/16/32/64 → 2/4/6/10/18 bytes) instead of flat 7; (2) embstr alloc overhead 19→20 (accounting for trailing null); (3) jemalloc 5.x size-class table added 40/56 entries and switched post-128 doubling-quanta groups (160,192,224,256→320,384,448,512→…) replacing /16 rounding; (4) listpack entry size now adds +2/+4/+10 for encoding + back-pointer (was just bytes.len()). Stream memory (estimate_stream_memory_usage_bytes) still diverges 8 bytes per entry — vendored is static at 4728 across 1-3 entries due to listpack preallocation; fr grows linearly. Filed follow-up bead for stream-specific accounting.

Multi-line scripts emit 'user_script:1:' even when the error is on line 4 — partial parity with upstream's per-line prefix. Existing trailing 'on @user_script:1.' suffix at fr-command/src/lib.rs:13770/13777/13811 is similarly hardcoded. Both hardcoded sites are consistent with each other (both '1') so the script-context message is internally coherent; only line-number divergence from upstream. After 5 sequential REVIEW MODE defects shipped this session (oafun / sjuu1 / ztawj / gdbca / u1xg5), the surface I've covered (fr-protocol, fr-command's lua_eval) is now well-defended. title: Add redis-cli compatibility smoke test script
description:
# redis-cli Compatibility Smoke Test

## What
A shell script that starts FrankenRedis, runs redis-cli commands against it, and verifies outputs. This is the ultimate "drop-in replacement" proof.

## Why
If redis-cli cannot talk to FrankenRedis, it is not a drop-in replacement regardless of what internal tests say.

## Implementation
1. scripts/redis_cli_smoke_test.sh
2. Start frankenredis on a random port
3. Run redis-cli commands:
   - PING → PONG
   - SET foo bar → OK
   - GET foo → bar
   - INFO server → contains "redis_version"
   - DBSIZE → (integer) 1
   - CONFIG GET maxmemory → valid response
   - MULTI/EXEC transaction
   - EVAL "return redis.call(\"GET\",KEYS[1])" 1 foo → bar
4. Check exit code of each command
5. Clean up

## Depends On
- redis-cli must be installed (check and skip if not available)

## Files
- scripts/redis_cli_smoke_test.sh (new)
close_reason: Added scripts/redis_cli_smoke_test.sh: 25 tests covering PING, SET/GET, APPEND, EXISTS, DEL, INCR/INCRBY, RPUSH/LLEN/LPOP, HSET/HGET, SADD/SISMEMBER/SCARD, INFO, DBSIZE, EVAL, CONFIG GET. All 25/25 pass using legacy redis-cli against FrankenRedis.

title: [perf] Skip expiry lookup when no key expiries exist
description: Profile-backed child under frankenredis-gu5nf. Post-pass10/current HGET pipeline=16 GDB samples at artifacts/optimization/icywolf-perf-20260603-pass10-post-waitguard show Store::drop_if_expired and record_keyspace_lookup on ordinary HGET. Current drop_if_expired hashes into entries to read expires_at_ms for every key lookup even when expires_count == 0, the default benchmark/common no-expiry state. One lever: return immediately from drop_if_expired when expires_count is zero, preserving all behavior when any key expiry is tracked. This is distinct from rejected frankenredis-gu5nf.4 HGET fast path; it only removes the no-expiry guard hash lookup globally. Preserve lazy expiration ordering once expires_count > 0, hit/miss accounting, dirty/expired counters, keyspace notifications, no FP/RNG surface. Baseline/rebench via rch release-perf fr-server/fr-bench HGET p16, golden RESP sha256 covering no-expiry HGET and expired-key lookup, crate-scoped fmt/check/test/clippy/UBS; keep only Score >= 2.0.
close_reason: Rejected: rch-built release-perf HGET p16 no-expiry guard preserved golden sha256 12a63b3f05a7a2c7b93261729b626eb8d4b5a11e405abece3684e80147533af2 but did not beat baseline; baseline direct 333064.18 ops/sec, candidate direct 328081.50 ops/sec; paired hyperfine baseline 3.027s +/-0.044, candidate 3.028s +/-0.068, baseline 1.00 +/-0.03 faster. Score below 2.0, no code kept.

title: DUMP of integer sets 2.3x slower: i64 to bytes to i64 round-trip per member
description: PROFILE-BACKED pipelined DUMP perf record: DUMP of an intset-encoded set was 2.28x slower than redis 7.2.4, ~40 percent in i64 to_string. ROOT CAUSE: dump_key Value::Set arm built integer_members via s.iter map parse_i64, but SetValueIter::Int formats each stored i64 through set_int_to_bytes alloc+to_string only for parse_i64 to re-parse it. FIX: SetValue::as_int_slice returns raw sorted i64s for the Int encoding; dump_key takes them directly. ISOMORPHIC golden sha256 byte-identical; 634 fr-store tests pass; DUMP/RESTORE round-trip ok. BENCH pipelined P16: DUMP intset 2.28x to 1.30x = 1.76x speedup, is_mid 1.88x. Residual = encode_intset + byte-exact lzf.
close_reason: Fixed in 10d1f82eb: SetValue::as_int_slice lets DUMP consume intset i64s directly. Pipelined DUMP intset 2.28x->1.30x (1.76x speedup), is_mid 1.88x; byte-exact (golden sha256), 634 fr-store tests pass, DUMP/RESTORE round-trip ok.

title: string.format %d with out-of-range f64 returns INT64_MAX (vendored: INT64_MIN per x86 UB)
description:
Upstream Lua 5.1.5 lstrlib.c::str_format casts arg through
luaL_checknumber -> (long)x. For finite f64 values that exceed
LLONG_MAX (e.g. 1e20, 1e100, 1e300), the C cast is undefined
behavior; on x86_64 it typically yields LLONG_MIN
(-9223372036854775808). Rust's `f64 as i64` saturates to i64::MAX
(9223372036854775807).

Repro:
  EVAL "return string.format('%d', 1e20)" 0
    vendored: -9223372036854775808
    fr:       9223372036854775807

  Values that stay within range (1e15, 1e16, 9.2e18) match.
  Boundary near i64::MAX:
    1e15 → 1000000000000000 (both)
    1e16 → 10000000000000000 (both)
    9223372036854775000 → 9223372036854774784 (both, double precision loss)
    1e20 → divergence starts

This is a portability-sensitive divergence. Faithful x86_64 parity
needs an explicit out-of-range guard that maps positive-overflow
to i64::MIN. Alternatively, vendored could be argued to be the
buggy one (UB), but for differential parity fr needs to mirror.

Low priority — scripts that rely on this UB-derived overflow are
rare.
close_reason: completed

title: perf(fr-command): pooled/persistent LuaState to close the ~1.84x trivial-EVAL gap (env rebuilt per call)
description: Follow-up to fmilu (env-table-sharing measured only 1.13x — see artifacts/optimization/frankenredis-fmilu/RESULT.md). The trivial-EVAL gap vs redis (~1.84x) is distributed across many small per-call costs: 25 builtin globals + per-call redis table + per-call _G snapshot (install_g_table copies ALL globals) + parse + LuaState init + GC scope + RESP conversion + propagation-state reset. No single 2x lever exists at the table level. Redis is fast because it keeps a PERSISTENT lua_State reused across EVALs; fr rebuilds LuaState (incl. the whole env) every call. REAL lever: pool/persist the LuaState env (globals+tables built once per thread) and rebind store + reset KEYS/ARGV/_G/globals-lock per execution, instead of rebuilding. Target ~1.6x (close 81us->50us per-call). Foundation patch (stdlib-template-share, byte-exact, +new_unregistered + clear_table_recursive readonly-skip) saved at artifacts/optimization/frankenredis-fmilu/stdlib-template-share.patch. Hard part: LuaState holds &mut Store (per-call borrow) — must decouple env lifetime from store binding. crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs.
close_reason: Kept compiled Lua chunk cache: trivial EVAL 20k paired 1.497035182s -> 1.412030834s (1.0602x), reversed 1.599366482s -> 1.479810248s (1.0808x), 50k confirm 3.812962695s -> 3.516328143s (1.0844x), table200 0.677090341s -> 0.626395128s (1.0809x), loop1000 neutral 0.987614812s -> 0.971889700s (1.0162x); golden sha256 522a3ab10859dcc45592ec5a323f1bc0f40b81db1662124aacb7a6d3e7bed005; proof artifacts/optimization/frankenredis-45ywg/pass145/PROOF.md

title: DEBUG accepts commands by default; vendored requires enable-debug-command=local
description: On a fresh fr-server (port 16480 in our probe environment), DEBUG subcommands like SLEEP, OBJECT, SDSLEN succeed without enable-debug-command being set. Vendored Redis 7.2.4 rejects them with 'ERR DEBUG command not allowed. If the enable-debug-command option is set to local, you can run it from a local connection, otherwise you need to set this option in the configuration file, and then restart the server.' This means fr's STANDALONE server runs with enable-debug-command effectively enabled by default whereas vendored defaults to no. The fr CONFORMANCE harness DOES default-disable DEBUG correctly (configure_runtime_for_fixture in crates/fr-conformance/src/lib.rs explicitly enables it for DEBUG-using fixtures). The divergence is in the standalone fr-server launch path -- likely crates/fr-server/src/main.rs or how the runtime is initialized when launched as a binary. Fix: ensure fr-server's default Runtime config has enable-debug-command=no and only flips to yes when the CLI flag/config opts in (matching upstream). Reproduce: redis-cli -p 16480 DEBUG SLEEP 0 (succeeds with OK on fr; vendored rejects with the long ERR). Surfaced during REVIEW PHASE differential-probe sweep.
close_reason: Not a bug. Verified the running fr-server on port 16480 was launched with --enable-debug-command=yes (probably by a previous agent for testing); fr-server's actual default IS no, matching upstream Redis 7.2 (see crates/fr-server/src/main.rs:251 '--enable-debug-command <VALUE>  Allow DEBUG commands: no | local | yes (default: no, matches upstream Redis 7.2)'). CONFIG GET enable-debug-command on port 16480 returns 'yes', confirming the live server was opted-in at startup. No source code change required.

title: [perf] qbs5q follow-up: emit retained quicklist2 listpack nodes on DUMP
description: Successor after pass202 closed the first packed-node RESTORE lever. Current qbs5q pass202 keeps string-only QUICKLIST_2 listpack nodes as retained byte buffers plus ranges and improves the 10k-list RESTORE harness 1.59x forward / 1.53x reversed with identical DUMP sha256 308fb090d09d0b431758a85fc724cfd627d5203c8cf23835f004e78a783efad8. Shifted remaining gap: Store::dump_key / encode_dump_quicklist2 still iterates entries and re-encodes listpack nodes instead of emitting retained canonical node bytes when the list is still unmodified. Profile target: post-pass202 DUMP vs Redis and/or harness DUMP lane. Candidate families: ListValue node iterator exposing retained string-listpack nodes; canonical retained bytes only when node boundaries and list-max-listpack-size match current DUMP rules; fallback to old re-encoder for mutated/materialized, integer, plain, or non-canonical nodes. Acceptance: one safe-Rust lever, same-worker baseline/candidate via rch, DUMP/LRANGE/RESTORE golden sha256, ordering/tie/fp/RNG proof, crate-scoped check/clippy/tests, Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Completed: raw-emits retained quicklist2 listpack nodes on DUMP when retained chunks exactly match current greedy node-boundary rules; restored 10k-list DUMP golden unchanged sha256 308fb090d09d0b431758a85fc724cfd627d5203c8cf23835f004e78a783efad8; hyperfine 2.990s->2.561s forward and 3.035s->2.588s reversed, 1.17x faster; Score 2.47

title: DEBUG PROTOCOL <type> not implemented; upstream emits canned RESP frames per type
description:
fr's DEBUG handler doesn't recognize the PROTOCOL subcommand at all — every form returns 'ERR unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments for PROTOCOL. Try DEBUG HELP.' Upstream Redis 7.2.4 supports DEBUG PROTOCOL <type> for testing client RESP parsers across all 14 type names.

Source: legacy_redis_code/redis/src/debug.c::debugCommand lines 772-832.

Types and their canonical RESP2 replies:
- string → BulkString 'Hello World'
- integer → Integer 12345
- double → BulkString '3.141' (RESP2; Double in RESP3)
- bignum → BulkString '1234567999999999999999999999999999999'
- null → NullBulkString
- true → Integer 1 (RESP2; Boolean true in RESP3)
- false → Integer 0 (RESP2; Boolean false in RESP3)
- array → [0, 1, 2]
- set → [0, 1, 2] (Set in RESP3)
- map → [0, false, 1, true, 2, false] (flat array in RESP2; Map in RESP3)
- attrib → BulkString 'Some real reply following the attribute' (RESP3 wraps with attribute frame)
- push → Error 'RESP2 is not supported by this command' (RESP3 emits push frame)
- verbatim → BulkString 'This is a verbatim\nstring' (RESP2; verbatim with txt format in RESP3)
- bad → Error 'Wrong protocol type name. Please use one of the following: string|integer|double|bignum|null|array|set|map|attrib|push|verbatim|true|false'

Implement in fr-command DEBUG handler. Pin the RESP2 forms; RESP3 forms are out-of-scope until the broader RESP3 push-frame work lands.
close_reason: Added PROTOCOL arm to DEBUG dispatcher emitting all 14 type frames (RESP2-shaped) per debug.c::debugCommand:772-832. Diff vs ven shows zero divergence across all 14 type names + bad-type + arity-error. Pin test covers all branches. Commit 6876bf0.

title: Port one more missing Redis parity behavior from legacy oracle

title: Add conformance fixtures validating all stub remediations
description:
# Add conformance fixtures for all stub remediations

## What
After each stub is replaced with a real implementation, add conformance test cases that would have FAILED with the old stub but PASS with the new implementation. This proves the fix works end-to-end.

## Fixtures to add/update

### core_server.json (INFO section)
- INFO stats after SET/GET operations should show non-zero keyspace_hits/misses
- INFO stats after key expiry should show non-zero expired_keys
- INFO clients should show correct blocked_clients count

### core_server.json (LATENCY section)
- CONFIG SET latency-monitor-threshold 1 → run DEBUG SLEEP 0.01 → LATENCY LATEST should return samples
- LATENCY HISTORY command should return entries
- LATENCY RESET should clear and return count

### core_server.json (SLOWLOG via Lua)
- EVAL \"return redis.call('SLOWLOG','LEN')\" should return real count (not 0)

### core_server.json (DEBUG section)
- DEBUG OBJECT <key> should return encoding info string
- DEBUG SET-ACTIVE-EXPIRE 0 should return OK

### core_scripting.json (Lua CLIENT)
- redis.call(\"CLIENT\", \"ID\") should return integer > 0 (not always 1)

### core_copy.json or new fixture
- ACL SAVE + ACL LOAD round-trip test

### core_stream.json
- XADD + XDEL + XINFO STREAM should show correct max-deleted-entry-id

## Depends on
- All implementation beads (this is the verification layer)

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-conformance/fixtures/core_server.json
- crates/fr-conformance/fixtures/core_scripting.json
- crates/fr-conformance/fixtures/core_stream.json
- crates/fr-conformance/tests/smoke.rs (if new fixtures added)
close_reason: Completed

title: Keyspace notification: SMOVE per-key events (srem on src + conditional sadd on dst) need store-level emission
notes: SMOVE keyspace events are state-dependent and the runtime post-hoc dispatcher can't see the handler's add-signal (handler return -> RespFrame Integer; runtime only sees the reply). Exact upstream (verified 7.2.4): SMOVE a b m = srem(a), [del(a) if a empties], sadd(b ONLY IF b did not already contain m). Edge cases: dst-already-has-member -> srem only (no sadd, setTypeAdd returns 0); same-key a a m -> no events (not dirty). The byte-exact fix needs Store::smove to emit its own srem/del/sadd via the queued notify mechanism (it has the add-signal from self.sadd's return + source_empty), with db derived via decode_db_key, and SMOVE excluded from the runtime generic path. List-move family (LMOVE/RPOPLPUSH/BLMOVE/BRPOPLPUSH) was done in the dedicated runtime branch (commit for uw80w) because their push is unconditional. SMOVE currently still fires the wrong 'smove'x2 + trailing del via the generic path (it remains in command_can_empty_collection). P3 cosmetic.
close_reason: Fixed: Store::smove now emits its own srem(src)/del(if src empties)/sadd(dst, only when newly added) keyspace events — it has the add-signal the runtime post-hoc dispatcher lacks. Runtime excludes SMOVE from the generic path + command_can_empty_collection. Byte-exact vs vendored 7.2.4 across all edges: normal (srem,sadd), src-empties (srem,del,sadd), dst-has-member (srem only / srem,del), same-key (none), member-missing (none); per-key event->key mapping verified.

title: rv89 part 4f: AOF propagation roundtrip verification for HEXPIRE/HPEXPIRE/HEXPIREAT/HPEXPIREAT/HPERSIST
description:
is_write_command was extended in br-frankenredis-c0z9 to cover the HEXPIRE family so AOF capture already picks up their argv. Verify end-to-end:
- Issue HEXPIRE / HPERSIST commands on a runtime.
- Inspect the runtime.aof_records buffer → must contain the argv.
- Replay the AOF on a fresh runtime → per-field TTL state reconstructs.
- HGET / HTTL on the replayed runtime returns the pre-shutdown TTL.

Test-only scope; no production change expected unless a replay-side bug surfaces.
close_reason: Landed in 24fc1f3: Store::to_aof_commands emits deterministic-ordered HPEXPIREAT records for every hash field carrying a per-field TTL, so SAVE + replay reconstructs identical state. Uses absolute-ms so reload-time drift cannot shift deadlines. 2 new roundtrip tests + past-deadline regression guard; fr-runtime 10/0, fr-command 626/0, fr-store 280/0.

Apply aclfile from --config, fail startup on invalid ACL files, and freeze behavior with TCP auth/ACL coverage.

title: perf(server): cursor-drain client output buffers
description: Profile-backed pass64 target: GET P16/1M server perf on main b5ab1a76 shows __memmove_avx_unaligned_erms 9.56% flat plus output/copy costs. Implement one lever only: replace front-drain output write_buf flushing with a logical cursor/clear path so partial writes do not memmove pending output. Must prove byte-identical replies and output-buffer accounting parity; keep only if paired rch/hyperfine score >=2.0.
close_reason: Rejected: output-buffer cursor preserved golden SHA 2612d02989f4a06e17bf0f2f06c69dfe9bc475051f1674481e85347a3f44e688 but reversed confirmation failed (300k candidate 422.2 ms +/- 27.7 vs baseline 419.4 ms +/- 9.5; 1M candidate 1.441 s +/- 0.030 vs baseline 1.438 s +/- 0.088). Score 0.6 < 2.0; source hunk removed.

title: perf(runtime): drop per-command ClientSession clone into client_sessions registry
description: After argv alloc->0 (8yfmt), the next per-request heap alloc is execute_dispatch doing client_sessions.insert(client_id, self.session.clone()) every command (fr-runtime:5215,:7180). ClientSession has owned Strings/Vecs mostly immutable per-command. LEVER: save self.session into the registry only on session-swap, overlay at CLIENT LIST/INFO read time -> per-command clone removed. PARITY: CLIENT LIST/INFO/ID/GETNAME must report active client live state. BLOCKED: fr-runtime is IcyWolf-active (finishing 8yfmt residual).
close_reason: Rejected on fresh rch-built paired SET p16 benchmark: 500k paired looked noisy/favorable to candidate (1.815s +/-0.330 baseline vs 1.649s +/-0.042 candidate), but confirming 2M paired gate rejected it: baseline 6.9345s +/-0.3710, candidate 7.4874s +/-0.1327, baseline 1.08x +/-0.06 faster. Last-run throughput/tail also favored baseline (273185 ops/s, p99 4687us vs 269142 ops/s, p99 6107us). Candidate source removed; no code retained. Duplicate of previously rejected gu5nf.24 batch-publish lever. Next attack should be a deeper runtime primitive, not per-command registry publication.

title: fr-protocol golden coverage gap: 3 of 10 RESP3 prefixes (verbatim '=', attribute '|', blob error '!') lack fail-closed-rejection goldens
description:
/testing-golden-artifacts audit:

  ls crates/fr-protocol/tests/golden | grep unsupported_resp3
    unsupported_resp3_big_number_prefix.golden     '('
    unsupported_resp3_boolean_prefix.golden        '#'
    unsupported_resp3_double_prefix.golden         ','
    unsupported_resp3_map_prefix.golden            '%'
    unsupported_resp3_null_prefix.golden           '_'
    unsupported_resp3_push_prefix.golden           '>'
    unsupported_resp3_set_prefix.golden            '~'

The fail-closed gate at lib.rs:238-241 rejects 10 RESP3 prefixes when allow_resp3=false:

  b'~' | b'%' | b'#' | b',' | b'_' | b'(' | b'=' | b'|' | b'>' | b'!'
      if !config.allow_resp3 =>
  {
      Err(RespParseError::UnsupportedResp3Type(prefix))
  }

But only 7 of those 10 have rejection goldens. Missing:
  '='  verbatim string
  '|'  attribute (recently touched by xfxtd + oafun)
  '!'  blob error

The xfxtd / oafun fixes specifically modified the attribute path; without a rejection golden a future refactor that accidentally allows '|' through in fail-closed mode would slip past golden tests.

Fix: add 3 goldens following the existing parse_and_snapshot pattern (lib.rs:261). Each pins the UnsupportedResp3Type rejection wording for its respective prefix when allow_resp3=false.
close_reason: Added 3 missing RESP3 fail-closed-rejection goldens for verbatim '=' (byte 61), attribute '|' (byte 124), blob error '!' (byte 33). Each pins UnsupportedResp3Type(<prefix-byte>) the way the existing 7 goldens do for big_number/boolean/double/map/null/push/set. Golden suite now covers the full 10-prefix matrix matching the fail-closed gate at fr-protocol/src/lib.rs:238-241. All 41 fr-protocol golden tests pass; clippy clean. Catches a future refactor that accidentally allows '=', '|', or '!' through in fail-closed mode.

title: cjson.decode: missing /-unescape and trailing-comma strictness
description:
fr's cjson.decode has several divergences from vendored Redis 7.2.4's bundled lua_cjson:

1. **`\/` decode passthrough**: `cjson.decode('"\/"')` returns the literal two bytes `\/` on fr. Vendored unescapes to `/`. fr's escape match in lua_eval.rs:9515 omits the `'/'` branch, so the catch-all preserves `\` + `/`.

2. **Trailing comma accepted in arrays**: `pcall(cjson.decode, '[1,]')` returns `true` on fr (decoder treats trailing empty slice as nil), `false` on vendored.

3. **Leading comma accepted in arrays**: `pcall(cjson.decode, '[,1]')` returns `true` on fr, `false` on vendored.

4. **Double comma accepted in arrays**: `pcall(cjson.decode, '[1,,2]')` returns `true` on fr, `false` on vendored.

5. **Trailing comma accepted in objects**: `pcall(cjson.decode, '{"a":1,}')` returns `true` on fr, `false` on vendored.

Root cause for (2)-(5): split_json_values (line ~9660) splits on commas but doesn't validate that the resulting slices are non-empty. Empty slices then route to json_to_lua_value which silently returns Nil for empty input (matching the `null` arm).

Probe sweep #68 (probe138.sh focused):
  vendored cjson.decode('"\/"')     -> /       fr -> \/
  vendored pcall(_, '[1,]')         -> false   fr -> true
  vendored pcall(_, '[,1]')         -> false   fr -> true
  vendored pcall(_, '[1,,2]')       -> false   fr -> true
  vendored pcall(_, '{"a":1,}')     -> false   fr -> true

Out of scope for this bead (cjson.null sentinel): `cjson.decode('[null,null]')` length and `tostring(cjson.decode('{"a":null}').a)` both diverge because fr returns Lua nil while vendored returns a `cjson.null` userdata sentinel. That requires a wider refactor (separate bead).

Fix plan:
- Add `b'/' => result.push(b'/')` branch to the JSON string-escape match.
- Validate split_json_values output: any empty trimmed slice (anywhere — first, middle, last) becomes an "Expected value" error.
- Regression test pins (1)-(5) against vendored's strict behavior.
close_reason: completed

title: MEMORY USAGE for streams diverges from vendored: linear vs preallocated listpack
description: estimate_stream_memory_usage_bytes diverges from vendored Redis 7.2.4 by 8 bytes for 1 entry and grows linearly while vendored stays static at 4728 for 1-3 entries. Probe results from frankenredis-40v6f: empty=656(v)/4688(fr), 1=4728(v)/4720(fr), 2=4728(v)/4744(fr), 3=4728(v)/4768(fr). Two structural issues: (1) REDIS_STREAM_BASE_OVERHEAD_BYTES=4_656 over-counts the empty case by ~4000 bytes — vendored empty stream is just the stream struct + rax base (~656). The 4656 constant collapses listpack reservation INTO the base instead of allocating it on first entry. (2) The per-entry payload calc uses estimate_listpack_entry_bytes (newly fixed for general listpack) but does not model the stream listpack 'master entry' preamble (count + deleted + num-fields + per-field-listpack-entries + terminator) nor the per-record postlude (flags + entry-id + num-fields + field+value pairs + lp-count). Properly modeling this needs: empty stream = stream-struct + rax-base ~= 656; new entry triggers a listpack-node allocation rounded to next jemalloc class (>=4096); subsequent entries fit in the existing node up to capacity, then a new node allocates. Test case crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:14905 (memory_usage_small_encoding_baselines_match_vendored_redis xs stream case) currently asserts Some(4_720) for the fr-aligned value — when fixed, restore expected to Some(4_728). Filed during REVIEW PHASE; see frankenredis-40v6f closure notes.
close_reason: MEMORY USAGE for streams now matches vendored Redis 7.2.4 byte-for-byte across 0, 1, and small-N entry cases. Verified: empty stream (XGROUP CREATE xs g 0 MKSTREAM) = 656 (vendored) = 656 (fr). 1-entry stream = 4728 (both). The fix: (1) REDIS_STREAM_BASE_OVERHEAD_BYTES dropped from 4_656 to 624 (the actual value-side overhead: robj(16) + stream struct(80) + 2 rax bases). The previous 4_656 inflated the empty case by ~4000 bytes because it collapsed the listpack-node allocation INTO the baseline. (2) Added REDIS_STREAM_LISTPACK_NODE_BASE_BYTES = 4072 and REDIS_STREAM_NODE_MAX_ENTRIES = 100 to model upstream's per-node behavior: each batch of <=100 entries occupies one listpack node with ~4072B initial reservation. (3) estimate_stream_memory_usage_bytes now computes ceil(n / NODE_MAX_ENTRIES) nodes and uses max(node_count * NODE_BASE_BYTES, listpack_alloc_size(payload)) so dense entries that overflow the per-node base reservation grow correctly. Test fixture in crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:14946 restored to the canonical vendored value Some(4_728). Verified: 336 fr-store + 194 fr-conformance tests pass. Caveats: this model holds for the common XADD-with-tiny-entry case. Dense large-entry streams (where per-entry payload >= 40B, pushing node alloc past 4072) will still diverge slightly because fr does not track per-node listpack boundaries -- it sums payload globally rather than per-node. Acceptable for MEMORY USAGE reporting; if precise large-stream accounting is needed later, simulate the upstream listpack-node split discipline. Bundles MEMORY USAGE stream parity completion with the earlier 40v6f win.

title: Lua interpreter error wording divergences (index prefix, arith wording, for step=0)
description: Three small Lua-runtime divergences from vendored 7.2.4: (1) 'attempt to index a TYPE value' lacks the user_script:1: prefix (same pattern as 8w2ag for call). (2) 'attempt to perform arithmetic on a non-number' should say 'attempt to perform arithmetic on a <type> value' -- vendored uses the specific Lua type name (e.g. 'string value'). (3) 'for i=1,10,0 do break end' errors in fr with "'for' step is zero" but vendored allows step=0 silently -- it's an infinite loop, but a body break or external termination is the user's problem. Fix: add 'user_script:1: ' prefix to the indexing error, change arithmetic error to use type_name, remove the step=0 rejection. The 'local NAME (a TYPE value)' context detail for indexing is tracked in md71j.
close_reason: Three Lua interpreter error divergences fixed: (1) all 8 'attempt to index a TYPE value' Err call sites in lua_eval.rs now carry the 'user_script:1: ' source-location prefix; (2) arithmetic error in eval_binop uses lv.type_name()/rv.type_name() instead of the generic 'non-number' wording, emitting 'attempt to perform arithmetic on a string value' / 'a boolean value' / etc to match upstream's per-type detail; (3) removed the eager 'for step is zero' rejection at the for-loop runtime so 'for i=1,10,0 do break end' executes silently (Lua 5.1 doesn't enforce step!=0; if the body doesn't break, MAX_ITERATIONS still catches infinite loops). Live differential probe: 6 cases byte-exact (for step=0 returns 'ok', arith on string/bool errors match wording, numeric and numeric-string arith regression-clean). The 'local NAME (a TYPE value)' context detail for indexing is tracked in md71j -- the prefix and base error are landed here. Tests: fr-command 910 pass.

title: perf(store): niche-pack Entry expiry as Option<NonZeroU64> — Entry 80B->72B (w2t01 pt3)
close_reason: Shipped 35e6513e8: expires_at_ms Option<u64>(16B)->Option<NonZeroU64>(8B niche-packed). Entry 80->72B. CUMULATIVE (s5ou4+knbp2+4iu08): Value 120->32, Entry 168->72 = 96B/key (~57%), ~960MB on 10M keys. Byte-identical (expiry_ms()/set_expiry_ms() accessors; ~30 sites). fr-store 515, fr-runtime 411. NOTE: boxing List/Stream is a NO-OP (24B String+8B tag floors Value at 32). Remaining memory lever: SSO on Value::String (value-bytes inline, saves the per-small-string heap block) — multi-firing.

title: [perf] Pass213 confirm large SET recv/syscall residual
description: Pass 213 evidence bead. Heap-buffer reuse under mimalloc is rejected; the remaining plausible large-SET target is recv/syscall count and nonblocking read chunk behavior. Baseline current origin/main with rch-built release-perf binaries, run focused large SET 64KiB/256KiB/1MiB benchmarks and syscall-count evidence. Do not edit source unless the focused evidence names a real target and the one-lever Score estimate clears 2.0. Proof requirements for any later lever: raw RESP SET/STRLEN/GET golden sha256 across 64KiB/256KiB/1MiB, ordering/side effects unchanged, crate-scoped rch check/clippy/tests.

No source lever clears Score>=2.0; artifacts in artifacts/optimization/coralox-pass214-current-profile/.

title: ACL GETUSER/LIST commands string: rule order not preserved (categories vs individual)
description: ACL GETUSER 'commands' field reorders rules vs insertion order. SETUSER u +get +set +@read -> oracle 'commands'='-@all +get +set +@read' (insertion order), fr='-@all +@read +get +set' (categories grouped/reordered). Redis preserves the exact order rules were added (ACLDescribeUser walks the command rules list in order). fr appears to emit categories then individual commands sorted. Same divergence in ACL LIST. Found via ACL differential probe.
close_reason: Fixed: ACL GETUSER/LIST 'commands' field now re-emits rules in application order, matching upstream acl.c::ACLDescribeUserCommandRules. fr previously rebuilt the string from sorted HashSets (categories then commands, each sorted), so +get +set +@read rendered as '-@all +@read +get +set'. Added an ordered command_rule_tokens Vec (source of truth for rendering; HashSets retained for enforcement): each +cmd/-cmd/+@cat/-@cat records a token, replacing any prior token for the same target at the new position; +@all/-@all/reset clear it. commands_string emits baseline + tokens in order. Verified byte-exact vs vendored 7.2.4 across 14 canonicalization scenarios (insertion order, dedup, sign-flip, +@all clearing, subcommands). New test acl_getuser_commands_preserve_rule_insertion_order; 405 fr-runtime lib + 68 ACL + 194 fr-conformance pass, clippy clean.

title: perf: SCAN resume cache single-slot -> LRU so concurrent scans stay O(N) — 11.4x for 4 interleaved scans
description: The 3e92e SCAN resume cache was a single Option<ScanResume> slot, so K CONCURRENT scans (distinct cursors from different clients interleaved) thrashed it: each call mismatched the other scan's cached entry, falling every interleaved call back to the O(N^2/batch) skip walk. Replaced with a small LRU (Vec<ScanResume>, cap 8): each in-flight scan keeps its own resume point matched by (cursor, generation); on hit the entry is taken+removed, advanced, re-inserted; >cap interleaved scans evict oldest (FIFO) and fall back. 11.41x on 4 interleaved full scans of 12k @ COUNT10 (single-slot 286ms->LRU 25ms). Byte-identical: K interleaved full scans return the same per-scan keys under LRU, single-slot, and no-cache, each the full keyspace; 582 fr-store + 11 fr-command scan tests pass. Extends 3e92e to the concurrent-client case.
close_reason: Shipped 95672f677: scan_cache is now a Vec<ScanResume> LRU (cap 8) instead of a single Option slot; each interleaved scan keeps its own (cursor,generation)-matched resume point. 11.41x on 4 interleaved full scans of 12k. Byte-identical (5 interleaved scans == single-slot == no-cache, each returns full keyspace, COUNT 1/3/7/16); 582 fr-store + 11 fr-command scan tests pass. Single-client path unchanged. Extends 3e92e to concurrent clients.

title: core_expiry live oracle skips all wall-clock-stable TTL cases
description: core_expiry live-oracle coverage currently returns early and skips the entire 256-case expiry fixture because absolute-time cases are fixture-clock-vs-wall-clock asymmetric. That skip also drops wall-clock-stable cases that do not need an upstream clock override: missing-key TTL/PTTL, no-expiry TTL/PTTL, relative EXPIRE/PTTL/TTL with harness sleep alignment, PERSIST, wrong-arity cases, option conflict errors, parser errors, and past/zero expiry deletion checks where both implementations should agree immediately. Fix: replace the blanket skip in crates/fr-conformance/src/lib.rs with a curated stable case list driven through run_live_redis_diff_for_cases, leaving absolute EXPIREAT/PEXPIREAT/EXPIRETIME/PEXPIRETIME fixture-clock-dependent cases documented as deferred. Acceptance: live_redis_core_expiry_matches_runtime runs at least the stable subset against vendored Redis and passes without FR_CONFORMANCE_LIVE_ORACLE_STRICT exclusions.
close_reason: Replaced the blanket core_expiry live-oracle skip with an 86-case wall-clock-stable subset covering missing/no-expiry TTL, immediate relative expiry, PERSIST, option conflicts, parser errors, and non-positive expiry deletion paths. Focused live oracle passes 86/86; workspace check and clippy pass.

title: Config-default parity: hash-max-listpack-entries (512) + list-max-listpack-size (-2) match redis COMPILED defaults but not the shipped redis.conf (128/128) the gauntlet oracle uses
description: fr's encoding-threshold defaults match redis 7.2.4 COMPILED defaults exactly: list-max-listpack-size=-2 (config.c:3154), hash-max-listpack-entries=512 (config.c:3215). The gauntlet oracle (and the shipped redis.conf, and apt/docker redis) set BOTH to 128. Result: OBJECT ENCODING + DUMP for lists >128 entries and hashes 129-512 entries diverge from the oracle (oracle quicklist/hashtable at 128; fr stays listpack to -2/512). fr's CODE is correct — CONFIG SET fr list-max-listpack-size 128 + hash-max-listpack-entries 128 makes OBJECT ENCODING and DUMP byte-exact vs oracle (verified). DECISION for maintainer: should fr default to the shipped-redis.conf values (128/128) to match the gauntlet reference + most real deployments, or keep redis compiled defaults? NOT changed unilaterally: list -2 is deliberate (frankenredis-10lqb comment) and a flip would shift OBJECT ENCODING for any 129-512 element hash / 129+ element list. All other encoding thresholds already match. See memory project_config_default_vs_oracle.
close_reason: NOT A BUG / harness methodology. fr's hash-max-listpack-entries=512 and list-max-listpack-size=-2 match redis 7.2.4 COMPILED defaults and are DELIBERATE (see fr-store/src/lib.rs:1829 frankenredis-0o5hj, which corrected a prior wrong 128 to 512 to match bare vendored redis). The gauntlet oracle on :17380 is started with a non-default config (shipped-redis.conf 128/128), so OBJECT ENCODING/DUMP differential probes for lists >128 and hashes 129-512 spuriously diverge. Resolution: this is a probe-harness config difference, NOT a fr code change. For encoding/DUMP differential probes, start the oracle with bare defaults OR CONFIG SET both servers identically first. fr's encoding code is verified correct (byte-exact OBJECT ENCODING + DUMP once configs match). Documented in memory project_config_default_vs_oracle.

title: CONFIG SET ranges/enums for defrag/migration/slowlog/sanitize/shutdown/repl-diskless-load
description: Multiple CONFIG SET fields lacked range/enum validation matching upstream config.c: (1) maxmemory-eviction-tenacity (range 0-100), active-defrag-cycle-min/max (range 1-99) accepted any value (2) cluster-migration-barrier returned generic parse_i64_arg wording instead of CONFIG SET wrapper with range [0, 2147483647] (3) slowlog-log-slower-than emitted generic parse_i64_arg error wording (4) active-defrag-max-scan-fields, sanitize-dump-payload, shutdown-on-sigint/sigterm, tls-protocols, hll-sparse-max-bytes were not in CONFIG_STATIC_PARAMS so returned 'Unknown option' (5) repl-diskless-load enum {disabled, on-empty-db, swapdb} silently accepted invalid values

title: Honor live-oracle matrix case filters in orchestrator

The orchestrator therefore runs whole fixtures instead of the pinned stable subset, making matrix coverage drift from direct smoke tests and blocking safe promotion of partial command-family suites.
close_reason: live_oracle_orchestrator now deserializes matrix case_names, validates command-mode use, and forwards them to live_oracle_diff as --case filters

title: Conformance fixture core_scripting.json missing 7 XFAIL entries listed in test code

The fixture file fr-conformance/fixtures/core_scripting.json doesn't contain these case names. Either:

Pre-existing on main, blocks fr-conformance test pass. Not introduced by recent commits.
close_reason: Removed 7 stale XFAIL_CASES names (eval_redis_call_config_get_*) from fr-conformance/src/lib.rs that no longer exist in core_scripting.json after commit 478bc0d. Replaced inline with a comment explaining the relationship to the consolidated noscript pin. live_redis_core_scripting_matches_runtime now passes. Commit 77ff738.

title: call_function bad-callable arm checks current_invocation_name (wrong) instead of caller's frame kind
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4:

  pcall(function() for k,v in nil do end end)
    vendored: user_script:1: attempt to call a nil value
    fr:       attempt to call a nil value                   (missing prefix)

  pcall(function() for k,v in 1 do end end)
    vendored: user_script:1: attempt to call a number value
    fr:       attempt to call a number value                (missing prefix)

The fix from frankenredis-o3epl made the call_function bad-callable arm conditional on `self.current_invocation_name.is_some()` for the pcall(C-builtin) case. But that breaks the in-Lua-function case: pcall clears current_invocation_name, and when the inner Lua function body triggers a for-loop iterator call to nil, current_invocation_name stays None — so fr omits the prefix even though there's a Lua frame above.

Upstream luaG_addinfo derives source:line from the immediate Lua caller's frame. The right discriminator is whether the *caller* of the bad call is a Lua frame (or the top-level chunk, which counts as Lua).

Fix at fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs ~line 4470:
- Before pushing to lua_frame_kinds in call_function, snapshot whether the CALLER is a Lua frame:
    caller_is_lua = lua_frame_kinds.last() != Some(&false)
    (i.e., true if last frame is true OR stack empty/top-level)
- Use that flag in the bad-callable arms (Nil / Coroutine / other) instead of `current_invocation_name.is_some()`.

The current_invocation_name check is correct for the C-builtin argerror path (frankenredis-o3epl's math.random etc.) — pcall clears the name, and call_function for a C-builtin doesn't reset it before invoking call_builtin. So that path keeps working.
close_reason: Replaced the current_invocation_name check in call_function's bad-callable arm with a caller_is_lua_frame snapshot captured BEFORE pushing to lua_frame_kinds. Tracks upstream luaG_addinfo: a Lua-frame caller (Lua function body OR the top-level chunk — empty kind stack) supplies source:line; a C-frame caller (RustFunction, e.g. pcall directly calling a non-callable) leaves it empty. New regression test call_function_bad_callable_uses_caller_frame_kind_4ovjf pins both shapes (3 Lua-caller cases + 2 C-caller cases). Existing math_random_and_attempt_to_call_errors_pcall_shape_o3epl + 1012-test suite still passes. Live-diff vs vendored 16380: 7/7 cases match byte-for-byte.

title: perf(server): blocking-command wakeup index + timeout heap — O(blocked) per tick -> O(ready-clients + due-timeouts)
description: check_blocked_clients (fr-server/main.rs:2584) iterates ALL blocked tokens every tick, testing each against the ready-key set AND its deadline — O(total_blocked) per tick regardless of how many keys are ready or timers are due. Redis: db->blocking_keys dict (key->client list) wakes only clients on a ready key, + a timeout radix/heap for due timers. PLAN: (1) HashMap<Vec<u8>, Vec<Token>> blocking_keys, maintained on block/unblock; check_blocked_clients drains ready_keys and processes only blocking_keys[k] per ready k. (2) BinaryHeap/BTreeMap<deadline, Token> for timeouts; pop due. Parity-DELICATE: wakeup ORDER when multiple clients block on one key — redis is FIFO (block order); fr currently uses HashSet iteration (arbitrary) so this could FIX order (verify no test pins current arbitrary order). TARGET: large for many-idle-blocked-clients workloads (e.g. 10k BLPOP consumers); ~1x for few blockers. main.rs is large + cargo-fmt-dirty (selective-stage).
close_reason: Completed: safe-Rust BlockedWakeIndex sidecar narrows blocked-client wake checks from O(blocked) tick scans to ready-key/due-timeout/WAIT candidates. rch A/B: 10000 blocked clients x3 keys, one ready key, scan 147 ms -> index 38 us focused (3849.78x), full fr-server replay 147 ms -> 31 us (4721.72x). Behavior proof and sha256 evidence in artifacts/optimization/crimsonhill-perf-20260604-4pbq8-blocked-index/.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Partial: alloc-free tick scan landed (1463666dc) — BlockingOp::any_key_ready borrows keys instead of keys() deep-cloning Vec<Vec<u8>> per blocked client per tick (1.37x, alloc->0). STRUCTURAL part still open: O(blocked)/tick -> O(ready+due) needs reverse key->tokens index (per-key FIFO) + timeout min-heap. Wakeup-order parity-delicate; a stale incremental index = missed-wakeup correctness bug. Needs dedicated multi-step pass.

title: Fix ZINCRBY to allow infinite score results
description: ZINCRBY incorrectly rejected increment operations that resulted in positive or negative infinity. Redis allows infinite scores in Sorted Sets. Fixed by removing the is_infinite() check while keeping the is_nan() rejection.

title: Implement SENTINEL SET rename-command parity
description: Redis 7.2.4 sentinel.c supports SENTINEL SET <master> rename-command <oldname> <newname>, rejects empty old/new names, removes a prior mapping for the old command, and does not store a self-mapping when old/new are the same command. fr-sentinel currently rejects rename-command as an unknown SENTINEL SET option. Scope: crates/fr-sentinel only.
close_reason: Completed

close_reason: Completed

title: fr-runtime: CLIENT LIST output diverges from upstream Redis 7.2 (missing argv-mem/multi-mem/rbs/rbp/redir, extra watch, flags position)
description:
fr-runtime's CLIENT LIST output diverges from upstream Redis 7.2 in several ways: (1) MISSING fields: argv-mem (input argv memory), multi-mem (queued MULTI memory), rbs (read-buffer size), rbp (read-buffer position), redir (CLIENT TRACKING REDIRECT target id, default -1). (2) EXTRA field: watch=N (count of WATCHed keys) — Redis 7.2 dropped this field. (3) FIELD ORDER: fr emits flags=N at the very end; Redis 7.2 emits flags right after idle, before db. (4) lib-name / lib-ver order: Redis emits these AFTER resp; fr emits before. (5) fd is hardcoded to 0 vs Redis's actual epoll fd — minor but a real divergence; tools that grep on fd=N may misbehave.

Acceptance: a fresh fr-server with one connected client emits CLIENT LIST in a single line whose tokens (and order) match upstream Redis 7.2's CLIENT LIST output for an equivalent client, modulo the fd field (fd=0 stays since fr doesn't track real fds for clients) and the per-instance values (id, addr port, qbuf, qbuf-free, tot-mem). Tools like redis_exporter that parse the line on field names (regex) will match. The conformance harness's CLIENT LIST live-oracle test gains a side-by-side text diff that asserts identical token ordering.

Reference: ran 'CLIENT LIST' against fr (port 16500) and vendored redis 7.2.4 (port 16501) with the same shape of single connected redis-cli client. fr line: 'id=2 addr=... laddr=... fd=0 name= age=0 idle=0 db=0 sub=0 psub=0 ssub=0 multi=-1 watch=0 qbuf=0 qbuf-free=0 obl=0 oll=0 omem=0 tot-mem=0 events=r cmd=client|list user=default lib-name= lib-ver= resp=2 flags=N'. Redis line: 'id=3 addr=... laddr=... fd=7 name= age=0 idle=0 flags=N db=0 sub=0 psub=0 ssub=0 multi=-1 qbuf=26 qbuf-free=20448 argv-mem=10 multi-mem=0 rbs=16384 rbp=16384 obl=0 oll=0 omem=0 tot-mem=37786 events=r cmd=client|list user=default redir=-1 resp=2 lib-name= lib-ver='.
close_reason: Both fr-runtime's client_info_line_for_session and fr-command's client_info_line now emit CLIENT LIST/INFO with upstream Redis 7.2 field order: id addr laddr fd name age idle flags db sub psub ssub multi qbuf qbuf-free argv-mem multi-mem rbs rbp obl oll omem tot-mem events cmd user redir resp lib-name lib-ver. Dropped watch=N (removed in 7.2), added redir + buffer-accounting fields (always 0 since fr doesn't track per-client buffers). Pinned by client_list_field_order_matches_upstream_redis_72 in fr-runtime. Shipped in 057c446.

title: fr-command: BITPOS parses trailing args before key lookup, leaking syntax errors for missing keys
description:
Upstream t_string.c::bitposCommand looks up the key BEFORE parsing the trailing [start [end [BIT|BYTE]]] args. A missing key short-circuits to (bit ? -1 : 0) regardless of any unknown trailing arguments.

fr-command's bitpos parsed the args first via the strict argc<3/argc>6 gate, returning 'ERR syntax error' for cases like 'BITPOS nokey 1 0 -1 NOSUCH' even though the key didn't exist.

Differential probe vs vendored redis 7.2.4 (FLUSHALL state):
  BITPOS nokey 1 0 -1 NOSUCH  → fr SyntaxError, redis -1
  BITPOS nokey 1 0 -1 BYTE BIT → fr SyntaxError, redis -1

Fix: reorder bitpos to (1) parse the bit arg, (2) check key_type — None → return Integer(if bit==1 {-1} else {0}); wrong type → WrongType — (3) parse trailing args. Update the bitpos_too_many_arguments_is_syntax_error test to set a key first.

title: fr-command/build.rs: ACL category emission alphabetizes via BTreeSet, but COMMAND INFO requires JSON declaration order to match upstream
description:
crates/fr-command/build.rs accumulates per-command ACL categories into a `BTreeSet<String>`, which iterates in lexicographic order. The generated UPSTREAM_ACL_CATEGORY_ENTRIES therefore stores categories alphabetically, and `COMMAND INFO get` returns:

  fr in-process: [@fast, @read, @string]
  upstream:      [@read, @string, @fast]

vendored Redis 7.2.4 emits categories in the order they were declared in commands/*.json + the flag-derived order (e.g. GET's get.json declares acl_categories: ["READ", "STRING"] and FAST is derived from the FAST flag, so the upstream order is read, string, fast).

The conformance fixture core_server.json::command_info_get pins the upstream order. fr's in-process Runtime fails the test because of the BTreeSet sort. Live fr-server happens to also use this path so my earlier "live probes match" check was wrong — the OLD live fr binary (May 2 build) was from BEFORE 86f8207 introduced the build.rs derivation; the NEW code (cargo test) regressed.

Fix: switch the per-command accumulator from BTreeSet<String> to a deduplicated Vec<String> that preserves JSON-declaration-then-flag-derived order. The flag-derived appends already happen in a deterministic flag-check order (write, read, admin/dangerous, pubsub, fast, blocking, slow) — making them positional preserves upstream's declaration order.

Acceptance: COMMAND INFO get returns [@read, @string, @fast]; core_server_conformance command_info_get fixture passes.

Reference: /reality-check final cleanup of the al4ak/16vnh/o1w54 chain on 2026-05-05.
notes: Fixed crates/fr-command/build.rs to emit ACL categories in upstream Redis 7.2.4's CMD_CATEGORY_* bitmap iteration order (server.h enum order: keyspace, read, write, set, sortedset, list, hash, string, bitmap, hyperloglog, geo, stream, pubsub, admin, fast, slow, blocking, dangerous, connection, transaction, scripting). Replaced BTreeSet<String> (lexicographic) with Vec<String> + push_unique + sort_canonical post-processing. COMMAND INFO get now returns [@read, @string, @fast] matching upstream byte-for-byte. Conformance core_server_conformance: 281 passing → all passing (last red case fixed). ACL CAT golden tests still pass.

title: redis parity: runtime EVAL BGREWRITEAOF regression should expect NOSCRIPT
description: Full fr-runtime runs are still red on eval_bgrewriteaof_rewrites_aof_snapshot because command parity now rejects BGREWRITEAOF from Lua with NOSCRIPT, but the runtime regression still expects the old success reply. Update the runtime expectation to the Redis-aligned NOSCRIPT surface and verify through rch.
close_reason: Completed

title: math.randomseed silently accepts non-numeric / missing arg (vendored errors)
description:
Upstream lmathlib.c::math_randomseed calls luaL_checkint(L,1) which
raises 'bad argument #1 (number expected, got TYPE)' (or 'got no
value' when no arg passed). fr's implementation silently accepts
any value via .to_number() and a guard `if let Some` so missing
args / nil / bool / table / non-numeric strings become no-ops.

Repro:
  EVAL "local ok,e = pcall(math.randomseed, nil); return tostring(ok)..':'..tostring(e)" 0
    vendored: false:bad argument #1 to '?' (number expected, got nil)
    fr:       true:nil

  EVAL "local ok,e = pcall(math.randomseed, {}); ..."
    vendored: false:bad argument #1 to '?' (number expected, got table)
    fr:       true:nil

  EVAL "local ok,e = pcall(math.randomseed); ..."     (no args)
    vendored: false:bad argument #1 to '?' (number expected, got no value)
    fr:       true:nil

Numeric and numeric-string seeds still work on both
(math.randomseed(42), math.randomseed('42')).

Fix at fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs:6339 — replace the silent guard
with a required-number arg-check that routes through the upstream
'bad argument' wording.
close_reason: completed

title: redis parity: ACL SETUSER should support skip-sanitize-payload
description: Upstream ACLSetUser in legacy_redis_code/redis/src/acl.c supports both skip-sanitize-payload and sanitize-payload, but local AuthState::set_user at crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:751 only treats sanitize-payload as a no-op and has no state for the skip variant. Add explicit sanitize mode state plus GETUSER, ACL LIST, and ACL SAVE parity for the two flags.
close_reason: Completed

title: perf: ZRANDMEMBER count avoids O(n) full-zset materialize — 8.5x for small count over large zset
description: zrandmember_count cloned EVERY (member,score) of the zset (zs.iter_asc().map(to_vec).collect()) before sampling, even for ZRANDMEMBER key 1 — O(n) allocations. SRANDMEMBER (rndcnt) and HRANDFIELD already avoid this via O(1) IndexMap get_index, but the zset is BTreeMap-backed (no O(1) nth) so it took the materialize shortcut. Added SortedSet::members_at_indices: single ordered pass cloning ONLY the chosen indices (stop once all found) -> O(distinct picks) allocations instead of O(n). Same next_rand() index-draw sequence => byte-identical output (4000 random + edge cases vs materialize reference, golden 0x3f2b2cc40d718c60). 8.46x on ZRANDMEMBER 4-of-100k. Graveyard primitive: sample-without-materialize.
close_reason: Shipped 3ad34e595: SortedSet::members_at_indices single-pass clones only chosen indices -> ZRANDMEMBER count allocation O(n)->O(picks). 8.46x on 4-of-100k. Byte-identical output (same next_rand sequence; 4000 random + edge cases vs materialize reference, golden 0x3f2b2cc40d718c60); 573 fr-store tests pass.

close_reason: Added metamorphic property tests for STRING SETRANGE/GETRANGE operations in fr-store

title: Freeze Redis config form-feed splitter boundaries
description: Add sdssplitargs conformance coverage for form-feed handling: accepted as C isspace after closing quotes and while skipping blanks, but preserved inside bare tokens because Redis only breaks bare tokens on space, LF, CR, tab, or NUL.
