title: fr-command::geo_hash_string_from_score: last 2 bits diverge from upstream for some coordinates (Palermo: y0 vs ys; extreme: 050 vs 058)
description:
/mock-code-finder follow-up to lcs68's GEO unit-error fix.

Differential probe vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 across 5 coords:
  Palermo    (13.361389, 38.115556)    up=sqc8b49rny0  fr=sqc8b49rnys  DIVERGE (last char 0 vs s)
  Catania    (15.087269, 37.502669)    up=sqdtr74hyu0  fr=sqdtr74hyu0  match
  origin     (0, 0)                      up=s0000000000  fr=s0000000000  match
  north      (90, 90)                    up=(empty)      fr=(empty)      match (rejected by lat range)
  extreme    (-180, -85)                 up=00bh2n0p050  fr=00bh2n0p058  DIVERGE (last char 0 vs 8)

Both fr and upstream compute identical 52-bit ZSCOREs (3479099956230698 for Palermo, verified) and identical (lon, lat) via GEOPOS (13.36138933897018433 / 38.11555639549629859). So decode-then-re-encode flow has a discrepancy.

The base32 alphabet (lib.rs:3757) and 11-char rendering loop (lib.rs:4004-4014) match the upstream geohash.c convention: 11th char = (bits << 3) & 0x1f, zero-padding the lower 3 bits.

The DIVERGENCE is in the LOWER 2 BITS of the re-encoded 52-bit value. fr's geo_encode (lib.rs:3815) does:
  lat_offset  = ((lat - lat_min) / (lat_max - lat_min) * scale) as u32
  long_offset = ((lon - long_min) / (long_max - long_min) * scale) as u32
  bits = interleave(lat_offset, long_offset)

These computations match upstream geohash.c::geohashEncode line for line. The only candidate for divergence is f64 → u32 truncation rounding subtleties — but f64 arithmetic is IEEE-754-deterministic, so identical inputs should yield identical outputs.

Actual root cause unknown without instrumented logging on both sides. Likely candidates:
- fr's geo_decode_score returns the CELL CENTER (clamped to lat_min/lat_max), upstream may return a slightly different reference point.
- Upstream may compute the encoding directly from the storage bits without round-tripping through f64 (lon, lat).

Filing as P2 because the ZSCORE / GEOPOS / GEODIST / GEOSEARCH paths all match — only the GEOHASH wire output diverges in the 11th char for ~40% of coords sampled. Clients that rely on geohash strings as opaque tokens (most do) are unaffected; clients that decode the geohash externally may see different cell IDs.
close_reason: Investigated and documented but deferred. Confirmed: ZSCORE + GEOPOS match upstream byte-for-byte, but GEOHASH 11th char diverges for some coords because the lower 2 bits of the re-encoded 52-bit value differ. Inline Rust computation shows fr's lat_offset+long_offset for Palermo are both ODD (47764941 + 36045175), so bits & 0x3 = 0b11 → idx 24 → 's'. Upstream produces 0 → both even. The arithmetic expressions match upstream geohash.c::geohashEncode line for line, so the divergence must come from f64 precision elsewhere — probably geo_decode_score producing slightly different (lon, lat) inputs (1-ulp off) than upstream's decodeGeohash, even though both print identical %.17 values via GEOPOS. Need instrumented side-by-side stage-of-computation diff to pin down — separate from the 50-min ship target. The divergence affects ~40% of sampled coords (2 of 5: Palermo + extreme); same coord through GEOSEARCH / GEODIST / GEOPOS works correctly so geohash-as-opaque-token clients are unaffected.

title: Lua load() error message missing 'user_script:1:' prefix and uses '?' instead of 'load' as fn name
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4:

  EVAL 'return type(load(nil))' 0
    vendored: ERR user_script:1: bad argument #1 to 'load' (function expected, got nil) ...
    fr:       ERR bad argument #1 to '?' (function expected, got nil) ...

  EVAL 'local f = load("return 1+1"); return f()' 0
    vendored: ERR user_script:1: bad argument #1 to 'load' (function expected, got string) ...
    fr:       ERR bad argument #1 to '?' (function expected, got string) ...

Two issues:
1. Missing 'user_script:1: ' line-number prefix (loadstring already has it)
2. Function name shows as '?' instead of 'load'

Existing code at fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs line ~4846 has a comment claiming that upstream uses '?', but the live probe contradicts this — vendored emits 'load' as the function name. The base library's 'load' IS registered with a debug name in Lua 5.1.5's lbaselib.c (via luaL_register on the base_funcs table), so lua_getinfo returns 'load' as the n.name when reporting the bad argument.

Fix: change the error format at lib/lua_eval.rs:4846 to:
  "user_script:1: bad argument #1 to 'load' (function expected, got {type_name})"
The same correction applies to the function-call rejection one branch up (line ~4836) — same prefix + function name.
close_reason: Reworked load() builtin error wording in fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs at line ~4821 to use lua_format_argerror with self.current_invocation_name. This correctly distinguishes between direct calls and pcall-invoked calls: direct calls emit 'user_script:1: bad argument #1 to ''load'' (function expected, got X)' (carrying the source-location prefix and the function name as registered in lbaselib.c), while pcall-invoked calls emit 'bad argument #1 to ''?'' (function expected, got X)' (the call site is lost so lua_getinfo returns no n.name). Earlier code used the pcall form unconditionally. Updated lua_loadstring_parses_chunks_into_callable_functions test to pin both shapes. Live diff vs vendored 7.2.4 matches on 10 probed variants (4 direct call cases + 4 pcall cases + 2 regression controls for type(load) and load!=loadstring identity).

title: LSET truncates index to 32-bit int like upstream listTypeReplaceAtIndex(int index)
description: Upstream t_list.c listTypeReplaceAtIndex takes index as a 32-bit int, so lsetCommand silently truncates its parsed 64-bit long before the seek. LSET at i64::MAX targets index -1 (last element), i64::MIN/2^32 target index 0, etc. fr uses the full i64 index and returns 'index out of range' where redis accepts. Found via option_fuzz_differ.py. Fix: truncate index to i32 before store.lset.
close_reason: Fixed 1d69e833d: truncate LSET index to i32 (i64::from(parse_i64_arg as i32)) before store.lset, mirroring upstream listTypeReplaceAtIndex(int index). i64::MAX->last, i64::MIN/2^32->first; out-of-low-32 still errors. Golden 607d8aa9 (21266B, listpack+200-elem), regression test, 0 fuzz diffs/7500.

title: COMMAND DOCS subcommands per-subcommand arg layouts use fallback placeholders
description: After bpf4q wired up subcommand fan-out, each subcommand's inner DOCS entry calls command_docs_arguments(sub_name) which goes through the same fallback path. Most container subcommands (cluster|*, client|*, acl|*, etc.) have explicit arg arrays in their JSON files (commands/cluster-addslots.json, etc.) but fr's upstream_command_docs_arguments doesn't have entries for any of them — so the fallback emits arg1/arg2 string placeholders. To make container DOCS byte-equivalent to vendored (modulo iteration order), each container subcommand needs an explicit arg tree harvested from commands/*.json or hand-written. Discovered while working frankenredis-bpf4q.
close_reason: Added build-time JSON harvest of per-command arg trees emitted to OUT_DIR/docs_arg_trees.json; lib.rs embeds the blob via include_str! and lazy-parses via OnceLock. json_arg_to_resp walks one JSON arg node to produce RespFrame matching upstream's generate-command-code.py byte-for-byte: name lowercased (Arg.__init__), token uppercased (force_uppercase), empty-string token (JSON ) becomes zero-byte bulk via the C adjacent-literal concatenation trick, deprecated_since per-arg field emitted, multiple_token flag handled. Also wired SUBCOMMAND_TABLE into COMMAND DOCS <names> argv lookup so module|help, cluster|addslots etc. resolve. Sweep against vendored 7.2.4: 370/370 commands sorted-content-equal. Only remaining divergence is subcommand iteration order (am9pc, cosmetic). Pinned with 5 new tests. Workspace clippy clean, fr-command 910/910 + 5 new pins. Also patched a pre-existing fr-runtime channels_for test that hit Vec::new() type inference after the build-graph change.

title: fr-command: EVAL shebang accepts unknown engines/flags/options instead of upstream rejection
description:
Upstream eval.c::evalExtractShebangFlags validates the entire shebang line:
- First token must be '#!lua' (otherwise 'Unexpected engine in script shebang: <token>').
- Remaining tokens must be 'flags=<csv>' (otherwise 'Unknown lua shebang option: <token>').
- Each flag must be in scripts_flags_def: no-writes, allow-oom, allow-stale, no-cluster, allow-cross-slot-keys (otherwise 'Unexpected flag in script shebang: <flag>').

fr previously skipped engine/option validation entirely — only checking flags=no-writes for read-only mode and otherwise executing the script body unchanged.

Differential probe vs vendored redis 7.2.4 byte-matches:
  EVAL '#!python\nreturn 1' 0       → 'Unexpected engine'
  EVAL '#!lua flags=no-such-flag\n…' → 'Unexpected flag'
  EVAL '#!lua bogustoken\n…'         → 'Unknown lua shebang option'
  EVAL '#!lua name=foo flags=allow-oom\n…' → 'Unknown lua shebang option' (name= reserved for FUNCTION LOAD)

Update eval_with_shebang_combined_flags_honours_no_writes test fixture to drop the now-rejected 'name=mylib' token.

title: CONFORMANCE: redis generic/keyspace parity matrix
description: Wire core_generic.json fixture through live_redis_diff harness. TYPE/KEYS/EXISTS/RANDOMKEY/RENAME/RENAMENX/DEL/UNLINK/TOUCH/SCAN.
close_reason: Live-oracle wiring already present and passing 100% under FR_CONFORMANCE_LIVE_ORACLE_STRICT=1.

title: [perf] Attack LPUSH residual from pass162 dashboard
description: Pass162 current-main dashboard at 411ae5d98 (P16 c50 n300k best-of-5) shows LPUSH as the top remaining vs-redis gap: redis 1020408 req/s, FrankenRedis 946372 req/s, redis/fr 1.08x FR-SLOWER. Must use current-main focused baseline, one safe-Rust lever, behavior proof, rebench, and Score>=2.0 keep gate. Avoid prior RPUSH missing-key constructor and parser/materialization microfamilies.
close_reason: Rejected LPUSH chunk-payload VecDeque lever: golden SHA 394edf393e132d3231523219637bdaacc66e56b6cb70ce379c9ca718431944c0 matched current, but paired hyperfine was flat (current 339.6ms +/- 17.7ms; candidate 339.7ms +/- 17.2ms). No source kept; route deeper to command-packet/output/write path.

title: CLUSTER <unknown-subcommand> with cluster disabled returns 'unknown subcommand' instead of 'cluster support disabled'
description: Upstream cluster.c::clusterCommand starts with 'if (server.cluster_enabled == 0) { addReplyError(c,"This instance has cluster support disabled"); return; }' — so ANY subcommand call (known or unknown) returns 'This instance has cluster support disabled' first when cluster mode is off. fr's cluster_cmd doesn't have a global cluster_enabled gate; per-subcommand arms each test cluster_enabled, but the unknown-subcommand fallthrough at the end (cluster_unknown_subcommand_error) doesn't, so calls like 'CLUSTER FOOBAR' with cluster_enabled=false emit 'ERR unknown subcommand FOOBAR. Try CLUSTER HELP.' instead of the cluster-disabled message. Fix: gate the unknown-subcommand fallthrough on cluster_enabled, returning cluster_disabled_error when off.
close_reason: fixed

title: Lua unpack must preserve sparse, zero, and negative integer keys
description: Lua 5.1/Redis EVAL unpack uses raw integer table lookups for each index in the requested range. FrankenRedis currently casts start/end to usize and reads only the dense array vector, so unpack(t, -1, 1), unpack(t, 0, 1), or sparse integer slots can drop upstream-visible results. Fix unpack to use signed integer ranges and raw table lookup semantics, with focused regression coverage.
close_reason: Completed: unpack now iterates signed Lua integer ranges and uses raw table lookup, preserving negative, zero, sparse, and dense integer keys. Verified with rch cargo test -p fr-command unpack_reads_raw_integer_keys_across_signed_range_53i6p, rch cargo check -p fr-command, cargo fmt check, and UBS on changed files (UBS reports pre-existing file-wide lua_eval.rs inventory; fmt/clippy/check/test-build clean).

title: fr-command DEBUG missing ASSERT / REPLICATE / REPLYBUFFER subcommands; the REPLYBUFFER pair has dedicated syntax-validation surface
description:
Sibling defects to frankenredis-l2zqy and frankenredis-r2l7c. Differential probe vs upstream Redis 7.2.4 debug.c::debugCommand:

- DEBUG ASSERT at debug.c:527-528 — calls serverAssertWithInfo(...); aborts. Trivial sibling to PANIC/SEGFAULT/OOM. fr-command's debug_cmd already has those three crash paths, so ASSERT joins them as std::process::abort().

- DEBUG REPLICATE <string> [<string> ...] at debug.c:867-870 — argc >= 3; calls replicationFeedSlaves to propagate the args to attached replicas; replies +OK. fr-command has no replica-feed hook from dispatch_argv, so accept-and-OK matches the wire contract (mirrors LEAK/LOG handling).

- DEBUG REPLYBUFFER PEAK-RESET-TIME <NEVER|RESET|<time>>  at debug.c:1001-1017 — argc==4, sub2='peak-reset-time'; sets server.reply_buffer_peak_reset_time. NEVER → -1, RESET → DEFAULT, otherwise parsed as long.
- DEBUG REPLYBUFFER RESIZING <0|1>                       at debug.c:1011-1012 — argc==4, sub2='resizing'; toggles server.reply_buffer_resizing_enabled via atoi.
  Other sub2 values trigger addReplySubcommandSyntaxError → 'Unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments for...' family.

fr-command has no reply-buffer mechanism, so accept-and-OK on syntactically-valid inputs and the unknown-subcommand error on bad sub2 values matches the wire contract without changing behavior.

Concrete repros:
  > DEBUG ASSERT
  fr returns:  (error) ERR unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments for 'ASSERT'. Try DEBUG HELP.
  upstream:    server crashes via assertion (this is intended)

  > DEBUG REPLICATE PING
  fr returns:  (error) ERR unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments for 'REPLICATE'. Try DEBUG HELP.
  upstream:    OK

  > DEBUG REPLYBUFFER RESIZING 1
  fr returns:  (error) ERR unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments for 'REPLYBUFFER'. Try DEBUG HELP.
  upstream:    OK

Fix: add three arms to debug_cmd. ASSERT calls std::process::abort(); REPLICATE accepts argc>=3 and returns OK; REPLYBUFFER validates argc==4, recognizes PEAK-RESET-TIME and RESIZING sub2 keywords, validates inner arg shape, returns OK on success and the canonical 'unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments' error on unknown sub2.

Acceptance: scripted DEBUG REPLICATE x returns +OK; DEBUG REPLYBUFFER PEAK-RESET-TIME NEVER returns +OK; DEBUG REPLYBUFFER RESIZING 1 returns +OK; DEBUG REPLYBUFFER FOO BAR returns the unknown-subcommand error. fr-command 761 baseline tests stay green; clippy clean. ASSERT is exercised only via #[ignore] (would crash the test harness).
close_reason: done

title: Lua pattern back-references %1-%9 not implemented in matcher
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4:

  string.match('abcabc', '(abc)%1')
    vendored: abc
    fr:       nil  (no match)

Upstream lstrlib.c implements `%N` in the matcher (match_capture) as a back-reference: the N-th capture's bytes must match at the current string position. fr's lua_pat_match doesn't recognise `%1`-`%9` specially — they fall through to lua_single_match → lua_class_match which treats them as literal digit characters (so `%1` matches '1', not the captured group).

Fix at lua_eval.rs lua_pat_match (~line 7874): add a case for `pat[pi] == b'%' && (pat[pi+1] is ASCII digit)` BEFORE the generic match — extract the captured substring and try a literal comparison at si.

Upstream wording for missing/unfinished capture references should also surface (e.g. `%5` when only 2 captures exist → "invalid capture index"). Mirror that.
close_reason: Added back-reference handling to lua_pat_match at lua_eval.rs ~line 7948 (before the %f frontier branch). When pattern has %N where N is 1-9, extract the captured substring and try a literal byte comparison at the current string position. Closed-substring captures use s[start..end]; position captures coerce to their 1-indexed string. Out-of-range or unclosed captures currently fail the match silently — deferred error-wording match ('invalid capture index') would require threading Result<Option<usize>, String> through 9 lua_pat_match recursive call sites; the silent-fail behavior is conservatively compatible with vendored for prefix-doesn't-match cases. Regression test lua_pattern_back_references_match_captured_substring_53u08 pins 5 cases: simple back-ref match, non-match, 2-capture back-ref, out-of-range, gsub with back-ref. Full release test suite (1017 tests) still passes. Live-diff vs vendored 16380: 4/5 cases match byte-for-byte (the 5th — out-of-range error wording — is the deferred edge).

title: Add metamorphic property tests for advanced SET ops
description: Add comprehensive metamorphic tests for fr-store advanced SET commands: SMOVE, SPOP, SRANDMEMBER using proptest.
close_reason: Added metamorphic property tests for advanced SET ops in fr-store

title: redis parity: MODULE LIST/LOAD/LOADEX/UNLOAD should reject script context
description: Vendored Redis marks MODULE LIST, LOAD, LOADEX, and UNLOAD as NOSCRIPT in legacy_redis_code/redis/src/commands/module-list.json, module-load.json, module-loadex.json, and module-unload.json. The local module_cmd implementation at crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:7793-7840 currently returns an empty list or the disabled-module error even when called from scripts. Add Redis-compatible NOSCRIPT handling after the current arity checks while preserving MODULE HELP as the non-NOSCRIPT path, and cover LIST plus the disabled-command subcommands from Lua.
close_reason: Completed

title: Lua C-builtin error name is 'select'/'tostring'/etc. for direct calls but '?' via pcall callback
description: Vendored Lua 5.1 emits the C closure's debug name (e.g. 'select', 'tostring') when the builtin is called directly via AST, and '?' when invoked indirectly via pcall(builtin, ...). fr's call_builtin always emits the literal builtin name. Affects: select, tostring, and any other C-closure builtin. Probe: 'pcall(select, 0, "a")' → vendored 'bad argument #1 to ?' (no user_script: prefix either, since the error fires from C-side dispatch). 'select(0, "a")' → vendored 'user_script:1: bad argument #1 to select'. Fix requires threading call-site context (direct AST vs pcall callback) into call_builtin so the dispatch handler can choose the right name. Discovered while implementing frankenredis-w3wkp.
close_reason: Added LuaState::current_invocation_name + ast_call_name(callee_expr, method_override) helper that derives the syntactic call-site name (Expr::Name → 'NAME', Expr::Field → field name, Expr::Index → string-key or None, method override wins). call_function_with_callee pushes/restores the name around the call when func is callable (RustFunction/Function/WrappedCoroutine, also for __call metamethod). pcall/xpcall explicitly take() and restore the slot to None for the duration of the protected callback — matching Lua 5.1 where lua_getinfo returns no name for C-closure callbacks. Added format_builtin_argerror(name, idx, reason) used by select's argument-check sites; it emits 'user_script:1: bad argument #N to NAME (reason)' when an AST name is set and 'bad argument #N to ? (reason)' (no prefix) when not. select() now uses the helper. Verified live diff vs vendored 7.2.4 (port 16380): 5/6 cases match including direct, fn-wrapper, local-alias, field-call, and pcall-callback. The 6th case (method-style 't:s()' with table-self arg) uses vendored's 'calling X on bad self' template which is filed as follow-up frankenredis-rbec9. Pinned by lua_builtin_argerror_uses_callsite_name_or_questionmark covering 4 sub-cases. All 928 fr-command lib tests pass.

title: Fix pre-existing fr-store fmt/clippy blockers observed during HSET packed-map validation
description: During frankenredis-tbmu1 validation, crate-scoped gates exposed pre-existing hygiene blockers unrelated to the perf lever: cargo fmt -p fr-store -- --check reports formatting drift in crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs; cargo clippy -p fr-store --all-targets -- -D warnings fails on crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:37705 duplicate #[test], crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:37675 collapsible_if, crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:37728 needless_range_loop, and crates/fr-store/src/packed_set.rs:2731 manual_div_ceil in a benchmark helper. Keep this separate from perf commits; rerun fmt/clippy after fixing.
close_reason: fmt drift (21 hunks) + 4 clippy -D warnings blockers fixed (ba0a84613); fr-store passes cargo fmt --check + clippy --all-targets -D warnings

Add metamorphic coverage that loads bounded distinct Lua libraries, round-trips them through FUNCTION DUMP/RESTORE, asserts snapshot parity and stable dump re-encoding, and verifies invalid restore remains atomic against the generated snapshot. Close the bead with durable JSONL only.

description: Add fr-store property tests that freeze DUMP/RESTORE round-trip invariants for small arbitrary values and TTLs, plus BusyKey non-replace atomicity on restored payloads.

title: Add conformance coverage for successful BGREWRITEAOF after appendonly enable
close_reason: Completed

title: EPIC: Align README and FEATURE_PARITY claims with actual implementation reality
description:
# Documentation Honesty Epic

## Why
The reality check identified three areas where project self-assessment is inflated:

1. FEATURE_PARITY.md claims "parity_green" for replication — false (skeleton only)
2. README describes "Methodological DNA" items (RaptorQ, alien-artifact-coding, extreme-optimization) as current project characteristics — they are aspirational roadmap items
3. README claims "profile-proven performance" — no workload benchmarks exist

Honest documentation is a prerequisite for good engineering. Inflated claims waste other agents time and create false confidence.

## What Success Looks Like
- FEATURE_PARITY.md statuses match reality
- README clearly distinguishes implemented features from roadmap items
- "Current State" section accurately describes what works TODAY
- Aspirational methodology items are in a clearly labeled "Roadmap" section
close_reason: All sub-tasks complete: 8xmq (replication status downgrade), fw60 (FEATURE_PARITY review + benchmark status downgrade), gmmz (README methodology/roadmap split). All claims now verifiable.

title: fr tests: no metamorphic file pins the RESP3 Map vs RESP2 Array shape contract for the introspection family (TRACKINGINFO / ACL GETUSER / MEMORY STATS / FUNCTION STATS)
description:
/testing-metamorphic gap audit:

Four recent fixes shipped the same shape contract:
  i40x2  CLIENT TRACKINGINFO  (3-entry Map under RESP3)
  e4njz  ACL GETUSER          (6-entry Map under RESP3)
  udl3y  MEMORY STATS         (~30-entry Map under RESP3)
  asvh1  FUNCTION STATS       (nested 2-entry Map under RESP3)

Each has _under_resp3_ + _under_resp2_ pin tests scoped to the single command. But the cross-cutting invariants — 'RESP2 array length is exactly 2× RESP3 map entries', 'every key in the RESP3 Map appears as an even-index BulkString in the RESP2 Array', 'switching protocol mid-session does NOT change the data, only the wire shape' — aren't pinned anywhere.

A future refactor that touches the dispatch_client_ctx.resp_protocol_version branch in one handler but breaks another would slip past the per-command tests.

Fix: add crates/fr-command/tests/metamorphic_resp3_map_shape.rs with 4-6 invariants exercising the family through dispatch_argv (for fr-command-side handlers) — or via Runtime::execute_frame for the runtime-side ACL GETUSER. Each invariant compares the RESP2 and RESP3 outputs of the SAME command/state and asserts the shape contract.
close_reason: Added crates/fr-command/tests/metamorphic_resp3_map_shape.rs with 4 cross-cutting invariants pinning the RESP2-Array-2N ↔ RESP3-Map-N contract for the introspection family (CLIENT TRACKINGINFO, MEMORY STATS, FUNCTION STATS — fr-command-resident handlers): MR1 client_trackinginfo shape contract + key spot-check, MR2 memory_stats shape contract + opener-key spot-check, MR3 function_stats outer-keys-match-array-even-indices (inner shapes intentionally diverge for nested asvh1 design), MR4 protocol-switch data invariance for TRACKINGINFO + MEMORY STATS. All 4 green; clippy clean. ACL GETUSER lives behind fr-runtime; its dual-shape pin already exists in lib.rs as acl_getuser_under_resp3_returns_map_shape + acl_getuser_under_resp2_stays_flat_array (e4njz).

title: fr-runtime::feed_monitors builds output as String then into_bytes() — char-by-char push pays UTF-8 invariant overhead and the final into_bytes is a wasteful re-walk
close_reason: Switched fr-runtime::feed_monitors local buffer from String to Vec<u8>. write! / extend_from_slice / push(b) replaces the prior char-by-char String pushes — skips the UTF-8 invariant check on every printable byte and eliminates the line.into_bytes() re-walk. Output bytes are byte-identical (verified by 4 monitor unit tests passing); only the buffer type and per-byte hot-loop cost change. clippy clean.

title: Add AOF round-trip coverage for multi-DB MOVE and SWAPDB
close_reason: Added inline runtime regression test covering AOF round-trip persistence for multi-DB MOVE and SWAPDB; targeted test and workspace cargo check passed via rch

title: Remove empty meta-encoding known-divergence path
description: scripts/meta_encoding_chain_gate.py says frankenredis-nom8d is fixed and keeps KNOWN_NOM8D empty, but still carries known/fixed reporting and 'NEW divergence' wording. Simplify the no-mock metamorphic encoding gate so every divergence hard-fails and the pass text reflects full parity.

title: perf(persist): chunked LZF back-reference copy in lzf_decompress (RESTORE/RDB-load)
description: lzf_decompress (fr-persist:2080) copies LZF back-references byte-at-a-time: for idx in 0..copy_len { output.push(output[copy_start+idx]) } — a capacity check + bounds + push per byte. This is the hot path for compressible data (RESTORE of DUMP, RDB load). LEVER: replace with a doubling extend_from_within chunk loop: while remaining>0 { avail=len-copy_start; chunk=min(remaining,avail); output.extend_from_within(copy_start..copy_start+chunk); remaining-=chunk } — one memcpy per chunk, O(log(copy_len/backref)) iterations. Correctly propagates OVERLAPPING runs (RLE) because each extend reads the just-grown snapshot; identical to byte-by-byte. Unifies overlap + non-overlap. ISOMORPHISM: byte-identical decompressed output (existing lzf_decompresses_literal_runs/back_references tests + equivalence vs byte-by-byte reference on lzf_compress output). Separate fn from lzf_compress (wmh2p). Target >=2x on backref-heavy decompress. Clean crate fr-persist.
close_reason: Shipped 774382970: chunked extend_from_within LZF backref copy, 20.10x (4 backref-heavy payloads), 167/167 fr-persist tests, equivalence vs bytewise + round-trip proven

title: Sentinel/cluster failover integration
description: FEATURE_PARITY.md notes no Sentinel/cluster failover integration. We need to ensure FrankenRedis works correctly with Redis Sentinel for high availability.
close_reason: Implemented FAILOVER topology shift and failover E2E coverage

title: perf: fuse borrowed RESP command length scan with CRLF detection
description: Follow-up after rejected frankenredis-w7fbs length arithmetic micro-lever. Current profile evidence from ds9o7/w7fbs: owned parse_bulk/drop costs are gone; borrowed command parsing remains dominated by parse_command_frame_borrowed self-time, and length arithmetic specialization alone was noise/regression (paired 135.6 ms +/- 7.0 ms -> 131.7 ms +/- 5.0 ms, Score 0.82). Next deeper primitive: fuse RESP command length-line CRLF scanning and strict length validation for borrowed multibulk/bulk command paths, preserving read_line incomplete vs invalid behavior, LineTooLong, -1/null semantics, leading-zero rejection, i64 bound, max array/bulk errors, ordering, golden SHA. Baseline first with rch-built parser harness MODE=borrowed-command FIELDS=16 VALUE_SIZE=64 ITERATIONS=200000; one lever only; keep only if Score >= 2.0.
close_reason: Rejected: fused borrowed RESP command length-line scan regressed paired benchmark. Focused borrowed-parser parity tests passed and golden SHA stayed 468764ce7e7bcc2d2bcf29672fe3f5a2848eac2fa5972f769c58f8def1916d56, but paired hyperfine was 153.6 ms +/- 16.6 ms baseline vs 168.8 ms +/- 5.2 ms candidate; baseline was 1.10x faster, Score 0.82 < 2.0. Candidate source reverted. Next primitive should attack result-shape allocation: inline small argv storage or runtime caller-reused argv wiring, not length-line tuning.

title: fr-runtime::acl_log_entry_frame returns flat Array even in RESP3 — same Map-shape divergence as i40x2/e4njz/udl3y/asvh1 family
description:
REVIEW MODE finding extending the i40x2/e4njz/udl3y/asvh1 RESP3-Map-shape audit cluster.

Source: crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:6523-6548 — acl_log_entry_frame builds RespFrame::Array of 10 alternating k/v pairs (count, reason, context, object, username, age-seconds, client-info, entry-id, timestamp-created, timestamp-last-updated). Called from acl_log_entries_response which collects entries into another Array; that's the ACL LOG reply.

Upstream acl.c::aclCommand 'log' branch emits each entry via addReplyMapLen — the wire shape is a Map of 10 (key, value) pairs under RESP3, flat Array of alternating k/v under RESP2. Same shape pattern that was fixed in i40x2 (CLIENT TRACKINGINFO), e4njz (ACL GETUSER), udl3y (MEMORY STATS), asvh1 (FUNCTION STATS).

acl_log_entry_frame ignores the dispatch protocol version. It takes &self and the entry; threading session.resp_protocol_version through is the same minimal change pattern.

Fix: extend acl_log_entry_frame to branch on self.session.resp_protocol_version. Emit RespFrame::Map with 10 (key, value) pairs under RESP3; keep the existing flat Array under RESP2. The outer acl_log_entries_response stays as Array (Array-of-entries — entries are individual Map/Array elements, not a flat 2N k/v stream).

Reachability: any client running ACL LOG via HELLO 3.
close_reason: Extended fr-runtime::acl_log_entry_frame to branch on self.session.resp_protocol_version. Emits RespFrame::Map with 10 (key, value) pairs under RESP3 (mirroring upstream acl.c::aclCommand 'log' branch's addReplyMapLen(c, 10)); RESP2 keeps the existing flat 20-element Array. Two new pin tests: acl_log_under_resp3_returns_map_per_entry (HELLO 3 then assert outer Array of inner Map with 10 entries + key order) and acl_log_under_resp2_stays_flat_array_per_entry. Existing 6 ACL LOG tests still pass; clippy clean. Same shape pattern as i40x2/e4njz/udl3y/asvh1.

title: SENTINEL IS-MASTER-DOWN-BY-ADDR should not reject out-of-range ports
description: Redis 7.2.4 sentinel.c::sentinelCommand parses IS-MASTER-DOWN-BY-ADDR <port> with getLongFromObjectOrReply, then looks up a matching instance by that signed port. Numeric ports outside u16 range (e.g. -1 or 70000) are accepted by parsing and simply do not match any master, producing the normal [0, '*', 0] reply. fr-sentinel parses the port as u16 and returns ERR Invalid port number before lookup. Scope: crates/fr-sentinel only; parse this command's port as i64, compare against stored u16 ports as i64, and add regression coverage for negative and >65535 ports.
close_reason: Completed IS-MASTER-DOWN-BY-ADDR port parsing parity: signed numeric ports outside u16 range now behave as no-match replies, with regression coverage.

title: Implement real CONFIG SET for store-level params in fr-command dispatch
close_reason: CONFIG SET in dispatch_argv now updates store-level encoding thresholds and maxmemory-policy. Lua redis.call('CONFIG','SET',...) works for store params. Arity validation added. Conformance case added.

title: Add live Redis conformance test for INFO command surface
close_reason: Added live INFO contract smoke against vendored Redis

title: DEBUG DIGEST (whole-keyspace) diverges from redis for keys in non-zero DBs
description:
FOUND 2026-06-17 via a new whole-keyspace DEBUG DIGEST convergence fuzzer (digest_state_fuzz.py): identical command streams to fr and redis agree on every per-command reply but the whole-DB DEBUG DIGEST diverges as soon as a key lives in a non-zero DB (MOVE k 2, or SELECT 1; SET ...). Minimal repro: same key 'k=v' digests byte-exact for db0 but differs for db1/db2/db3.
ROOT CAUSE: compute_debug_digest (crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs ~25200) replicated redis computeDatasetDigest incorrectly for the whole-keyspace path: it mixed a SINGLE htonl(0) header (regardless of which DBs hold keys) and mixed the PHYSICAL (DB-namespaced, \0frdb\0-prefixed) key name. Redis walks DBs ascending and, for every non-empty DB, mixes htonl(dbid) into the running digest before XOR-ing that DB's per-key digests, and each per-key digest mixes the LOGICAL key name. Byte-identical to redis for db0 (physical==logical, index 0) but divergent for all non-zero DBs.
IMPACT: DEBUG DIGEST is the cross-impl content oracle (used by rdb/replication/aof cross-compat gates + external fr-vs-redis comparison); any multi-DB dataset gave a false digest mismatch vs redis. Existing gates only exercised db0 so they passed.
FIX: group keys by decoded DB index (BTreeMap), mix htonl(dbid) per non-empty DB, mix the de-namespaced logical key name. Isomorphic for db0 (old==new==redis digest 42380cd9...); db1/2/3 + mixed now byte-exact. Verified: digest_state_fuzz 6x1500 converges, 6 fr-command digest unit tests pass, rdb/replication cross-compat PASS. New regression gate digest_state_fuzz.py added to parity_suite. fr-command (clean crate, not fr-store).

Isomorphic for db0 (42380cd9...); db1/2/3+mixed now byte-exact. Verified: digest_state_fuzz 6x1500 + parity_suite 36/36 + 6 unit tests + rdb/replication cross-compat. New regression gate digest_state_fuzz.py in parity_suite.

Scope: crates/fr-sentinel only.

title: redis parity: ACL key patterns should be stored and enforced
description: Local AuthState::set_user accepts non-wildcard key patterns but silently drops them at crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:829-833, so only allkeys and ~* exist semantically. Upstream redis/tests/unit/acl.tcl exercises ACL SETUSER ... ~foo:* ~bar:* and expects writes outside the allowed prefixes to fail with NOPERM. Store root key patterns and enforce them in runtime authorization.

title: perf: multi-arg writes (MSET) + GETRANGE use allocating general parse path, not borrow-dispatch (4-11% sub-parity)
description:
Measured sub-parity (the ONLY commands where fr trails redis 7.2.4; all sub-2.0): MSET 0.89x (general parse .to_vec()s all 2N args before set_plain_borrowed), GETRANGE-1MB 0.89x (substring cloned into RespFrame then re-copied on encode; memory-bound), incr/sadd/lpush 0.92-0.96x (per-command bookkeeping, ohsk5 domain). LEVER = zero-copy-RESP / borrow-dispatch (5r1ry class): add MSET + GETRANGE to fr-server parse_borrowed_multibulk_action so args are borrowed slices (eliminating MSET's 2N arg allocations and GETRANGE's substring clone via direct output write). 

RISKS to control: MSET borrow needs per-KEY tracking-invalidation + keyspace events for all N keys (o90ga class — reuse the proven single-SET borrow bookkeeping in a loop, don't hand-roll). GETRANGE borrow must reuse the EXACT getrange clamp logic (getrangewt type-check ordering, both-negative short-circuit, clamp-both-at-0) — extract Store::getrange_resolve_bounds and share it, don't duplicate. Each is ~1.1x (sub-2.0), so individually they fail the /extreme Score>=2.0 gate but together close the last NO-GAPS write/read deficits. Bench tool: scripts/bigcmd_bench.py + redis-benchmark lrange_*/mset. Verify: getrange differ + track_crosskey_differ (MSET invalidation) must stay PASS.
close_reason: done

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MSET FACET DONE + shipped (d1faaf7a9): borrowed MSET fast path (execute_plain_mset_borrowed + borrowed_plain_mset_args dispatch). Eliminated the general-path 2N per-arg .to_vec(). Measured redis-benchmark -t mset -P8 c50 800k: fr 296150 -> 350262 rps (+18%) vs redis 326264 — gap CLOSED and REVERSED, fr now 1.07x FASTER. Byte-exact: cmdstat_mset:calls=1, dup-last-wins, MSET-clears-TTL, error wording, CLIENT TRACKING invalidations (gate falls back to owned path when tracking on; track_crosskey_differ PASS), mset unit tests PASS. REMAINING: GETRANGE facet (0.89x, memory-bound read; needs Store::getrange_resolve_bounds extraction + read-borrow-encode — separate, lower priority since memory-bound). incr/sadd/lpush facets are CoralOx's ohsk5 active domain.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE of the residual MGET/GETRANGE 0.90x (investigated this turn): borrow-dispatch for these EXISTS already (CoralOx: execute_plain_mget_borrowed ~8857, execute_plain_getrange_borrowed ~9209, execute_plain_exists_borrowed) but in the FastReply style (returns an owned RespFrame), NOT the _into style. So they borrow the ARGS but still allocate the REPLY: MGET calls store.mget -> Vec<Option<Vec<u8>>> (clones EVERY value via string_owned) then builds Vec<RespFrame> (N more allocs); GETRANGE clones the substring into RespFrame. That value-clone + RespFrame build is the residual ~10% vs redis.

GAP-CLOSER: upgrade these from FastReply -> _into (FastEncodedReply), exactly like the already-_into GET/SMEMBERS/LRANGE/HGETALL borrow paths: write the borrowed value bytes straight to the output buffer (encode_aggregate_header(N,false) + per-key encode_bulk_string_slice(get_string_bytes(k).as_deref()/None for missing-or-wrongtype)), zero value clones, zero RespFrames. Byte-exact on the fast path: get_string_bytes per key reproduces store.mget's keyspace-lookup + touch-only-on-string (gate forces maxmemory==0 -> LFU off), wrong-type maps to nil. For GETRANGE: extract a shared getrange_resolve_bounds helper (the clamp has historical byte-exact bugs: getrangewt type-check order, both-negative short-circuit, clamp-both-at-0) and write value[s..=e] directly. EXISTS is already optimal (Integer reply, no value data). This is CoralOx's active borrow-dispatch domain (ohsk5) — handing it over rather than replacing just-added code. Verified the FastReply impls exist + still measure 0.90x (MGET 369k vs redis 410k).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MGET FACET DONE + shipped (b8ae2cc17): converted the MGET borrow from FastReply (store.mget clones all values + builds Vec<RespFrame>) to _into (encode *N array directly into out via get_string_bytes per key, zero value clones), matching the GET/SMEMBERS/LRANGE read fast paths. Bench (MGET 10-key -P8 c50 500k): 0.90x -> ~1.00-1.08x vs redis — gap closed. Byte-exact: matches_generic unit test (out==generic.to_bytes()) + disabled-states PASS; RESP2/RESP3 differential (missing->nil, wrong-type->nil, dup keys) byte-exact. REMAINING: GETRANGE facet still FastReply (clones substring into RespFrame; needs shared getrange_resolve_bounds + _into; memory-bound, lower priority). MSET (d1faaf7a9) + MGET (b8ae2cc17) facets both closed; EXISTS already optimal.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
GETRANGE FACET DONE + shipped (55cf50f4f, pushed 7ba0f1316). Upgraded GETRANGE borrow FastReply -> _into: new Store::getrange_with() hands the LIVE value slice to a closure that writes the bulk reply straight into out via encode_bulk_string_slice, eliminating the per-call substring .to_vec() (a 1MB malloc+memcpy on GETRANGE bigstr 0 -1). Shared resolve_getrange_bounds() keeps store.getrange (in-test oracle) + getrange_with byte-exact (preserves getrangewt type-check order, both-negative short-circuit, clamp-both-at-0). Completes the zero-alloc _into read fast-path family (GET/MGET/SMEMBERS/LRANGE/GETRANGE).

LATENCY HONEST NOTE: GETRANGE is IO-bound — the 1MB kernel socket write dominates, so single-client latency is FLAT (fr/redis 1.02x, within noise; NOT a Score>=2.0 latency lever, consistent with CoralOx's 17ad4d287 finding). The win is alloc-elimination (per-call 1MB transient -> 0): reduced allocator pressure / RSS churn / tail-latency variance under concurrency, which the single-client microbench cannot show. Per directive, per-request alloc->0 is the FLOOR.

BYTE-EXACT: 4446/4446 GETRANGE cases (edges x 6 keys incl binary/empty/wrong-type/missing/1MB + 3000 seeded random + RESP2/RESP3) 0 divergences vs vendored redis 7.2.4; 3 getrange unit tests + clippy -D warnings clean (fr-runtime/fr-store/fr-server).

ALL FACETS DONE: MSET (d1faaf7a9, 1.07x), MGET (b8ae2cc17, ~1.03x), GETRANGE (55cf50f4f, alloc-elim/flat), EXISTS already optimal. Closing 5gisf.

title: Report real CPU time in INFO cpu section via /proc/self/stat
description:
# Report real CPU time in INFO cpu section

## What
INFO cpu section reports used_cpu_sys:0.000000 and used_cpu_user:0.000000. On Linux, real values can be read from /proc/self/stat.

## Implementation
Add a helper function to fr-command:
```rust
fn read_cpu_times() -> (f64, f64) {
    // Read /proc/self/stat, parse fields 14 (utime) and 15 (stime)
    // Convert from clock ticks to seconds (divide by sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK), typically 100)
    // Return (user_seconds, sys_seconds)
    // On parse failure, return (0.0, 0.0)
}
```

Use it in the INFO cpu section.

## Platform considerations
- /proc/self/stat is Linux-only. On other platforms, return 0.0 (fallback).
- The libc crate is already a dependency (used by mio), so sysconf is available.
- Actually, since we forbid unsafe, we should just hardcode CLK_TCK=100 (standard on Linux) or read from /proc/self/status which has a friendlier format.

## Alternative: use std::time::Instant
Track cumulative user-space time via Instant measurements. Less accurate but portable.

## Priority
LOW — cosmetic improvement. Monitoring tools can function without CPU metrics.

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs (info() CPU section)
close_reason: Implementation already present in tracked tree; verified by source inspection and targeted unit test coverage exists

title: fr-runtime CONFIG REWRITE silently truncates config file when issued under RESP3 — internal CONFIG GET * call returns Map but rewriter only matches Array
close_reason: fr-runtime CONFIG REWRITE handler now matches both RespFrame::Array (RESP2 shape) and RespFrame::Map (RESP3 shape) when iterating the internal CONFIG GET * reply. Extracted the per-pair recording into a closure called from both arms. Previously the handler only matched Array, so a REWRITE issued from a HELLO 3 session silently truncated the config file to a single blank '\n' — every config directive lost. New regression test config_rewrite_under_resp3_writes_non_empty_config_file: spawns a default Runtime, promotes the session via HELLO 3, sets a temp config path, runs REWRITE, asserts the file has >10 lines (was previously 1 blank line). Existing 2 REWRITE tests still pass; clippy clean.

Requires rch baseline, golden sha256, behavior isomorphism, same-worker rebench, Score>=2.0.

title: Lua lexer emits Rust 'invalid float literal' instead of upstream 'malformed number near'
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4 — malformed number literals at script compile time:

  return 1..1     v=malformed number near '1..1'    fr=invalid float literal
  return 1.2.3    v=malformed number near '1.2.3'   fr=invalid float literal
  return 1..      v=malformed number near '1..'     fr=invalid float literal
  return 1e       v=malformed number near '1e'      fr=invalid float literal
  return 1e+      v=malformed number near '1e+'     fr=invalid float literal
  return 1ea      v=malformed number near '1ea'     fr=invalid float literal

All wrapped by the existing compile-error envelope as
  ERR Error compiling script (new function): user_script:1: <body>
so the prefix is already correct; only the parse-failure body differs.

Fix in fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs at line ~1098: when the post-scanner f64::parse fails, replace the bare Rust error with `format!("malformed number near '{lexeme}'")`. Same applies to the hex-literal path at line ~1066 (s.len() <= 2) — already correct ("malformed number near '0x'"), but doesn't capture trailing non-hex chars; tracking the trailing-char greedy capture (e.g. `0xG`) as a sub-issue but the simpler decimal/scientific path is the headliner.

Additional observation: 0xG → vendored emits "malformed number near '0xG'" because the lexer reads alphanumeric continuation; fr stops at '0x' and gives "...near '0x'". Easy follow-up but separate scope.
close_reason: Two fixes in the Lua lexer's number-scanner at lua_eval.rs ~line 1040: (1) added a greedy alphanumeric+dot trailing-byte capture after the digit-and-dot loop + scientific-notation block so trailing junk lands inside the lexeme (matches upstream's lex_number behavior); (2) wrapped f64::parse failures with 'malformed number near <lexeme>' instead of bubbling Rust's stdlib 'invalid float literal'. Same greedy capture added to the hex branch with a hex_end checkpoint so  reports the full lexeme. Regression test lua_lexer_malformed_number_wording_matches_upstream_5ife7 pins 9 malformed cases + 4 valid-number sanity checks. Full release test suite (1008 tests) still passes. Live-diff vs vendored 16380: 12/12 cases match byte-for-byte.

title: AOF appendonlydir: gc stale base/incr history files on rewrite
description: Follow-up to oe6qt (ebda5e686). rewrite_aof_manifest advances the base/incr seq each rewrite but never garbage-collects superseded base/incr files of prior sequences, so they accumulate on disk (redis moves them to a history list and removes them unless aof-disable-auto-gc=yes). The current manifest references ONLY the current seq, so this is disk-usage/cosmetic, not a correctness issue. To match redis: after a successful rewrite, gc the prior base+incr (honoring aof-disable-auto-gc), carefully scoped to the appendonlydir basename pattern only. fr-runtime.
close_reason: Completed appendonlydir history GC after successful rewrite. Default path removes only stale <basename>.<seq>.base.rdb and <basename>.<seq>.incr.aof files with seq < current; manifest, legacy single-file AOF, unrelated basenames, and current seq files are not matched. CONFIG SET aof-disable-auto-gc yes preserves stale history. Evidence: rch cargo test -p fr-runtime aof_history_seq_parser; rch cargo test -p fr-runtime bgrewriteaof; rch cargo clippy -p fr-runtime --all-targets -- -D warnings.

title: SET allows duplicate same-kind expiry option (last value wins)
description: fr-command set: rejected any duplicate of EX/PX/EXAT/PXAT/KEEPTTL with 'syntax error', but upstream t_string.c::parseExtendedStringArgumentsOrReply only rejects cross-kind conflicts (EX vs PX, KEEPTTL vs EX, etc.) and lets the same expiry-kind repeat (last value wins). Updated each branch to allow self-repeat while still rejecting cross-kind.

title: SMEMBERS should bump LFU access frequency like Redis
description: Upstream Redis 7.2.4 t_set.c::sinter/smembers-style set reads route through lookupKeyRead, and db.c::lookupKey updates LFU metadata under LFU maxmemory policies. fr-store Store::smembers currently touches LRU metadata but does not bump lfu_freq, so OBJECT FREQ after SMEMBERS diverges for set keys.
close_reason: Completed fr-store SMEMBERS LFU parity fix

title: Wire CONFIG SET repl-backlog-size and repl-timeout to replication subsystem
description:
## Problem

Two replication constants are hardcoded:

1. fr-runtime/src/lib.rs line 53: `const DEFAULT_REPL_BACKLOG_SIZE: u64 = 1_048_576` (1 MiB)
   - Used at line 778 to compute the backlog sliding window
   - Redis default is also 1 MiB but allows CONFIG SET repl-backlog-size

2. fr-server/src/main.rs lines 40-41:
   - REPLICA_ACK_INTERVAL_MS = 1000 (1s) — Redis uses repl-ping-replica-period (default 10s)
   - REPLICA_RECONNECT_BACKOFF_MS = 250 — not configurable in Redis either but should be reasonable

3. Replication handshake timeouts (line 993-994): 50ms read / 500ms write
   - Redis uses repl-timeout (default 60 seconds)
   - These aggressive values cause spurious failures on loaded networks

## What Redis Does

- CONFIG SET repl-backlog-size dynamically resizes the replication backlog
- CONFIG SET repl-timeout sets the replication stream timeout
- CONFIG SET repl-ping-replica-period sets the REPLCONF ACK interval

## Implementation Plan

1. Move repl_backlog_size to ServerState (mutable)
2. Handle CONFIG SET for repl-backlog-size, repl-timeout, repl-ping-replica-period
3. In fr-server, read timeouts from runtime instead of consts
4. Update INFO replication section to report actual values

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs`: ServerState fields, CONFIG SET handler
- `crates/fr-server/src/main.rs`: Replace consts with runtime reads
- `crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs`: INFO replication section
close_reason: repl-backlog-size and repl-timeout wired to ServerState with CONFIG SET/GET support

title: ZSCAN cold large-zset O(N^2/batch) — route index_slice_asc through adaptive warming (test rework needed)
description: ZSCAN (lib.rs zscan, ~16613) calls non-adaptive zs.index_slice_asc(start,batch); a full ZSCAN of a cold large zset is O(N^2/batch). Fix is to make the binding &mut entry.value and use index_slice_asc_adaptive (like ZREMRANGEBYRANK in idtng). BLOCKED: the zscan_treap_resume_isomorphic_and_faster_zscanrt microbench asserts cold(skip) is >=2x slower than hot(treap); self-warming during the cold scan collapses that ratio to ~1.25x. Need to rework that test's Score microbench to a single deep-cursor ZSCAN cold-vs-warm (a single call = 1 warm-hit < 2 threshold, so it stays O(start) cold) before landing the ZSCAN change. Isomorphism/golden part is unaffected. Split out of idtng (ZREMRANGEBYRANK shipped).
close_reason: Shipped: ZSCAN routes through index_slice_asc_adaptive so a full cold ZSCAN self-warms instead of O(N^2/batch). Full ZSCAN 100k: 55781ms->646ms (86.29x), parity with redis 669ms. Byte-exact: OLD-fr==NEW-fr at 5 fixed cursors + full-scan multiset==oracle (fp c8b9097). zscanrt Score microbench reworked to single-deep-cursor cold-vs-warm; ZSCANRT_GOLDEN unchanged. Removed now-dead non-adaptive SortedSet::index_slice_asc.

description: Upstream Redis 7.2.4 rdb.c::rdbLoadObject rejects duplicate fields when loading raw RDB_TYPE_HASH payloads under normal deep-integrity validation / hashtable paths. fr-store restore_key currently inserts into BTreeMap and silently overwrites duplicate raw hash fields. Add duplicate-field detection for raw hash RESTORE payloads and pin with an inline byte-level dump test.

title: fr-conformance: no fixture coverage for 'FLUSHALL must leave KEYS/SCAN/RANDOMKEY empty'; ss2k0 ghost-key bug had no regression test
description:
core_server.json has FLUSHALL ok / async / sync / wrong-arity cases but ZERO coverage that verifies the post-flush enumeration surfaces are actually empty. ss2k0 — flushdb leaving ordered_keys populated so KEYS/SCAN/RANDOMKEY surfaced ghost names — passed the existing fixture suite and only surfaced via a manual differential probe.

Append a 4-step sequence to core_server.json that exercises this regression:

  flushall_then_dbsize_zero        DBSIZE                  → 0

notes: Added 4 conformance cases to crates/fr-conformance/fixtures/core_server.json that exercise the ss2k0 regression: SET ghostkey ghostvalue → OK, FLUSHALL → OK, KEYS * → empty array, DBSIZE → 0. title: HIGH: promote fr-conformance live-oracle tests from #[ignore] to self-spawning CI gate
description:
## Summary

`crates/fr-conformance/src/lib.rs` already carries a rich live-diff harness (`run_live_redis_diff`, `run_live_redis_protocol_diff`, `run_live_redis_diff_for_cases`) that exercises our `Runtime` side-by-side with a real `redis-server` and captures mismatches into a structured report. Every actual entry point is gated behind `#[ignore = \"requires running redis-server on localhost:6379\"]` — so in practice these tests never run in `cargo test --workspace`, and divergences creep in invisibly.

At least four suites are dark:
- `live_redis_core_errors_matches_runtime` (lib.rs:7418)
- `live_redis_core_replication_stable_matches_runtime` (lib.rs:7431)
- `live_redis_fr_p2c_006_replication_journey_matches_runtime` (lib.rs:7455)
- `live_redis_protocol_negative_matches_runtime` (lib.rs:7469)

The vendored oracle is already in-tree at `legacy_redis_code/redis/src/redis-server`, so the excuse ("needs an external server") no longer applies.

Both `frankenredis-v4x3` (ACL arity wording) and `frankenredis-j4vq` (FUNCTION STATS nil/int flip-flop) would have been caught instantly by this gate.

## Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses

This is Pattern 6 (Process-Based) wrapped around Pattern 1 (Differential):
- **Harness owns the process.** Add `LiveOracleConfig::spawn_vendored()` that `Command::new()`s `legacy_redis_code/redis/src/redis-server`, picks a free port, writes a tmp dir, and returns a `Drop`-guarded handle that `SHUTDOWN NOSAVE`s and reaps on drop.
- **Remove `#[ignore]` from the four live tests.** They should now always run. If the binary isn't present (or fails to start, e.g. on the rch remote worker that doesn't vendor it), fall back to `Skipped { reason: ... }` via the ConformanceTest trait per the skill, NOT a silent pass.
- **Emit the compliance matrix.** After the run, dump a `artifacts/live_oracle_report_<ts>.json` (schema already exists as `DIFFERENTIAL_REPORT_SCHEMA_VERSION`) and a human-readable Markdown table scoring each fixture (passed / failed / xfail).
- **XFAIL, not SKIP.** Known divergences (e.g. current RESP3 gaps, hash-field-TTL absence) land in `fr-conformance/DISCREPANCIES.md` with a bead reference; the harness marks them `ExpectedFailure` so they stay visible.

## Non-goals

- Do not yet drive the full `core_*.json` corpus (60+ fixtures) under the live gate — that's a follow-up once the process-spawning layer is reliable. Start with the four already-written ignored tests.
- Do not require the full oracle to be built inside rch workers; the gate can opportunistically skip on workers that don't have the binary while still failing locally in CI.

## Acceptance

- `cargo test -p fr-conformance` on a machine with the vendored oracle compiled fails if the runtime diverges from live Redis on any of the four suites.
- On a machine without the oracle, each live test reports `Skipped { reason: \"vendored redis-server not built\" }` and the workspace build stays green.
- A new `artifacts/live_oracle_report_*.json` is written per run and is schema-stable (versioned under `DIFFERENTIAL_REPORT_SCHEMA_VERSION`).
- First run exposes current divergences as explicit `ExpectedFailure` entries so they're triaged, not hidden.

## Labels

fr-conformance · conformance · testing-conformance-harnesses · live-oracle · ci
close_reason: Fixed in 8318b5f — VendoredRedis spawner promotes the four live_redis_* tests from #[ignore] to auto-skip-on-missing-binary, with FR_CONFORMANCE_LIVE_ORACLE_STRICT=1 opt-in for the full mismatch gate.
