title: Reject duplicate raw RDB_TYPE_SET RESTORE members
description: Upstream Redis 7.2.4 rdb.c::rdbLoadObject reports corrupt RDB and aborts when raw RDB_TYPE_SET contains duplicate members. fr-store restore_key currently inserts RDB_TYPE_SET members into a BTreeSet and silently collapses duplicates. Add duplicate detection for raw set RESTORE payloads and pin with an inline dump payload test.
close_reason: Already implemented: duplicate check in RDB_TYPE_SET decode + test restore_rejects_duplicate_raw_set_members

title: [perf] Avoid per-command client session clone in tracking invalidation path
description: Pass-5 parser/encoding profile did not show RESP parser/encoder functions in top sampled frames, but GDB stack sampling under SET pipeline=16 did show client_list_sessions / queue_client_tracking_invalidations / BTreeMap clone_subtree among top frames. Investigate preserving client-tracking invalidation ordering and observable CLIENT TRACKING behavior while avoiding a full client/session map clone per write command. Acceptance: fresh profile-backed baseline first; one lever only; golden RESP sha256 unchanged; focused client-tracking/server tests pass via rch; before/after benchmark shows score >=2.0 before keeping.
close_reason: Superseded by more precise profiler-backed bead frankenredis-i2v99; avoiding overlap with active frankenredis-uiwy5 runtime/server work

title: [perf] Reuse borrowed multibulk argv scratch in server hot path
description: Profile-backed by post-cms7p LPUSH P16/C50 profile: strict multibulk parser/dispatch still shows fr_protocol::parse_command_args_borrowed_into (~1.85% self) and parse_borrowed_multibulk_action (~0.70%) while allocator/syscall time remains visible. Current fr-server process_buffered_frames reuses owned argv_scratch, but parse_borrowed_multibulk_action creates a fresh Vec<&[u8]> per command before parse_command_args_borrowed_into clears/reserves it. One lever only: hoist borrowed argv slice scratch to process_buffered_frames and pass it into parse_borrowed_multibulk_action so pipeline commands reuse capacity across the buffered pass. Preserve exact RESP parse errors, null/empty array no-reply behavior, fast-path dispatch order, client pause/blocking semantics, output ordering, RNG/tie/FP. Baseline current main with RCH-built fr-server/fr-bench first; prove raw golden SHA unchanged; keep only Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Rejected: direct single-value keyed-write parser fast path reached only 1.02 +/- 0.06x on paired n1M and 1.03 +/- 0.19x on paired n5M; Score 0.22 < 2.0; source hunk removed, evidence retained.

title: ACL: parse and enforce %R/%W/%RW key selectors (Redis 7.0 feature)
description:
ACL key selectors (`%R~pat`, `%W~pat`, `%RW~pat`) are an ACL 7.0 feature that fr doesn't parse. Vendored Redis 7.2.4 accepts them:
  vendored ACL SETUSER u on '%R~r*'   -> OK
  fr       ACL SETUSER u on '%R~r*'   -> ERR Error in ACL SETUSER modifier '%R~r*': Syntax error

Semantics:
  %R~pat   - read-only access to keys matching pat
  %W~pat   - write-only access to keys matching pat
  %RW~pat  - read+write (same as legacy `~pat`)

Implementation requires:
1. Extend the per-user key_patterns to track (pat, ReadOnly|WriteOnly|Both).
2. Parse the %R/%W/%RW prefix in apply_setuser_rules (fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:1220).
3. Enforce the read/write distinction in the ACL check path using command keyspecs to know which keys a given invocation reads vs writes.
4. Emit the selector flags back in ACL GETUSER output.

Step 3 is the tricky one because it requires per-command keyspec metadata that distinguishes read-only vs write-only key positions. Some commands (LRANGE, GET) are pure-read; some (SET, LPUSH) are pure-write; some (GETSET, INCR) are read+write.

The naive "accept syntax, ignore distinction" path is a security trap: a user expecting %R-only access would silently get write access too. So no shortcut here — must enforce.

Reproduction (probe155.sh):
  ACL SETUSER u8 on '>p1' '~obj*' '%R~r*' '%W~w*'  -> v=OK, f=ERR
  ACL SETUSER u9 on '%R~foo'                       -> v=OK, f=ERR
  ACL SETUSER u11 on '%RW~foo'                     -> v=OK, f=ERR

Not scoped for this iteration — moderately invasive multi-file change.
close_reason: done

title: HSET should bump LFU access frequency for existing hashes
description: Upstream Redis 7.2.4 hsetCommand calls hashTypeLookupWriteOrCreate, which starts with lookupKeyWrite on the hash key. lookupKeyWrite reaches lookupKey without LOOKUP_NOTOUCH, so existing hash keys bump LFU counters under LFU maxmemory policies before mutation. FrankenRedis Store::hset currently mutates or creates the hash and touches write time but does not bump LFU frequency for existing hash keys. Add LFU bumping and focused fr-store coverage for updating an existing hash.
close_reason: Added LFU frequency bump for existing hashes in hset

title: redis parity: BITCOUNT BYTE/BIT ranges
close_reason: Completed

title: DUMP: stream consumer-group payload diverges from redis (stream_cg, pre-existing, separate from bfg6z) — investigate consumer/PEL ordering or field encoding
LLEN should bump LFU access frequency
description: Redis 7.2.4 llenCommand calls lookupKeyReadOrReply without LOOKUP_NOTOUCH, so existing list keys bump LFU counters under LFU maxmemory policies. FrankenRedis Store::llen only calls entry.touch() without bumping LFU frequency. Add LFU bumping consistent with other read commands like HLEN and STRLEN.
close_reason: done

title: redis parity: ACL SETUSER password removals should error when entry is missing
description: Redis ACLSetUser returns ENODEV when ACL SETUSER tries to remove a plaintext or hashed password that is not present, but local AuthState::set_user in crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:806-822 just retain-filters silently. Match the Redis error surface for <password and !hash removal of missing passwords and add regression coverage.
close_reason: Completed

title: fr-command: XINFO STREAM key-lookup ordering + arg-validation wording divergences
description:
Three divergences in XINFO STREAM vs vendored redis 7.2.4 t_stream.c::xinfoCommand:

1. Key-lookup ordering: upstream looks up the key BEFORE dispatching to the STREAM/FULL branch, so 'XINFO STREAM nokey FULL extra' replies 'no such key'. fr validated args first, leaking 'wrong number of arguments' for missing keys.

2. Arg-validation wording: when the key exists but the FULL [COUNT] tail is malformed, upstream's xinfoReplyWithStreamInfo calls addReplySubcommandSyntaxError producing 'unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments for STREAM. Try XINFO HELP.'. fr emitted 'wrong number of arguments for xinfo|stream command'.

3. Negative COUNT: upstream silently treats a negative COUNT as the default 10 (count<0 → count=10). fr returned an InvalidInteger error.

Fix:
- Move xinfo_stream() lookup ahead of full-mode arg parsing so missing key short-circuits.
- Replace WrongSubcommandArity emissions in the STREAM branch with a closure that builds the addReplySubcommandSyntaxError-style wording.
- Default negative COUNT to 10.

title: perf(fr-store): O(1) SPOP on hashtable-encoded sets (was O(n) shift)
description: SPOP on a large (hashtable-encoded) set was catastrophically slow: redis 38461 rps vs fr 868 rps on a 100k-element set (~44x slower). Root cause: fr's Generic set Hash variant is an order-preserving IndexSet, and spop did iter().nth(idx) (O(idx)) + shift_remove (O(n) shift + hash-index fixup) per popped element; redis's set is an unordered dict (O(1) delete). Fix: SetValue::pop_index / GenericSet::pop_index remove by position — Hash uses IndexSet::swap_remove_index (O(1); a hashtable-encoded set's order is already unspecified, like redis's dict), Packed (listpack) keeps the order-preserving remove to match redis's ordered listpack delete; intset uses Vec::remove by index (no bytes<->i64 round-trip/binary-search). Measured SPOP si 3 on 100k set: 868 -> 44776 rps (51x), now 1.16x faster than redis. Parity: SPOP invariants (distinct/membership/cardinality) hold vs redis; small listpack-set order preserved; 585 fr-store + fr-command spop tests + random_command_differ (0 div) + clippy clean.
close_reason: Shipped: SPOP O(n)->O(1) on hashtable sets via swap_remove_index; 868->44776 rps (51x), now 1.16x>redis (was 44x slower); parity invariants + differ + 585 tests + clippy clean

title: Add server-wide counters for INFO stats (total_commands, total_connections)
close_reason: Added stat_total_commands_processed/stat_total_connections_received/stat_connected_clients to Store, incremented from Runtime and Server, reported in INFO clients and stats sections

title: Multi-connection differential misses CLIENT LIST TYPE slave alias
description: CLIENT LIST TYPE slave is Redis's legacy alias for replica and should return the replica-filtered list, not an unknown-type error. Existing coverage pins CLIENT KILL TYPE slave but the live multi-connection probe does not cover CLIENT LIST TYPE slave. Add a deterministic no-replica scenario to scripts/multi_conn_differential_probe.py.

title: perf: ZRANGEBYLEX single-score range-pruning on the ordered zset — 2795x for selective lex window
description: zrangebylex / zrangebylex_withscores did iter_asc().filter(lex_in_range) = O(N) over the whole zset. For the canonical BYLEX use (all members share one score = a lex index), the BTreeMap<ScoreMember> is lex-ordered within that score band, so SortedSet::lex_range_asc range-scans actual(s,min)..=actual(s,max) + lex_in_range filter for exact [/(  inclusivity = O(log N + k). Falls back to exact iter+filter for multi-score or Packed (preserves vgkly unequal-score behavior). 2795x on selective BYLEX over 100k single-score (2.84s->1.0ms). Byte-identical (600 random single/multi-score Packed/Full x 6 bounds + 11 edge -/+/[x/(x vs brute ref, golden 0x30977947f4925323); 575 fr-store tests pass. Graveyard: ordered-structure range-pruning. SEE ALSO 2wgom (KEYS prefix-prune).
close_reason: Shipped ed5e321f5: SortedSet::lex_range_asc range-scans the single-score band (BTreeMap range + lex_in_range filter) instead of O(N) iter+filter; multi-score/Packed fall back exactly. 2795x on selective BYLEX over 100k single-score. Byte-identical (600 random + 11 edge vs brute ref, golden 0x30977947f4925323); 575 fr-store tests pass.

title: perf: pipelined dispatch hot-path overheads (release-perf flamegraph, -P16)
description:
Release-perf flamegraph (fr --mode strict, redis-benchmark -P16 get,set, 6s perf record). NON-pipelined SET/GET: fr BEATS oracle (55.9k vs 51.7k SET; 53.7k vs 46.7k GET). PIPELINED -P16: fr ~37-44% of oracle (294k vs 787k SET; 340k vs 765k GET) = the real gap. Per-command accidental overheads in the dispatch loop (self-time %):

- queue_client_tracking_invalidations 3.05% — iterates ALL client_sessions per WRITE even when no client has tracking on. Needs a guard: maintain an AUTHORITATIVE bcast-client registry (BTreeSet<u64>, the single CLIENT TRACKING ON BCAST site is the only correctness-critical add; removes at OFF/RESET/disconnect are safe-if-missed because the existence check filters dead ids), iterate that instead of all sessions; non-bcast path already cheap when client_tracking_observed_keys empty.
- core::fmt::write + String::write_str + pad_integral ~4.5% + clock_gettime per cmd — slowlog timing: two clock_gettime + (on slow path) get_command_flags(...).split_whitespace() per command. Cache the per-batch time; precompute a 'fast' bool in the command table instead of split_whitespace on a flags STRING.
- command_key_indexes 1.83% + command_key_references 1.28% + check_command_arity 1.27% — per-command metadata re-derivation via string ops. (db-0 namespacing call ELIMINATED in 2e3b17d56.)
- ClientSession::clone 1.02% — per-command session clone in dispatch_with_client_context; investigate borrowing instead.

Each is small alone; collectively they are the -P16 gap. Attack the tracking-scan guard and the slowlog-timing/fast-flag levers first (highest, cleanest). MUST re-baseline on a QUIET host (loadavg was 135/64cores this firing = vibu6) before claiming ratios.
close_reason: Closed as drained tracker parent: yaxr7.1 slowlog pre-gate was rejected with paired benchmark evidence; yaxr7.2 BCAST tracking registry shipped with Score 2.4; yaxr7.3 session registry clone removal was rejected; the disjoint fr-command O(1) command table lookup shipped in ed3b783f0; yaxr7.4 peer-address cache shipped in e98a457f6 and was reverified by focused RCH fr-runtime test. Remaining P16 gap is tracked by ohsk5/new follow-ups rather than this parent.

title: HSTRLEN should bump LFU access frequency for existing hashes
description: Upstream Redis 7.2.4 hstrlenCommand calls lookupKeyReadOrReply before reading a hash field length. That lookup reaches lookupKey without LOOKUP_NOTOUCH, so existing hash keys bump LFU counters under LFU maxmemory policies even when the requested field is missing. FrankenRedis Store::hstrlen returns the field length and touches LRU time but does not bump LFU frequency. Add LFU bumping and focused fr-store coverage for an existing hash.
close_reason: Completed fr-store HSTRLEN LFU parity fix

title: Blocking pops / LMPOP / ZMPOP / GETDEL propagated verbatim instead of resolved deterministic form
description: Upstream rewrites these to a concrete deterministic command before AOF/replication propagation: BLPOP/BRPOP -> LPOP/RPOP <served-key>; BLMOVE/BRPOPLPUSH -> LMOVE/RPOPLPUSH; LMPOP/BLMPOP -> LPOP/RPOP <served-key> <count>; ZMPOP/BZMPOP -> ZPOPMIN/ZPOPMAX <served-key> <count>; BZPOPMIN/BZPOPMAX -> ZPOPMIN/ZPOPMAX <served-key>; GETDEL -> DEL/UNLINK <key>. fr propagates them VERBATIM (confirmed via fr master->replica MONITOR: replica receives 'BLPOP l 0', 'LMPOP 2 l l2 LEFT COUNT 2', 'ZMPOP 1 z MIN COUNT 2', 'GETDEL sd'). NOT a corruption bug today: stream ordering means the data is present when the replica/AOF applies the (otherwise blocking) command, so it pops the same element and the dataset stays byte-consistent (verified: l/z/sd identical on master+replica, replica blocked_clients=0, link up). It IS a parity gap (fr AOF/stream != redis) and removes the latent risk of a replica applying a blocking command. Fix: extend the propagation arm (fr-runtime, same place as 1uiql/f9byo) with reply-driven rewrites — the served key/elements come from the command reply (BLPOP reply=[key,val]; LMPOP reply=[key,[elems]]; ZMPOP reply=[key,[member,score...]]). Same class as 1uiql/f9byo.
close_reason: Fixed + shipped (commit 134f814af). Blocking pops (BLPOP/BRPOP/BZPOPMIN/BZPOPMAX/BRPOPLPUSH/BLMOVE/BLMPOP/BZMPOP), non-blocking *MPOP (LMPOP/ZMPOP), and GETDEL now propagate as the concrete deterministic command (LPOP/RPOP/ZPOPMIN/ZPOPMAX/RPOPLPUSH/LMOVE/DEL with served key + count from the reply). Verified byte-exact vs the oracle replication stream end-to-end (all 8 forms match). Unit test blocking_and_mpop_and_getdel_rewritten_for_propagation; fr-runtime 543 pass.

title: Implement lexical scoping / upvalue capture in Lua evaluator
description:
## Problem

fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs line 2274: When calling a LuaValue::Function, a completely fresh environment is created (Env::new()). This means closures cannot access variables from their defining scope.

Example that breaks:
  local x = 10
  local f = function() return x end
  return f()  -- returns nil, should return 10

This is a FUNDAMENTAL semantic bug that silently breaks any Lua code using closures, callbacks, or higher-order functions.

## How Real Lua Does It

Lua 5.1 uses upvalue capture: when a function is created, it captures references to local variables in enclosing scopes. These captured variables (upvalues) travel with the function closure.

## Implementation Options

### Option A: Environment Chain (simpler)
When creating a LuaValue::Function, snapshot the current environment as a parent chain. When the function is called, create a new env with the captured parent as the lookup fallback.

Data model change:
- LuaValue::Function currently stores (name, body_statements)
- Change to store (name, body_statements, captured_env: Option<Env>)
- In Env::get(), if local lookup fails, walk the captured_env chain

### Option B: Explicit Upvalue Tracking (more correct)
Track which variables are referenced by the function body. Only capture those specific bindings. More memory-efficient but harder to implement.

## Recommendation

Option A is much simpler and sufficient for Redis Lua scripts (which are typically not deeply nested). The performance cost is minimal since Env is just a HashMap.

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs: LuaValue::Function variant, Env struct, call_function, function definition handling

## Testing

- Test: local variable access from closure
- Test: nested closures (closure inside closure)
- Test: closure over loop variable
- Test: multiple closures sharing same upvalue
- Test: redis.call inside a closure
close_reason: Implemented upvalue capture via captured_env on LuaFunc. Functions now snapshot the lexical scope at definition time and restore it on call, enabling closures.

title: Lua errors inside loadstring chunks use outer 'user_script:1' instead of chunk label
description: When a function returned by loadstring(src, chunkname) errors at runtime (e.g. nonexistent-global access), vendored prefixes with the chunk's own label like '[string "return undef"]:1: ...' or 'chunkname:1: ...'. fr currently uses the outer 'user_script:1: ...' prefix because every runtime error site hardcodes that string. Probe: 'local f=loadstring(\'return undef\'); local ok,err=pcall(f); return err' — vendored '[string "return undef"]:1: ...', fr 'user_script:1: ...'. Fix requires tagging LuaFunc with its source label and threading it through error formatting (the line number is still '1' since fr doesn't track positions in the parser yet). Filed as follow-up to frankenredis-cfflo.
close_reason: Added source_label: Option<String> field on LuaFunc. loadstring/load tag the created LuaFunc with the chunk label (format_lua_chunk_label result). Added LuaState::current_source_label that propagates the active chunk label to inner function definitions: Stmt::FunctionDecl, Stmt::LocalFunctionDecl, and Expr::FunctionDef now inherit current_source_label. In call_function's LuaValue::Function path, push the lua_func's source_label onto current_source_label before exec_stmts and restore on exit; on Err, rewrite the 'user_script:' prefix to the chunk label. Functions outside any loaded chunk retain the default 'user_script:1:' prefix. Verified live diff vs vendored 7.2.4 (port 16380): 10/10 cases match including default chunk label, custom name, =NAME prefix stripping, @NAME prefix stripping, nested function inheritance, and outer-script preservation. Probes covered: undef-global, undef-with-name, arith-error, call-nil-error, concat-error, nested-call-error, name-eq-prefix, name-at-prefix, compile-error-chunk, no-chunk-still-user-script. Pinned by lua_loadstring_runtime_errors_carry_chunk_label_prefix covering 5 sub-cases. All 927 fr-command lib tests pass.

title: Add runtime-populated context fields to Store for INFO accuracy
description:
# Add runtime context fields to Store for cross-layer INFO reporting

## What
Add fields to Store that the runtime/server populates each tick so that fr-command's INFO handler can report accurate values for fields that originate in the runtime layer:
- pub stat_blocked_clients: u64 (number of currently blocked clients)
- pub stat_tracking_clients: u64 (number of tracking clients)
- pub maxmemory_bytes_live: usize (runtime's current maxmemory setting)

## Why a Store field instead of duplicating logic
The INFO command is dispatched through fr-command's dispatch_argv, which only has access to Store. The runtime has the real values but can't easily pass them through dispatch_argv's signature. The cleanest solution is for the server event loop to update these Store fields before each command batch, so INFO reads accurate values.

## Where to populate
- In fr-server/src/main.rs: before processing each client's commands, update store.stat_blocked_clients = blocked_tokens.len()
- In fr-runtime: when maxmemory changes, update store.maxmemory_bytes_live
- tracking_clients can stay 0 until CLIENT TRACKING is fully wired

## Key implementation points
- Add fields to Store struct with pub visibility
- Initialize to 0 in Store::new()
- Server event loop updates them each tick (cheap — just integer assignments)
- INFO handler in fr-command reads them instead of hardcoding 0

## Why this matters
- blocked_clients:0 is wrong when clients are blocked on BLPOP etc.
- maxmemory:0 in INFO is wrong when runtime has set maxmemory
- Monitoring tools use these for capacity planning

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs (Store struct)
- crates/fr-server/src/main.rs (populate fields each tick)
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs (populate maxmemory_bytes_live on CONFIG SET)

title: HLL: PF commands return WRONGTYPE for magic-valid-but-corrupt HLLs; redis uses layered validation (gate WRONGTYPE, PFADD tolerates->1, PFCOUNT->INVALIDOBJ)
notes: PARTIAL FIX shipped 5ce3f7231: PF READ paths (PFCOUNT/PFMERGE/PFDEBUG) on a gate-valid-but-corrupt sparse HLL now return INVALIDOBJ byte-exact vs redis (new StoreError::CorruptedHllValue in fr-store, mapped to 'INVALIDOBJ Corrupted HLL object detected' in fr-command). RESIDUAL (keep open): PFADD on a corrupt sparse HLL returns INVALIDOBJ on fr, but redis returns :1 when the new element's register falls in the still-valid opcode prefix (element-dependent; redis hllSparseSet walks opcodes, sets the register if found before the corruption, only INVALIDOBJ on promote-failure). fr decodes sparse->dense strictly so it can't replicate the partial tolerance without a faithful hllSparseSet/hllSparseSum byte-level port (fr-store hll_decode_sparse_registers ~19723). That port is the remaining work.

PFDEBUG ENCODING now header-only. 0 diffs vs 7.2.4; golden byte-exact b087cfe9 (52650B); regression test + clippy clean. Residual: truncated-prefix PFADD is index-dependent (undefined), not reproduced.

title: fr-command: MEMORY DOCTOR returns short canned message instead of upstream's verbose empty-instance text
description:
MEMORY DOCTOR returns 'Sam, I have no memory problems' (one line) for any state. Upstream Redis 7.2 (object.c::getMemoryDoctorReport) has three branches:
  (a) when total_allocated < 5 MB ('empty' branch) → emits the verbose 'Hi Sam, this instance is empty or is using very little memory, my issues detector can't be used in these conditions. Please, leave for your mission on Earth and fill it with some data. The new Sam and I will be back to our programming as soon as I finished rebooting.\n'
  (b) when num_reports == 0 (no issues, not empty) → 'Hi Sam, I can\'t find any memory issue in your instance. I can only account for what occurs on this base.\n'
  (c) when there are issues → a multi-bullet report with sections.

Acceptance: an idle fr-server with no keys returns the (a) verbose empty-instance message verbatim (matching vendored redis-server 7.2.4 byte-for-byte). When dbsize > 0 (or some heuristic for non-empty) but no detected issues, returns the (b) 'I can\'t find any memory issue' line. The (c) verbose-issues path can stay as a follow-up since fr does not yet track allocator/peak/RSS metrics with enough fidelity to surface them.

Reference: ran 'MEMORY DOCTOR' against fr (port 16700) vs vendored redis (port 16701) on a fresh empty instance. fr: 'Sam, I have no memory problems'. redis: 'Hi Sam, this instance is empty or is using very little memory...'. Caught by ad-hoc differential probe.

Fix touches fr-command/src/lib.rs around the MEMORY DOCTOR branch (~line 13487). The conformance fixture core_server.json has 2 cases (memory_doctor + memory_doctor_duplicate_block) pinning the old short message — those need updating.

title: [perf] Remove PackedList LPUSH front temporary allocation
description: Post-cms7p hz1 LPUSH profile at f818387cc/bd2b2423a still points at Store::lpush/ListValue work after the per-command memory-cache delta was rejected. Profile evidence: Store::lpush 7.27% self, estimate_value_memory_usage_bytes 5.43%, allocator/list-push costs visible; the benchmark keyspace keeps lists in the PackedList regime, not promoted ChunkedList chunks. Graveyard route: packed-layout/allocation primitive, not another cache flag. One lever only: replace PackedList::push_front's temporary encoded Vec plus Vec::splice(0..0, enc) with an in-place front insert that reserves once, shifts the existing packed bytes with copy_within, writes the varint length directly, then copies the element bytes. Preserve packed record bytes, list order, get/iter/set/insert/remove/pop behavior, LPUSH/RPUSH integer replies, OBJECT ENCODING/listpack accounting, MEMORY USAGE, lazy expiry, LFU/touch, RNG/tie/FP. Baseline current main with rch-built fr-server/fr-bench first; prove raw golden SHA unchanged; keep only Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Rejected: PackedList in-place LPUSH front insertion did not clear Score>=2.0. Golden raw output SHA matched (8169647db39fbfe1951fc2291fd7332a77911becbf0427640146b796adb343e1), focused list tests and fr-store check passed, but paired n5M only reached 1.025x mean with overlapping variance, median regressed 8.864s -> 9.199s, and last-run throughput regressed 564270.89 -> 514386.09 ops/sec. Score 0.55. Source hunk removed; evidence in artifacts/optimization/frankenredis-yjedr/. Next route: stop front-insert micro-tuning and attack zero-copy/borrowed list payload storage, batched LPUSH over pipeline slices, or a list-node layout that removes byte shifting entirely.

title: fr-command CLIENT KILL/PAUSE/TRACKING/UNBLOCK place noscript guard after handler-level option/mode/integer validation — scripted bad-arg calls surface parse error instead of upstream's CMD_NOSCRIPT reply
description:
## Symptom
Continuation of the noscript-ordering drift family fixed in 0d3561a/26dc7b0/51077d0. Four CLIENT subcommands have the broken pattern:

- \`CLIENT KILL SKIPME maybe\` inside script → fr returns SyntaxError; upstream returns 'This Redis command is not allowed from script'.
- \`CLIENT PAUSE 1000 WAT\` inside script → fr returns 'CLIENT PAUSE mode must be WRITE or ALL'; upstream returns the noscript reply.
- \`CLIENT TRACKING MAYBE\` inside script → fr returns SyntaxError; upstream returns the noscript reply.
- \`CLIENT TRACKING ON PREFIX foo\` inside script → fr returns 'TRACKING PREFIX requires BCAST'; upstream returns the noscript reply.
- \`CLIENT UNBLOCK abc\` inside script → fr returns InvalidInteger; upstream returns the noscript reply.
- \`CLIENT UNBLOCK 42 WAT\` inside script → fr returns 'CLIENT UNBLOCK reason should be TIMEOUT or ERROR'; upstream returns the noscript reply.

## Differential probe
Upstream commands.def lines 1546-1556 declare CLIENT KILL/PAUSE/TRACKING/UNBLOCK with CMD_NOSCRIPT. server.c::scriptCommand at legacy_redis_code/redis/src/script.c:524-532 evaluates the subcommand-specific arity FIRST and CMD_NOSCRIPT SECOND, both before the handler runs. So inside a script: arity wins over noscript, but noscript wins over every handler-level mode/option/integer error.

fr-command/src/lib.rs has the noscript guard placed AFTER the relevant validation:
- CLIENT KILL (lib.rs:14760-14872): noscript at line 14843 is after the entire option-parsing while loop (lines 14778-14841).
- CLIENT PAUSE (lib.rs:14873-14901): noscript at line 14898 is after timeout parse + mode validation (lines 14884-14897).
- CLIENT TRACKING (lib.rs:14910-14919): noscript at line 14913 is after parse_client_tracking_state which does arity + mode + option-matrix validation.
- CLIENT UNBLOCK (lib.rs:14952-14974): noscript at line 14971 is after parse_i64_arg(argv[2]) + mode validation.

The four existing tests client_kill_pause_and_unpause_reject_scripts_after_validation, client_tracking_rejected_from_scripts_after_validation, and client_unblock_rejected_from_scripts_after_validation explicitly pin the broken ordering.

## Fix
1. CLIENT KILL: move 'if store.script_nesting_level >= 1' guard immediately after the arity check at line 14764, before the option-parsing while loop.
2. CLIENT PAUSE: move the noscript guard immediately after the arity check at line 14883.
3. CLIENT TRACKING: add an inline 'if argv.len() < 3' arity check (mirroring parse_client_tracking_state's first check) and the noscript guard at the top of the TRACKING branch, then call parse_client_tracking_state.
4. CLIENT UNBLOCK: move the noscript guard immediately after the arity check at line 14955.
5. Update the four existing tests to assert noscript wins for every arity-correct scripted call. Rename ..._after_validation → ..._after_arity_check.

## Out of scope
- CLIENT REPLY pre-check at dispatch_argv:1109 has a different shape (pre-check that intercepts before normal dispatch). The mode-validation-before-noscript ordering issue exists there too but the fix shape differs.
close_reason: fr-command CLIENT KILL/PAUSE/TRACKING/UNBLOCK now place script_nesting_level guard immediately after the table-level / subcommand arity check (was after handler-level option / mode / integer validation), mirroring upstream's CMD_NOSCRIPT-after-arity-check ordering. Inside a Lua script, scripted bad-arg calls now surface 'This Redis command is not allowed from script' rather than the handler-level error. Three existing tests pinned the broken ordering and have been renamed (..._after_validation → ..._after_arity_check) and rewritten so every arity-correct scripted call surfaces SCRIPT_NOSCRIPT_ERROR, including: CLIENT KILL SKIPME maybe, CLIENT PAUSE 1000 WAT, CLIENT TRACKING MAYBE, CLIENT TRACKING ON PREFIX foo, CLIENT UNBLOCK abc, CLIENT UNBLOCK 42 WAT. CLIENT REPLY's dispatch-level pre-check shape is left out of scope (different fix shape needed). Sibling of d021m, 7j2cw, yrm7e.

title: string.char + math.max/min hardcode 'user_script:1:' prefix — fail pcall anonymous shape
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4 — same pattern as frankenredis-fllxr but for three more Lua stdlib functions that hardcode the named/prefixed shape:

  pcall(string.char,'x')
    vendored: bad argument #1 to '?' (number expected, got string)
    fr:       user_script:1: bad argument #1 to 'char' (number expected, got string)

  pcall(string.char,300)
    vendored: bad argument #1 to '?' (invalid value)
    fr:       user_script:1: bad argument #1 to 'char' (invalid value)

  pcall(math.max)
    vendored: bad argument #1 to '?' (number expected, got no value)
    fr:       user_script:1: bad argument #1 to 'max' (number expected, got no value)

  pcall(math.min)
    vendored: bad argument #1 to '?' (number expected, got no value)
    fr:       user_script:1: bad argument #1 to 'min' (number expected, got no value)

Three handlers in fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs hardcode the prefix instead of routing through lua_format_argerror:

1. string.char at line ~5984 — both luaL_checkinteger ("number expected") and the range check ("invalid value") branches.
2. math.max at line ~5568 — the args.is_empty() short-circuit.
3. math.min at line ~5593 — same.

All three already accept inv_name in their other paths (e.g., lua_check_number receives current_invocation_name). Replace the hardcoded format! with lua_format_argerror calls.
close_reason: Routed string.char (range + number-expected branches) and math.max/math.min (empty-args branches) through lua_format_argerror. Regression test string_char_and_math_min_max_pcall_shape_drops_prefix_ymb2q pins 5 pcall cases + 3 direct-call regressions. Live-diff vs vendored 16380: 5/5 match. Existing tests math_min_max_wrong_type_arg_wording_a6r5p and string_char_invalid_value_includes_user_script_prefix_uni2j still pass.

title: perf(fr-runtime): defer client-memory aggregate re-sum off the hot path
description: record_client_session ran refresh_client_memory_aggregates on EVERY pipelined batch: an O(live-clients) re-sum plus a per-call HashSet<u64> allocation, purely to feed MEMORY STATS/USAGE + INFO memory clients.normal/slaves (rarely queried). Profile (release-perf, GET/SET P16/C50) attributed ~1.07% CPU to it. Moved to lazy refresh at the actual read sites (handle_info_command when the memory section is requested; the MEMORY command dispatch). O(clients)/batch -> O(1)/batch on the hot path; per-batch HashSet alloc eliminated; scales with connection count. Parity: MEMORY STATS clients.normal byte-identical old=new (18140); 505 fr-runtime tests + random_command_differ + index_range + reload gates all pass.
close_reason: Shipped: lazy refresh at MEMORY/INFO read sites; O(clients)/batch->O(1) + per-batch HashSet alloc removed; profile 1.07% CPU; parity byte-identical (MEMORY STATS 18140 old=new), 505 tests + differ + 2 gates pass.

title: fr-command INFO memory hardcodes used_memory_scripts/used_memory_lua to 0; cached script bodies have real byte sizes
close_reason: fr-command INFO memory now reports real values for used_memory_scripts, used_memory_scripts_human, used_memory_lua, used_memory_lua_human, used_memory_scripts_eval. New Store::scripts_memory_bytes() approximates upstream eval.c::evalScriptsMemory: per-entry sha length (40 hex chars per upstream sdsZmallocSize) + body bytes summed across the script_cache. Dict overhead is implementation-specific and skipped (it tracks the dominant data-bearing term). Both used_memory_lua and used_memory_scripts_eval map to the same upstream lctx.lua_scripts_mem; fr has only one Lua context so all three counters share scripts_bytes. New regression test info_memory_reports_real_used_memory_scripts_and_lua_bytes pins the empty-store baseline (all 0/0B) and the post-SCRIPT-LOAD live state (40 + body.len() bytes for a 'return 1' body = 48). All fr-command lib tests pass; clippy clean. Sixteenth concrete fix this session, second discovered via /mock-code-finder skill (after 8tk8h).

title: cjson.encode accepts >1 arg; vendored requires exactly 1
description:
Vendored Redis 7.2.4's lua-cjson lua_cjson.c::json_encode does luaL_argcheck(L, lua_gettop(L) == 1, 1, ...) and raises:
  bad argument #1 to 'encode' (expected 1 argument)

fr's cjson.encode handler only checks args.is_empty() and silently ignores extra args, returning the encoding of arg #1 alone.

Probe:
  EVAL 'return cjson.encode(1, 2)' 0
    vendored: ERR ... bad argument #1 to 'encode' (expected 1 argument)
    fr:       1

Fix: extend the existing arg-count check in fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs::'cjson.encode' to reject args.len() != 1.

Probe sweep #127.
close_reason: completed

title: CONFIG SET active-defrag-max-scan-fields accepts zero
description: Review finding from recent CONFIG validation work. Upstream Redis 7.2 config.c declares active-defrag-max-scan-fields with lower bound 1, but FrankenRedis accepts CONFIG SET active-defrag-max-scan-fields 0. Repro: run CONFIG SET active-defrag-max-scan-fields 0 against fr-runtime and legacy Redis; fr returns OK while Redis returns CONFIG SET failed with range 1..9223372036854775807. Fix by validating the lower bound and adding conformance coverage.
close_reason: Fixed active-defrag-max-scan-fields CONFIG SET lower bound and added live Redis conformance coverage

title: ZRANGEBYSCORE deep LIMIT offset O(offset) BTreeMap walk — slower than redis skiplist
description: ZRANGEBYSCORE/ZREVRANGEBYSCORE/ZRANGE BYSCORE with a deep LIMIT offset applied .skip(offset) on the ordered BTreeMap range = O(offset) walk; BTreeMap iteration (~6ns/node) is ~3x slower than redis's skiplist walk (500k zset, LIMIT 450000 10: oracle ~0.8ms vs fr ~2.9ms = 3x). Fix: jump to the start rank via the order-statistic treap (rank_of bounds like score_bound_count i5896 + select per emitted element) = O(take*log n), gated to deep offsets (offset > n_emit*24) where it beats the scan, with adaptive warming (maybe_warm_rank_tree_for_index). Byte-identical. Beats redis.
close_reason: Fixed: ZRANGEBYSCORE/ZREVRANGEBYSCORE/ZRANGE-BYSCORE deep LIMIT offset jumps to the start rank via the order-statistic treap (rank_of bounds + select), O(take*log n), instead of an O(offset) BTreeMap walk. Gated to deep offsets (offset > n_emit*24) + adaptive warming; cold/shallow/packed fall back to the scan. ZRANGEBYSCORE LIMIT 450000 10 (500k): 2895us->41us (70x), parity-or-faster than redis. Byte-exact: 11 curated (ASC/REV/exclusive/WITHSCORES/edges) + zset_differ 4000 iters. golden 9b966294.

title: Hash/ZSet OBJECT ENCODING auto-demotes after threshold raise (vendored is sticky)
description:
fr's OBJECT ENCODING for Hash and SortedSet recomputes from scratch on each call, so a hash/zset that grew past the listpack threshold and was reported as `hashtable`/`skiplist` will auto-downgrade back to `listpack` if the threshold is later raised above the current size. Vendored Redis 7.2.4 never demotes: once a hash/zset converts to `hashtable`/`skiplist`, it stays even if the entries-count threshold is raised or the dataset shrinks below it.

Set has the correct sticky behavior via `force_set_hashtable_encoding` + `force_set_listpack_encoding` flags on the entry struct (fr-store/src/lib.rs:613-616). Hash and SortedSet are missing the equivalent flags.

Reproduction:
  CONFIG SET hash-max-listpack-entries 128
  HSET h f1..f129 v1..v129        # crosses threshold -> hashtable on both
  CONFIG SET hash-max-listpack-entries 200
  OBJECT ENCODING h               # vendored: hashtable / fr: listpack

Same with zset-max-listpack-entries (zset 130 entries -> skiplist; raise to 200 -> vendored: skiplist / fr: listpack).

Set is correct (already has sticky flags).

Fix plan:
1. Add `force_hash_hashtable_encoding: bool` and `force_zset_skiplist_encoding: bool` to the entry struct.
2. Add helper methods `refresh_hash_encoding_state` and `refresh_zset_encoding_state` that check the current data against thresholds and set the sticky flag if conversion is warranted. Never clear (no demotion).
3. Call the refresh helpers from every hash-mutating method (HSET, HSETNX, HMSET, HINCRBY, HINCRBYFLOAT) and zset-mutating method (ZADD, ZINCRBY).
4. `object_encoding` returns "hashtable"/"skiplist" when the sticky flag is set, falling through to the listpack/hashtable computation only when the flag is false.
5. Regression test covers: threshold raise post-conversion, threshold lower (no-op stays correct), big-value-then-shrink, and DEL+SET resets sticky.

Probe sweep #79 (probe164.sh + inline confirmation).
close_reason: completed

title: fr-command SCRIPT FLUSH/DEBUG and FUNCTION LIST/FLUSH place noscript guard after option parsing — scripted bad-arg calls surface mode/syntax error instead of upstream's CMD_NOSCRIPT reply
description:
## Symptom
Continuation of the noscript-ordering drift family fixed in 0d3561a (frankenredis-d021m) and 26dc7b0 (frankenredis-7j2cw). Four more handlers have the same shape: arity check → parse / option loop → noscript guard, but upstream evaluates arity FIRST and CMD_NOSCRIPT SECOND with no handler-level parsing in between.

Concrete cases (all assume script_nesting_level >= 1):
- \`SCRIPT FLUSH NOSUCH\` → fr returns 'ERR SCRIPT FLUSH only support SYNC|ASYNC option'; upstream returns 'This Redis command is not allowed from script'.
- \`SCRIPT DEBUG MAYBE\` → fr returns 'ERR Use SCRIPT DEBUG YES/SYNC/NO'; upstream returns the noscript reply.
- \`FUNCTION LIST NOSUCH\` → fr returns 'ERR Unknown argument NOSUCH'; upstream returns the noscript reply.
- \`FUNCTION FLUSH NOSUCH\` → fr returns 'ERR FUNCTION FLUSH only supports SYNC|ASYNC option'; upstream returns the noscript reply.

## Differential probe
Upstream commands.def lines 5318-5322 declare SCRIPT DEBUG/EXISTS/FLUSH/KILL with CMD_NOSCRIPT; lines 5138-5146 declare FUNCTION DELETE/DUMP/FLUSH/KILL/LIST/LOAD/RESTORE/STATS with CMD_NOSCRIPT. Upstream script.c::scriptCommand at legacy_redis_code/redis/src/script.c:524-532 evaluates arity FIRST and CMD_NOSCRIPT SECOND, both before the handler runs.

fr-command:
- script_cmd FLUSH (lib.rs:16923-16947): mode validation at line 16937 fires before the noscript guard at line 16943.
- script_cmd DEBUG (lib.rs:16948-16972): mode validation at line 16956 fires before the noscript guard at line 16967.
- function_cmd LIST (lib.rs:8760-8794): the option-parsing while loop at 8765-8791 (which can emit 'library name argument was not given' or 'Unknown argument <token>') fires before the noscript guard at line 8792.
- function_cmd FLUSH (lib.rs:9006-9028): mode validation at line 9018 fires before the noscript guard at line 9024.

## Fix
Move the 'if store.script_nesting_level >= 1' guard immediately after the arity / extra-arg check in each of the four handlers — before any option-parsing or mode-validation code. Mirrors the pattern already used by SCRIPT EXISTS/LOAD/KILL, FUNCTION DUMP/KILL/STATS/DELETE.

Add a test pinning the four scripted bad-arg cases to SCRIPT_NOSCRIPT_ERROR.

## Out of scope
- FUNCTION LOAD's noscript guard fires after the REPLACE flag is inspected, but that inspection has no error path before the guard, so it's already in spec.
- FUNCTION RESTORE has noscript after the policy utf8 parse — the policy parse can technically emit InvalidUtf8Argument before noscript, but the impact is bounded to non-utf8 policy strings and not in scope here.
close_reason: fr-command SCRIPT FLUSH/DEBUG, FUNCTION LIST/FLUSH/RESTORE now place script_nesting_level guard immediately after the table-level arity check (was after handler-level extra-arg / mode / option-loop validation), mirroring upstream's CMD_NOSCRIPT-after-arity-check-only ordering. Inside a Lua script, scripted bad-arg calls now surface 'This Redis command is not allowed from script' rather than the handler-level error. Two existing tests script_admin_subcommands_rejected_from_scripts_after_validation and function_admin_subcommands_rejected_from_scripts_after_validation pinned the broken ordering — renamed (..._after_arity_check) and rewritten to assert noscript wins for every arity-correct scripted input including SCRIPT FLUSH SYNC EXTRA, SCRIPT DEBUG MAYBE, FUNCTION LIST NOSUCH, FUNCTION FLUSH BOGUS, FUNCTION RESTORE payload FLUSH EXTRA. New test script_and_function_subcommands_run_noscript_guard_before_option_parsing covers the four core cases. 759 passed (up from 758) / 4 pre-existing failures (down from 5).

title: ZRANGEBYSCORE/ZCOUNT range bounds reject C99 hex-float that strtod accepts
description: parse_score_bound (fr-command, used by ZRANGEBYSCORE/ZREVRANGEBYSCORE/ZCOUNT/ZRANGE BYSCORE/ZRANGESTORE BYSCORE) parsed min/max via Rust f64::from_str (decimal only), but upstream zslParseRange uses strtod which accepts C99 hex floats (0x10, 0X1A, 0x1.8p3, exclusive (0x10). fr rejected these as 'ERR min or max is not a float'; worse, on a WRONG-TYPE key the float-parse error masked the WRONGTYPE redis returns after a successful range parse. The ZADD score path already handled hex via try_parse_hex_float; range path did not. Found by randomized differential fuzz (fuzz_bt.py) vs redis 7.2.4.
close_reason: FIXED: parse_score_bound now tries try_parse_hex_float before decimal f64::from_str, matching strtod. Differential vs redis 7.2.4: 15 targeted cases + 72-case golden battery (18 bounds x 4 forms) BYTE-EXACT, golden sha256 ec74721aa972aeb66478e722d77120d27b02b4adde38025f1d01b852e589cb0d (fr==redis). Wrong-type keys now correctly return WRONGTYPE (was masked). Regression test zrangebyscore_score_bound_accepts_c99_hex_float added; fr-command suite 1146 pass. Multi-seed fuzz residuals are only PTTL timing-jitter + a pre-existing EXPIRE control-char cosmetic edge.

title: Lua parser/lexer error wording diverges from upstream (4 sites)
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4:

  return 'unterminated     v=unfinished string near '<eof>'        fr=unterminated string
  return [[unterminated    v=unfinished long string near '<eof>'    fr=unterminated long string
  return (1+1))            v=' <eof>' expected near ')'             fr=unexpected symbol near ')'
  return foo:bar           v=function arguments expected near '<eof>'   fr=expected function arguments

Four parser/lexer error sites in fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs:

1. Line 971: "unterminated string" → upstream wording "unfinished string near '<eof>'"
2. Line 1028: "unterminated long string" → "unfinished long string near '<eof>'"
3. Line 1939: "expected function arguments" → "function arguments expected near '<eof>'"  (note "function arguments" first, then verb)
4. Line 1985: `"unexpected symbol near '{X}'"` → when the EOF-expectation context is "extra suffix after a complete expression" upstream emits `"'<eof>' expected near '<token>'"` instead. This is harder to fix surgically — needs end-of-input post-check after parsing.

Plus deeper, deferred:
- Long-string `=` levels (`[=[…]=]` / `[==[…]==]`) not implemented — emits "unexpected symbol near '['"
- Unterminated long comment emits `'=' expected near '<eof>'` instead of `'unfinished long comment near '<eof>''`
- Suffix `[expr]` accepted on numbers (`1[2]`) where upstream rejects at parse time

This bead tackles only the 4 wording fixes; the level-bracket parser feature is a separate scope.
close_reason: Four upstream-parity fixes to Lua parser/lexer error wording at fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs: (1) read_string EOF emits 'unfinished string near <eof>' (line ~971); (2) read_long_string EOF emits 'unfinished long string near <eof>' (line ~1032); (3) parse_call_args bad token emits 'function arguments expected near <token>' using peek's display (line ~1945); (4) parse_block exits after parsing a return statement so trailing tokens surface via the chunk-level <eof>-expected check at execute() rather than the generic primary-expression 'unexpected symbol' (line ~1480). Regression test lua_parser_lexer_error_wording_matches_upstream_yunl8 pins all 4 wording sites plus 2 valid-program sanity checks. Full release test suite (1009 tests) still passes. Live-diff vs vendored 16380: 7/7 cases match byte-for-byte.

title: perf(fr-store): ZRANDMEMBER count O(n) members_at_indices walk (~16x slower than redis)
description: Measured fr vs redis (100k zset, warm zrank tree): ZRANDMEMBER Z -100 = fr 501 req/s vs redis 8097 (~16x SLOWER); ZRANDMEMBER Z 100 = fr 518 vs 8130. SortedSet::members_at_indices (crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs ~936) does an O(n) iter_asc().enumerate() walk to fetch picked indices, IGNORING the order-statistic treap. index_slice_asc already uses tree.select(idx) O(log n) when the rank_tree is built. Fix: members_at_indices should use the Full variant's rank_tree.select() per index when built (O(picks log n)), falling back to the linear walk when cold (no regression) — byte-exact. SRANDMEMBER/HRANDFIELD already fast (IndexSet O(1) get_index). LOCKED: fr-store/lib.rs held by BlackThrush at filing time.
close_reason: Completed: warm rank_tree select in members_at_indices for ZRANDMEMBER count. Baseline hyperfine 5.510s +/- 0.117s, 200.26 ops/s; candidate 2.054s +/- 0.009s, 553.94 ops/s. Golden behavior sha256 and workload raw transcript sha256 unchanged.

title: MEMORY USAGE diverges across all value types — undercounts strings/lists/streams, overcounts hashes/zsets vs vendored 7.2.4
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4:
- SET sk hello       → ven 64, fr 47
- empty list         → ven 64, fr 44
- 5-elt list (lp)    → ven 72, fr 48
- 2-field hash (lp)  → ven 72, fr 81
- 3-elt zset (lp)    → ven 72, fr 116
- 1-entry stream     → ven 4728, fr 59 (massive)

Per object.c::objectCommandMemoryUsage + zmalloc accounting, vendored adds robj/dictEntry/listpack/listEntry overhead bytes. fr's MEMORY USAGE only counts payload bytes for some types. Needs per-type encoding-aware accounting that mirrors zmalloc_usable_size + overhead constants. Not high-priority since most clients use it advisorily, but would be nice for parity tooling. Acceptance: each of the above probes within 5% of vendored.
close_reason: Replaced payload-only MEMORY USAGE estimator with Redis-shaped key/SDS/object/listpack/stream accounting and pinned vendored small-encoding baselines. Also removed a proptest reject-cap failure in fr-store metamorphic expiry test encountered during full suite validation.

title: perf(expire): active-expire should sample volatile-only keys, not all keys — match redis activeExpireCycle effectiveness
close_reason: Shipped 51cf68654: volatile_keys TTL index; active-expire samples volatile-only (redis-faithful). Effectiveness test 1000 persistent+5 expired vol -> reaps 5/5 in one cycle; O(1) no-op when no TTL keys. fr-store 509/509, fr-runtime 411/411.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
FINDING (NobleRiver, static analysis). Store::run_active_expire_cycle (fr-store/src/lib.rs:5400) samples from self.ordered_keys (ALL keys, BTreeSet) up to sample_limit (~20) per cycle, then checks each via evaluate_expiry(entry.expires_at_ms). Redis activeExpireCycle samples db->expires (VOLATILE keys only). Under a mostly-non-volatile keyset (e.g. 1M persistent + 100 TTL keys), fr wastes ~all samples on non-volatile keys -> expired keys reaped far slower than redis (lingering in DBSIZE/used_memory/INFO expired_keys until lazy access). fr's cursor eventually visits every key so it is functionally CORRECT (all expired keys reaped eventually + lazy-expiry backstop), just sample-INEFFECTIVE.

DESIGN: add `volatile_keys: BTreeSet<Vec<u8>>` (db-encoded, parallel to ordered_keys). Maintain at the ~12 expires_count mutation sites (lib.rs:3641/3689/4268/5081/12341 add -> insert; 4600/5099/5121/12359 sub -> remove) + internal_entries_remove (deletion/expiry removal) + new()/flushdb/flush_prefix (init/clear). Iterate volatile_keys instead of ordered_keys in run_active_expire_cycle (cursor + wraparound logic identical, just over the smaller set). SAFETY NET: volatile_keys is only a SAMPLING HINT — correctness still comes from evaluate_expiry re-checking the real expires_at_ms, so any missed maintenance site degrades reaping efficiency for that key (lazy-expiry backstop) but NEVER breaks correctness.

PARITY/TEST RISK: changes active-expire effectiveness. fr-store test active_expire_cycle_wraparound_samples_each_key_once (lib.rs:19778) uses an all-volatile keyset -> behavior-identical (passes unchanged). BUT fr-runtime active-expire tests (lib.rs:18612 active_expire_cycle_propagates_del_for_reaped_keys, 26844) live in a crate often reserved by IcyWolf and may assert cross-key sampling — verify + coordinate. Needs a dedicated pass (cross-crate, ~18 mechanical edits + a mixed volatile/non-volatile sampling test asserting non-volatile keys are never sampled and volatile keys reaped within N cycles), NOT an hourly slot.

Add a focused test that loads one library into the target store, restores a disjoint payload with APPEND, and asserts both libraries remain present with stable dump output.

title: HSCAN/SSCAN/ZSCAN on small encodings should return all in one call (cursor=0)
description:
Differential probe vs vendored Redis 7.2.4:

Small (listpack/intset-encoded) collections:

  HSET h f1 v1 f2 v2 f3 v3
  HSCAN h 0 COUNT 1
    vendored: cursor=0, all 3 pairs returned (COUNT ignored)
    fr:       cursor=1, 1 pair returned (COUNT honored, chunked)

  HSCAN h 99999       (bogus cursor)
    vendored: cursor=0, all 3 pairs (cursor treated as wrap)
    fr:       cursor=0, empty array

Same divergence for SSCAN (listpack and intset variants) and
ZSCAN (listpack variant).

Upstream t_hash.c::hscanCommand, t_set.c::sscanCommand,
t_zset.c::zscanCommand all short-circuit on the small-encoding
path: they iterate the entire listpack/intset and reply with
cursor=0, regardless of COUNT or input cursor. COUNT is only a
hint for the hashtable/dict path. This is why an HSCAN over a
listpack-encoded hash can always be drained in a single round-trip,
which scripts and clients rely on.

fr was using its generic chunked iterator for ALL encodings —
honoring COUNT and treating an out-of-range cursor as "no data
left".

Fix locus: fr-store HSCAN/SSCAN/ZSCAN (or fr-command dispatch
layer if scanning is done there). For listpack/intset-encoded
values, return the full collection with cursor=0; only honor
COUNT/cursor for the dict-encoded path.

Test plan: pin three cases per command: small-encoding with
COUNT=1 returns everything + cursor=0; small-encoding with bogus
cursor returns everything + cursor=0; large/dict-encoding still
chunks normally per the cursor architecture.

(Same family as project_scan_cursor_architecture.md memory but
narrower — H/S/ZSCAN small-encoding short-circuit, not the full
SCAN cursor rewrite.)
close_reason: fixed

title: Container parent COMMAND INFO categories include extra ACL overrides not present upstream
description:
All container-parent commands (object, cluster, client, acl, config, slowlog, latency, module, function, script) emit excess ACL categories in COMMAND INFO compared to vendored 7.2.4:

- ven object → [@slow]; fr → [@slow, @keyspace]
- ven cluster → [@slow]; fr → [@slow, @admin, @dangerous]
- ven client → [@slow]; fr → [@slow, @admin, @dangerous, @connection]
- ven acl → [@slow]; fr → [@slow, @admin, @dangerous]
- ven config → [@slow]; fr → [@slow, @admin, @dangerous]
- ven slowlog → [@slow]; fr → [@slow, @admin, @dangerous]
- ven latency → [@slow]; fr → [@slow, @admin, @dangerous]
- ven module → [@slow]; fr → [@slow, @admin, @dangerous]
- ven function → [@slow]; fr → [@slow, @scripting]
- ven script → [@slow]; fr → [@slow, @scripting]

Root cause: STATIC_ACL_OVERRIDES table at fr-command/src/lib.rs:13772 is consumed by both COMMAND INFO categories emission AND ACL CAT lookup. Upstream sets the parent's ACL_CATEGORY = 0 (no extra) but each subcommand carries its own ACL_CATEGORY_*. fr's overlay bridges the two for ACL gating but leaks into COMMAND INFO output.

Fix: separate the COMMAND INFO categories emission from the ACL CAT lookup. The override should only apply when querying ACL CAT, not when emitting COMMAND INFO categories.
close_reason: Split ACL category map into by_command (with overlay, for ACL CAT) and by_command_info (without overlay, for COMMAND INFO emission). Container parents (cluster/client/acl/config/memory/slowlog/latency/module/object/function/script/debug) now emit only the flag-derived categories matching upstream byte-for-byte. ACL CAT @admin still surfaces per-subcommand entries (cluster|reset, config|set, etc.) via the unchanged UPSTREAM_ACL_CATEGORY_ENTRIES path. Pin test asserts both contracts. Commit 67253af.

title: Precompute ACL category->command mapping in OnceLock
description:
# Precompute ACL Category→Command Mapping in OnceLock

## What

command_categories() in crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:62 and
commands_in_acl_category() in crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:8924 recompute
ACL category→command mappings from scratch on every call. Each call iterates
21 ACL categories × the entire COMMAND_TABLE (~200 commands) and does
split_whitespace().collect::<Vec<&str>>() on each command's flags string —
~4,200 Vec allocations per invocation.

After the April 9 short-circuit (frankenredis-zjii), the DEFAULT user (no
category rules) never hits this code path. But any user with ACL category
rules (e.g., +@read -@dangerous) still pays the full cost on every command.

## Why This Matters

Production Redis deployments commonly use ACL categories for least-privilege
security. A web app might configure: user webuser +@read +@write -@dangerous.
That user has non-empty allowed_categories, so the short-circuit doesn't apply,
and every command pays ~4,200 allocations for category resolution.

## Design

Use OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> to build the mapping
once at startup:

```rust
static ACL_CATEGORY_MAP: OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> = OnceLock::new();

fn commands_in_acl_category_cached(category: &str) -> &'static [&'static str] {
    let map = ACL_CATEGORY_MAP.get_or_init(|| {
        let mut m = HashMap::new();
        for &cat in ACL_CATEGORIES {
            m.insert(cat, commands_in_acl_category_uncached(cat));
        }
        m
    });
    map.get(category).map(|v| v.as_slice()).unwrap_or(&[])
}
```

Also precompute the reverse mapping (command→categories) so
command_categories() is a single HashMap lookup:

```rust
static COMMAND_CATEGORY_MAP: OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> = OnceLock::new();
```

## Acceptance Criteria

1. commands_in_acl_category uses OnceLock-cached mapping
2. command_categories uses OnceLock-cached reverse mapping
3. Zero per-command allocations for ACL category resolution
4. All ACL conformance tests pass
5. Users with non-trivial ACL rules see no throughput regression vs default user
6. Memory overhead is bounded (21 categories × 200 commands = ~4200 pointers = ~33KB)
close_reason: Cached ACL category mappings + command reverse map

title: fr-server inline parser accepts closing quote followed by non-whitespace; upstream sdssplitargs errors
close_reason: fr-server's split_inline_args now requires the closing quote to be followed by whitespace or end-of-line, mirroring upstream sds.c::sdssplitargs lines 1049-1053 (double-quoted) and 1064-1068 (single-quoted): 'closing quote must be followed by a space or nothing at all'. Both branches gain a post-quote check that returns the same 'Protocol error: unbalanced quotes in request' error upstream uses. Two new regression tests pin: (1) double-quoted form rejects 'hello"world', accepts 'hello' (no trailer), 'hello' + space + 'extra' (whitespace trailer), and 'hello' + tab + 'extra' (tab trailer); (2) single-quoted form same rules. Wire-format conformance + security-adjacent issue: a client building inline commands by string concatenation could inject extra args via the prior leniency (key"payload"trailer would silently split into multiple args bypassing client-side argument-count or escaping logic). Discovered via /testing-fuzzing skill — audited fr-server's split_inline_args against upstream sdssplitargs. All 46 fr-server lib tests pass; clippy clean. Thirty-first concrete fix this session, first via /testing-fuzzing.

title: LZF compressed output diverges from vendored due to missing 'ref > in_data' check
description:
fr's `lzf_compress` (fr-persist/src/lib.rs:1814) produces more aggressive matches than vendored's lzf_c.c. The result is a valid LZF stream that decompresses correctly, but the byte output diverges from vendored — so DEBUG OBJECT serializedlength and DUMP byte-for-byte output differ.

Reproduction (DEBUG OBJECT serializedlength after SET k "x"*30):
  vendored: serializedlength=12  (compressed payload 9 bytes)
  fr:       serializedlength=8   (compressed payload 5 bytes)

The 4-byte gap is consistent across repetitive inputs (30B, 50B, 100B, 200B, ...).
Non-repetitive (random) strings match exactly.

Root cause: vendored's `lzf_c.c:164` match condition requires:
  ref > (u8 *)in_data
i.e., the back-reference pointer must be STRICTLY greater than the input start. fr's match condition only checks `r < ip`. The difference: at ip=1 with the first trigram pattern, fr matches against position 0, but vendored skips that match (because ref==in_data which is not strictly greater).

For "x"*30:
  vendored: emits [literal-2, x, x, long-match-26, literal-2, x, x] -> 9 bytes
  fr:       emits [literal-1, x, long-match-29]                     -> 5 bytes

Fix: add `r > 0` to fr_persist::lzf_compress's match guard (mirror vendored's `ref > in_data` check). Pin DEBUG OBJECT serializedlength parity across several payload lengths.

Probe sweep #117 (DEBUG OBJECT for repetitive strings, inline).
close_reason: completed

Server-only SET/P16/C50/3M on current main showed writer __send rows as real kernel cost but fanout was not the lever; next user-space row is fr_store::canonical_string_value_from_slice at 10.14% self. Candidate must be fundamentally different from rejected integer-parser micro-levers (pass105/pass106/pass107/pass110/pass113): use an alien-artifact value-normalization or representation-metadata primitive, not a branch-only parse tweak. Baseline first, one source lever only, byte-identical TCP golden sha256, focused fr-store/fr-runtime gates, paired and reversed SET/P16/C50, keep only Score>=2.0. Preserve Redis object encoding, integer canonicalization, DEBUG DIGEST/OBJECT behavior, expiry, ordering, FP/RNG, and reply bytes.

title: COMMAND GETKEYS for numkeys-based commands missing validation for bad/negative/oversized numkeys
description:
Differential probe vs vendored Redis 7.2.4:

  COMMAND GETKEYS LMPOP abc k1 LEFT
    vendored: ERR Invalid arguments specified for command
    fr:       (empty array)

  COMMAND GETKEYS LMPOP -1 k1 LEFT
    vendored: ERR Invalid arguments specified for command
    fr:       (empty array)

  COMMAND GETKEYS LMPOP 99 k1 LEFT
    vendored: ERR Invalid arguments specified for command
    fr:       k1, LEFT       (treats LEFT as a key — numkeys not bounded)

  COMMAND GETKEYS ZMPOP abc k1 MIN
    same divergence

  COMMAND GETKEYS SINTERCARD abc k1 k2
    same divergence

The zero-numkeys case is already handled:
  LMPOP 0 LEFT → both return "Invalid number of arguments..." (note
  the word "number"). vendored uses "Invalid arguments..." (no
  "number") for the non-numeric / negative / oversized variants.

Upstream src/db.c::getKeysFromCommand calls each command's
getkeys_proc; for the numkeys-based family
(genericGetKeys, lmpopGetKeys, sintercardGetKeys, etc.) the proc
parses numkeys via getLongFromObjectOrReply with C_OK gating. A
non-numeric value, a negative value, or one that would make the key
window exceed argc all return -1 from the getkeys proc, which
getKeysFromCommand translates to the "Invalid arguments specified
for command" reply.

fr's COMMAND GETKEYS dispatch presumably extracts numkeys via a
parse that silently coerces or falls back to 0, then iterates the
slice without error.

Fix locus: the COMMAND GETKEYS handler in crates/fr-command. For
each numkeys-based command (LMPOP, BLMPOP, ZMPOP, BZMPOP,
SINTERCARD, ZINTERCARD, ZDIFF, ZINTER, ZUNION, ZDIFFSTORE,
ZINTERSTORE, ZUNIONSTORE, SDIFF/SDIFFSTORE/SINTER/SINTERSTORE/
SUNION/SUNIONSTORE variants with explicit numkeys?), validate
numkeys: must be a strictly positive integer AND numkeys <= remaining
arg count.

Test plan: pin each variant for LMPOP/ZMPOP/SINTERCARD/ZINTERCARD
plus the existing zero-numkeys "Invalid number of arguments..."
case.
close_reason: fixed

title: parse_score_bound trim() over-accepts whitespace and rejects bare '(' that upstream coerces to exclusive 0
description: Differential probe vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 against `ZRANGEBYSCORE` / `ZCOUNT` / `ZRANGESTORE BYSCORE` / `ZRANGEBYSCORE` revealed three score-bound parsing divergences from upstream `t_zset.c::zslParseRange` (which calls strtod with the `eptr[0] != '\0'` trailing-junk gate): (1) bare `(` is parsed as exclusive 0 by strtod (empty-string -> 0.0, eptr at '\0'), but fr's `(`+`.parse::<f64>()` rejected it; (2) leading whitespace BEFORE `(` (e.g. ` (5`) routes to the non-open branch upstream and strtod fails on the literal `(`, but fr's `text.trim()` stripped the leading whitespace and accepted; (3) trailing whitespace (e.g. `(0 `, `0 `) trips upstream's eptr check and fails, but fr's outer `trim()` plus inner `rest.trim()` accepted both. fr's wording was already correct (`min or max is not a float`); only the strict-strtod gate behavior was off.
acceptance_criteria: parse_score_bound must mirror strtod-with-eptr semantics: (1) bare `(` parses as exclusive 0; (2) `(` must be the FIRST character — leading whitespace before `(` rejects; (3) leading whitespace AFTER `(` (or with no paren) is fine; (4) trailing whitespace anywhere rejects; (5) NaN rejects with the same wording. Existing inf literal handling (-inf/+inf/inf, with optional leading whitespace) still passes via Rust's parse::<f64>.
close_reason: Rewrote fr-command::parse_score_bound to drop the outer .trim(), use strip_prefix('(') to enforce that `(` is the first character, and call after_ws.parse::<f64>() with explicit empty -> 0.0 handling for bare `(`. Pinned with zrangebyscore_score_bound_matches_strtod_semantics covering bare `(` -> exclusive 0, `( 0` -> exclusive 0 with whitespace skip, ` (0` rejected (leading-before-paren), `(0 ` rejected (trailing whitespace), `0 ` rejected (trailing whitespace no paren), `(NaN`/`NaN` rejected. All 21 ZRANGE tests still green.

title: perf: large collection replies pay a Vec<RespFrame> + per-member clone/format intermediate (zero-copy encode lever)
description:
Heavy-op sweep (single 100k-elem collections + 50k sets): the ENTIRE compute/algebra surface is fr parity-or-faster vs vendored redis 7.2.4 (SINTERSTORE/SUNIONSTORE/SDIFF*/ZINTERSTORE/ZUNIONSTORE/ZDIFFSTORE all fr-faster; BITCOUNT 0.51x = fr 2x; PFCOUNT-2 0.23x = fr 4x; SINTERCARD/SRANDMEMBER fr-faster). The ONLY measured slowdowns are ~1.1x and memory/alloc-bound:
  SMEMBERS (50k intset): redis=280 fr=253 (1.11x slower)
  COPY (200k string):    redis=48780 fr=44692 (1.09x slower)  [pure memcpy, no clean lever]
  (last session also: LRANGE-all-100k 1.14x)

ROOT CAUSE (the systematic ~1.1x on large-array replies): fr builds collection replies as RespFrame::Array(Vec<RespFrame::BulkString(Some(Vec<u8>))>) — for a 50k reply that's a 50k-element Vec<RespFrame> (~1.6MB of enums) PLUS a cloned Vec<u8> per member (store.smembers does s.iter().map(into_owned).collect()) PLUS, for intsets, a n.to_string() heap alloc per member (set_int_to_bytes). Redis writes each member straight into the client output buffer via ll2string/addReplyBulk — no intermediate array object, no per-member heap alloc. So fr pays ~4x the reply size in extra memory traffic + 50k allocs.

LEVER (zero-copy collection-reply encode; target ~1.2-1.4x on large-array replies, BROAD — every collection read benefits: SMEMBERS/LRANGE/HGETALL/HKEYS/HVALS/ZRANGE/SUNION/SINTER/SDIFF/SORT): encode members DIRECTLY into the output buffer, skipping the Vec<RespFrame> and the per-member clone/format. Two implementation options:
  (A) Borrowed direct-encode fast path per hot collection command (mirrors execute_plain_get_borrowed_into -> FastEncodedReply): runtime method writes *N\r\n + \r\nmember\r\n directly into out, with a SetValue::for_each_member(f) that formats intset i64 into a reused stack buffer (no per-member alloc) and borrows generic-set members. Contained (fr-server borrowed dispatch + runtime method), one command at a time. Gate via plain_borrowed_default_key_read_allows; fall back (None) on wrongtype/db>0/special state.
  (B) A RespFrame::BulkStringArray(Vec<Vec<u8>>) variant that encodes as a bulk-string array directly (keeps the member clones but drops the 50k-element Vec<RespFrame>). Broader (all commands switch by wrapping the existing Vec<Vec<u8>>) but RespFrame is the universal reply type — adding a variant fans out to every exhaustive match site (encode_into/encode_into_resp3/strip_db_prefixes/digest/monitor/...); members are values not keys so db-prefix-strip is a no-op, but every walker needs an arm.

ISOMORPHISM: byte-identical reply (same members, same order, same RESP2 Array */RESP3 Set ~ framing); golden sha256 over SMEMBERS/LRANGE/etc. on int + generic + edge (empty/missing/wrongtype) sets; verify INFO keyspace_hits not double-counted (single keyspace lookup). Repro: build 50k intset, redis-benchmark -q SMEMBERS key. Note: ~1.1-1.3x ceiling (memory-bandwidth-bound for the actual reply bytes); per the memory-bound-is-~1x finding this is a modest broad win, not a 2x.
close_reason: Implemented + measured; lever DISPROVEN, reverted (not shipped). Built a byte-identical borrowed SMEMBERS direct-encode fast path (SetValue::for_each_member intset-format-into-reused-buffer + Store::smembers_for_each + execute_smembers_borrowed_into writing the array straight to the output buffer, no Vec<Vec<u8>> clones, no Vec<RespFrame>). Correct: raw-RESP golden sha256 IDENTICAL baseline-vs-fastpath across int/generic/small/empty/missing/wrongtype in RESP2+RESP3 (bc31db9e, 2700B), keyspace hits/misses identical (8/2), 117 set + 2 smembers tests pass. But perf is only ~1.04x vs baseline (SMEMBERS-int-50k new=259 base=248 redis=273, still 1.05x slower than redis; generic 1.03x). At ~250 req/s for a 50k-member reply the cost is per-member format+frame+copy + transfer, NOT the Vec<RespFrame> intermediate the bead hypothesized; eliminating 50k allocs/call barely moves throughput because fr per-member work already matches redis ll2string+addReplyBulk. Memory/transfer-bound ~1x. No provable Score>=2.0, not shipped. Whole-collection replies are near redis parity + transfer-bound; small-reply collection ops already fr-faster. Impl preserved in git stash. Next genuinely-different lever: parser SIMD RESP frame scanning / batched IO.

title: PFCOUNT single-key returns stale cache value for malformed HYLL string instead of WRONGTYPE
description: Store::pfcount single-key fast path calls hll_cache_read(data) (reads cardinality cache at header bytes 8..16) BEFORE validating the HLL structure. A string with the HYLL magic + cache-header shape but a malformed encoding byte or wrong length returned the stale bytes in the cache slot (e.g. 'HYLL'+12 zeros -> 0; 'HYLL_invalid_header_padding_xxxxxx' -> 7018130353242598497) instead of erroring. Upstream hyperloglog.c::pfcountCommand calls isHLLObjectOrReply (magic + len>=16 + encoding<=1 + dense->exact size) BEFORE consulting the cache. Found via differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4. Fix: hll_redis_header_is_valid() O(1) check applied before the cache read in pfcount; PFADD/PFMERGE/multi-key PFCOUNT already validate via hll_parse. Verified WRONGTYPE for all malformed HYLL inputs, valid HLLs unchanged.
close_reason: Fixed: hll_redis_header_is_valid() O(1) structural check (magic+len>=16+encoding<=1+dense exact size, mirroring isHLLObjectOrReply) applied before the cardinality-cache read in Store::pfcount. Malformed HYLL strings now return WRONGTYPE instead of stale cache bytes. Verified byte-exact vs vendored 7.2.4 across all malformed inputs; valid dense/sparse HLLs unchanged (479 fr-store tests pass, regression test added).

title: MEMORY STATS: clients.normal and clients.slaves hardcoded to 0 (per-client buffer summation missing)
description: Follow-up to frankenredis-jrqgd. After jrqgd wired up per-session qbuf_bytes / output_buffer_bytes, fr now has the per-client buffer data needed to compute MEMORY STATS' 'clients.normal' and 'clients.slaves' fields -- but fr-command/src/lib.rs:18513-18514 still hardcodes both to 0. Upstream object.c::getMemoryOverheadData iterates server.clients and sums clientMemUsage(c) per client into mh->clients_normal (CLIENT_TYPE_NORMAL) or mh->clients_slaves (CLIENT_TYPE_SLAVE), where clientMemUsage = querybuf_alloc + argv_len_sum + reply_buffer_size + reply_list_size + struct overhead. Live differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4 on an idle session: fr 'clients.normal: 0' vs vendored 'clients.normal: 1984'. Implementation: iterate self.server.client_sessions (already populated by Runtime::record_client_session) and sum the qbuf_bytes + qbuf_free_bytes + output_buffer_bytes + per-client struct overhead (~432 bytes from tepuj's tot-mem formula). Bucket by replica vs normal -- look at session.replica_or_primary flag (if exists) or default everything to clients.normal. Acceptance: differential probe shows non-zero clients.normal after at least one client has issued traffic; ideally tracks within the same order of magnitude as vendored's value. The byte-exact target depends on whether fr also needs to model argv_len_sum (currently 0) and reply_list_size separately.
close_reason: MEMORY STATS clients.normal and clients.slaves now emit real per-client buffer summation. Implementation: (1) Added stat_clients_normal_mem_bytes and stat_clients_replica_mem_bytes usize fields to Store (default 0, NOT cleared on CONFIG RESETSTAT because they represent current state, not cumulative counters). (2) Added Runtime::refresh_client_memory_aggregates that iterates self.server.client_sessions, sums PER_CLIENT_STRUCT_BYTES (432, same baseline as tepuj's tot-mem formula) + session.qbuf_bytes + session.qbuf_free_bytes + session.output_buffer_bytes per client, and buckets the result by self.server.replication_runtime_state.replicas.keys() membership (replica vs normal). (3) record_client_session and remove_client_session both call refresh so the aggregates stay current as clients come/go and as buffer sizes change. (4) fr-command's MEMORY STATS handler now emits 'clients.slaves: store.stat_clients_replica_mem_bytes' and 'clients.normal: store.stat_clients_normal_mem_bytes' instead of hardcoded 0. Live differential probe with one client doing SET k v: fr 'clients.normal: 432' vs vendored 'clients.normal: 1984'. fr's 432 = the per-client struct baseline (buffers drained between commands); the value scales up live with qbuf/obl during active traffic, exactly as upstream's getMemoryOverheadData does. Vendored's 1984 includes a larger fixed per-client overhead from its C struct (with many more fields) plus argv_len_sum (fr's argv-mem is still 0, separate accounting follow-up). The reported value is no longer a stub. Tests: fr-store 336 + fr-command 910 + fr-runtime 372 all pass.

title: [perf] io-threads: safe thread-pool offload of socket read/parse + reply-encode (all-safe alternative to io_uring/gnwuw)
description: The per-command gap is network/syscall-bound (gnwuw). io_uring is blocked by #![forbid(unsafe_code)]. Redis 7.2's OWN answer is 'io-threads': a fixed std::thread pool that offloads the READ+PARSE and WRITE+ENCODE phases off the main thread while command EXECUTION stays strictly serial (so behavior/parity is preserved — single-threaded command semantics, MULTI/EXEC/WATCH/pubsub ordering all intact). This is 100% safe Rust (std::thread + channels / crossbeam, no unsafe), matches upstream architecture exactly (CONFIG io-threads N, io-threads-do-reads), and targets the same syscall/network frontier io_uring would, without violating the no-unsafe policy. SCOPE: fr-server main.rs event loop restructure — main thread owns the keyspace + accepts/dispatches; a worker pool does recv()->parse into borrowed frames and encode->send(). Bounded queues; main thread executes commands in arrival order. Big undertaking, high-value (multi-x throughput on multi-connection/pipelined workloads), parity-preserving. Profile target: close the bulk of the ohsk5 ~2x P16 gap. Prereq: quiet-host baseline (vibu6).
close_reason: Pass109 kept bounded writer-pool handoff: current 1.12620941084s +/- 0.0832021003 vs worker-pool 0.88365094224s +/- 0.0926924170 paired; reversed worker-pool 0.98130812272s +/- 0.0320550834 vs current 1.12881418112s +/- 0.0439369090. Golden response sha256 unchanged bdcab17275344bc6c13913945fd5cad11c800de1108c6f77776e16b1a09cc1f4. Evidence artifacts/optimization/frankenredis-zhphm/pass109/. Reprofile shifted to canonical_string_value_from_slice, Store::set_plain_borrowed, foldhash/key policy, active-expire, not writer-boundary.

title: [perf] SET borrowed canonical value construction
description: Profile-backed child of frankenredis-zhphm. Baseline SET/P16 profile showed fr_store::canonical_string_value at 11.68% self under Store::drop_if_expired/plain SET. Implement one lever: construct canonical Value directly from borrowed SET payloads, avoiding transient Vec allocation for integer values while preserving Redis OBJECT ENCODING and GET bytes. Proof/bench artifacts: artifacts/optimization/frankenredis-zhphm/pass2/.
close_reason: Completed: borrowed canonical SET value construction. Baseline SET/P16/1M 1.327554s +/- 0.062030s; candidate 1.243681s +/- 0.048191s; paired 1.290546s -> 1.224995s (1.05x +/- 0.04); reversed 1.273594s -> 1.258108s. Golden RESP/OBJECT ENCODING sha256 baseline=candidate=54c98f8078ffcd6529f9a9b2e555cbd965961e6548c21bcc85566356916210ba. Re-profile moved canonicalization from 11.68% self to 1.68% self. Score 2.25.

description: Post frankenredis-zhphm.1 re-profile shows Runtime::refresh_client_memory_aggregates at 4.87% self on SET/P16/1M after canonicalization moved down. Current record_client_session rebuilds a replica-id HashSet and scans every client session on each readable batch. Lever: update clients.normal/clients.slaves memory counters by subtracting the old per-client bucket and adding the new one, preserving CLIENT/MEMORY observable values without full recompute.

Standalone candidate 1.192786s +/- 0.018928s vs noisy baseline 1.756033s +/- 0.350516s; paired baseline 1.217949s, candidate 1.852694s (baseline 1.52x +/- 0.33 faster); reversed candidate 1.295227s, baseline 1.408120s (candidate 1.09x +/- 0.12 faster). Golden sha baseline=candidate bdcab17275344bc6c13913945fd5cad11c800de1108c6f77776e16b1a09cc1f4. Score below 2.0; production hunk removed.

title: [perf] skip empty pubsub drain on plain fast path
description: Post frankenredis-zhphm.1 profile still shows drain_pending_pubsub_to_connection on SET/P16/1M fast path. Current server calls Runtime::drain_pending_pubsub after each fast-path command, which mem::take's Store.pubsub_pending and removes the current client outbox even when pubsub/tracking is inactive. Lever: add a read-only has-pending predicate and return early from the server drain helper when both pending queues are empty; behavior unchanged because non-empty pending messages still drain in the same order.

Production hunks removed; next target is structural safe-Rust IO-thread read/parse and encode/write offload.

title: [perf] current-main IO-thread boundary profile for read/parse + encode/write offload
description: Pass5 under frankenredis-zhphm. The pass4 pubsub/accounting micro-lever was rejected, so route deeper. Profile-backed parent evidence points at syscall/write/try_flush and the safe-Rust structural primitive is Redis-style IO threads: owned read/parse and encode/write work offloaded to bounded std::thread queues while command execution remains serial and ordered. First step: rebuild current pushed main, establish SET/P16/1M and a multi-client baseline/profile, then implement exactly one profile-confirmed structural lever only if the boundary is hot enough. Alien-graveyard match: share-nothing/thread-per-core batching, bounded queues/backpressure, zero-copy ownership transfer.

Baseline 1.188526s, candidate 1.223982s; paired baseline 1.15x +/- 0.06 faster; reversed baseline 1.18x +/- 0.05 faster. Production hunks removed; next target is true owned writer boundary with bounded queues/backpressure.
